# Maamarim Melukatim

## Part 1

The concept of Tzimtzum—which on the face of it appears self-contradictory, as it involves a concealment of the Ohr Ein Sof, yet in truth there is no contradiction—is understood through the analogy of a teacher who wishes to teach a profound concept to a student whose intellect is limited. The teacher must conceal his own vast understanding and descend to the level of the student’s comprehension. This concealment is not a negation of the teacher’s wisdom; rather, it is a necessary medium through which the wisdom can be conveyed. Similarly, the Tzimtzum is not a removal of the Divine presence, but a contraction that allows for the emergence of the Seder Hishtalshelus and the eventual creation of the worlds. Through this process, the Ein Sof manifests within the limitations of existence, enabling the avodah of the neshamah to reveal the ultimate unity of Hashem within the created order.

## Second Day of Rosh Hashanah 5723

"He chooses our heritage for us, the pride of Yaakov whom He loves, Selah." The Rebbe Rayatz brings in his maamar on this verse (in the *Hemshech* of Rosh Hashanah 5703) the interpretation of the Targum: "He chooses for us our inheritance, the Beis HaMikdash of Yaakov, whom He loves forever." He notes a difficulty: the Beis HaMikdash belongs to Hashem, as it is written, "And they shall make *Me* a sanctuary" [the Jewish people only perform the act of building, but the sanctuary itself is Hashem’s]. Why, then, is it called the "Beis HaMikdash of Yaakov"? Furthermore, what is the connection between the "pride of Yaakov," which refers to the Beis HaMikdash, and "our heritage"? The maamar explains that "our heritage" is the point of Yiddishkeit that dwells in the point of the heart, which is the "Beis HaMikdash of Yaakov," as it is written, "And they shall make Me a sanctuary, and I will dwell *within them*—not 'within it' [the sanctuary], but 'within them,' within every single Jew." Now, the simple interpretation of "He chooses for us... the pride of Yaakov" is that in the future, He will restore our Beis HaMikdash to us. We must understand the connection between the interpretation (in the maamar) that "pride of Yaakov" refers to the Beis HaMikdash within every Jew, and the (simple) interpretation that it refers to the Third Beis HaMikdash that will be built speedily in our days.

2. This verse is found in the psalm that we recite seven times before the blowing of the shofar. The connection of this psalm to the blowing of the shofar (simply put) is that this psalm states, "Hashem has ascended with a teruah, Hashem with the sound of the shofar." Our Sages taught that initially, Hashem sits (and ascends) on the throne of judgment—"Hashem has ascended with a teruah"—and through the fact that the Jewish people take their shofars and blow before Hashem, He rises from the throne of judgment and sits on the throne of mercy—"Hashem with the sound of the shofar"—and He is filled with mercy for them, has compassion on them, and transforms the attribute of judgment into mercy. Therefore, we recite this psalm before the blowing of the shofar in order to bring a proof from the Torah regarding the *hameshah* (that He rises from the throne of judgment and sits on the throne of mercy) which is brought about by the blowing of the shofar. From the fact that we recite the entire psalm before the blowing of the shofar (not just this verse), it is understood that all the verses of the psalm are related to the matter of Him rising from the throne of judgment and sitting on the throne of mercy through the blowing of the shofar. This is certainly true of the verse "He chooses for us...," which is adjacent to the verse "Hashem has ascended with a teruah," indicating that this verse is related to "Hashem has ascended with a teruah." We must understand that the concept of "He chooses for us" is that Hashem’s choice of the Jewish people is from His own Essence (and not that the avodah of the Jewish people awakens Him to choose them). Moreover, the matter of this choice is in the *Atzmus*, where the concept of *is'arusa d'lesata* (awakening from below) does not apply at all. That is, beyond the fact that the choice *in practice* is from His own Essence and is not awakened by *is'arusa d'lesata*, in the place where this choice exists, it is *impossible* to awaken it through *is'arusa d'lesata*. The concept of "Hashem has ascended with a teruah" is that the shofar blowing of the Jewish people awakens Him to rise from the throne of judgment and sit on the throne of mercy. The juxtaposition of the two verses implies that both matters are connected. That is, through the Jewish people taking their shofars and blowing before Hashem, they draw down not only the mercy awakened by avodah (the *is'arusa d'le'eila* awakened by *is'arusa d'lesata*), but through this, they also draw down the mercy that comes from Hashem’s choice of the Jewish people—"He chooses for us."

3. It should be added that the aforementioned matter (that through the Jewish people blowing the shofar, they also draw down the matter of the choice of the *Atzmus*, where it is impossible to awaken through *is'arusa d'lesata*) is also understood from the well-known fact that the primary aspect of Rosh Hashanah is the building of the *Malchus*, as our Sages said, "Recite before Me verses of *Malchiyos* so that you may make Me King over you." The reason the *mitzvah* of the day is the shofar is that the matter of "make Me King over you" is through the blowing of the shofar, as the continuation of the aforementioned statement of our Sages says, "And with what? With the shofar." The connection of Rosh Hashanah to the building of the *Malchus* (simply put) is because Rosh Hashanah is the renewal of the work of Creation. As we say, "This day is the beginning of Your works," for on every Rosh Hashanah, all the worlds are created anew just as they were the first time. The matter is that the creation of all the worlds is from the attribute of His *Malchus*. The *hameshah* of the *Malchus* on every Rosh Hashanah (including the first Rosh Hashanah at the beginning of creation) is only for one year, and on the eve of Rosh Hashanah at nightfall, it returns and ascends to be included in its source. However, this ascent and withdrawal is only in the *pnimiyus* (internal aspect) of the *Malchus*, which is the *taanug* and *ratzon* within the *Malchus*. The vitality of the world on Rosh Hashanah (even before the matter of "make Me King over you") is from the *chitzoniyus* (external aspect) of the *Malchus*, similar to a person who performs work while tired and weak, without any *taanug* in it. As the well-known saying goes, on the night of Rosh Hashanah (before the matter of "make Me King over you"), the world is in a state of fainting. This is the meaning of "Recite before Me *Malchiyos* so that you may make Me King over you"—that He should have *ratzon* and *taanug* in the kingship. It is known that the withdrawal on Rosh Hashanah is in all levels (action, speech, thought, *midos*, and intellect), as all things return to their origins. The *hameshah* of the *Malchus* (*ratzon* and *taanug* in kingship) on Rosh Hashanah is through drawing from the *Atzmus Ohr Ein Sof*. From the fact that the matter of "make Me King over you" is through the blowing of the shofar, it is understood that the blowing of the shofar reaches the *Atzmus Ohr Ein Sof*.

We must understand: the blowing of the shofar is a *mitzvah*, and the matter of commandments (*mitzvos*) applies only after He has become King, as it is said, "Accept My kingship first, and then I will decree decrees upon you." In order for "make Me King over you" (that the Jewish people *make* Him King) to occur, it is through reaching the *Atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* as it is before (above) He became King. How is it possible that through the blowing of the shofar (the fulfillment of the *mitzvah* and the King’s decree), they make Him King? From this, it is understood that there are two aspects to the blowing of the shofar: the *mitzvah* of blowing the shofar, and the fact that through the blowing of the shofar, one reaches the *Atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* that is above the level of King.

4. In other places, it is explained that the reason the building of the *Malchus* is through the *hameshah* from the *Atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* is that the essence of *Malchus* is revelation. Also, the *Malchus* in every *sefirah* is the power of revelation of that *sefirah*. However, the revelation through the *Malchus* of the *sefiros* is in *Atzilus*, and for there to be revelation in *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah*, it is through the *Malchus* as it is a *partzuf* unto itself. This is similar to speech in a person (*Malchus* is the matter of speech, "the *Malchus* of the mouth"), which is revelation to another. The matter of the building of the *Malchus* on Rosh Hashanah is that the revelation should be not only in the spiritual creatures of *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah*, but also in physical *Asiyah*, and within that itself—down to the lowest levels, even to the inanimate, as we say in the Rosh Hashanah prayers, "And let *all* that is made know that You made it," that even the inanimate (in *Asiyah*) should know that You made it.

This is the meaning of "Recite before Me *Malchiyos* so that you may make Me King over you... and with what? With the shofar." One of the reasons why reciting *Malchiyos* is not enough and one must also blow the shofar is because the matter of "make Me King" on Rosh Hashanah is that the revelation should be drawn into *Asiyah*, and this is done through the act of blowing the shofar. Through the act of blowing the shofar being that one blows into the horn of an animal, the revelation is drawn also into physical matters, the "animal" within man. And in order for the revelation to be drawn lower, down to the inanimate, the horn of the shofar that one blows into must be detached (not connected to the animal), inanimate.

Everything that is drawn lower is through the root of the *hameshah* being higher. As the example is brought from the power of *hafla'ah* (transcendence/abstraction) in *Chochmah*, where the descent is according to the manner of the *hafla'ah*. In order to bring down the intellect and clothe it in a parable (and for the parable to be accurate), it is specifically when one knows the essence of the matter. It should be noted that there are two aspects to the power of the descent of the intellect: it has the ability to *tzimtzum* (contract) the intellect so that it is according to the level of the student, and it has the ability to *hide* the intellect by clothing it in a parable. Even to *tzimtzum* the intellect, a higher power is needed. It can be said that the reason the maamar brings the matter of the descent of clothing in a parable is because it speaks here of the power of *hafla'ah* (abstraction) in *Chochmah*, and in this matter, the descent of clothing in a parable is more relevant. For even though the matter of *clothing* in a parable (unlike the descent of *tzimtzum*) is seemingly the *opposite* of the matter of *hafla'ah*, nevertheless, through clothing the intellect in a parable, the power of *hafla'ah* of *Chochmah* is recognized and revealed. The fact that clothing in a parable is the opposite of *hafla'ah* is when the *hafla'ah* is defined and pictured in a way that is above clothing in a parable, but from the perspective of the true nature of *hafla'ah*, clothing in a parable is also possible.

It can be said that the revelation through the *Malchus* as it is in the *sefiros* of *Ze'er Anpin*—revelation in *Atzilus*—is according to the descent of *tzimtzum*, and the revelation through the *Malchus* as it is a *partzuf* unto itself—revelation in *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah*—is according to the descent of clothing in parables. From this, one understands the greatness of the elevation of the *hameshah* through which the building of the *Malchus* is accomplished (and especially the building of the *Malchus* of Rosh Hashanah), which is not defined by the definition and picture that is above clothing, and therefore, through this *hameshah*, the revelation is drawn and clothed also in *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah*, and within them—even in the inanimate, that even the inanimate should know that You made it.

5. It can be said that according to this explanation, the reason that the building of the *Malchus* is through the *hameshah* from the *Atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* is because for there to be a descent to the lowest levels (and in a manner of clothing), it is through the *hameshah* of a wondrous power that is not defined. The descent (the revelation of *Malchus*) has a relationship to the wondrous power. Since its *hafla'ah* is that it is not defined by the picture above, and it has the ability to descend below, the descent below is the revelation of its *hafla'ah*. Moreover, the revelation of its *hafla'ah* is also *in the manner* of the descent. The more wondrous it is, the more it has the ability to descend lower. From this, it is understood that what is said in the *drushim* (where this explanation is explained) that on Rosh Hashanah there must be the *hameshah* from the *Atzmus Ohr Ein Sof*, the intention is to the *hafla'ah* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* (which is not defined), and not to the *Atzmus* itself. This is unlike the explanation above (Section 3) that the reason the *hameshah* of *Malchus* on Rosh Hashanah must be from the *Atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* is because the ascent and withdrawal of the *Malchus* is in all levels (and therefore the *hameshah* must be from the most elevated place), the *hameshah* is from the *Atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* literally.

6. It is known that there are two aspects to Rosh Hashanah. The primary aspect of Rosh Hashanah (1 Tishrei) is that on this day man was created, and every year on this day, the matter of the creation of man is renewed—"You are called 'man'." There is another aspect on Rosh Hashanah: since man is the purpose of creation, therefore, every year, on the day of the creation of man, the entirety of creation, which was created on 25 Elul, is also renewed. It can be said that this is what is meant by "This day is the beginning of Your works, a remembrance of the first day," for "This day is the beginning of Your works" refers to man (the essence of Your works) who was created on this very day, and "a remembrance of the first day" is that Rosh Hashanah is a remembrance of the first day of creation (25 Elul) on which the world was created. The fact that on Rosh Hashanah there is the building of the *Malchus* is for both reasons: so that through the *Malchus*, the worlds will be created anew (as above, Section 3), and the primary matter of the building of the *Malchus* is for the sake of the birth of the *neshamos* (the creation of man), for *neshamos* and *Malchus* are one matter, as is understood also from the fact that *Malchus* is called *Knesses Yisrael*.

Based on this, in order to accomplish the building of the *Malchus*, one must draw from a place where there is not (not only the existence of the worlds, but also not) the existence of *neshamos*. We must understand how it is possible for *neshamos* through their avodah to reach a place that is above their own existence.

The point of the explanation is that the fact that at the time of the withdrawal of the *Malchus* (in the levels above *Malchus*), there is seemingly no place for *neshamos* and their avodah (because *neshamos* and *Malchus* are one matter) is regarding the *existence* of the *neshamos*. The matter of "make Me King" (that the Jewish people request and plead to Hashem that He be King) is the *bitul* (nullification) that is internal, from the essence of the *neshamah*, which is rooted in the *Atzmus*. When the essence of the *neshamah* is revealed, it draws down the *Atzmus*. Through this, the building of the *Malchus* is also accomplished.

7. The explanation of the matter of the building of the *Malchus* that is accomplished through the *hameshah* of the *Atzmus* through the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* can be said based on what is explained in many places regarding the matter of "Israel arose in thought," that this thought is an essential thought. For thought in general is outside of the thinker [like a person who thinks of something, the thought of the thing is below the person himself, and consequently it is as if it is outside of him], but the thought of Israel is one with the thinker himself. This is the meaning of "With whom did He consult? With the *neshamos* of the *tzaddikim*" ("And your people are all *tzaddikim*"), that the consultation in "With whom did He consult" is not only regarding the creation of the worlds (whether to create worlds or not to create), but also regarding the thought of the existence of the worlds (whether there should be the thought of the existence of the worlds or not). From this, it is understood that the *neshamos* of Israel (with whom He consulted whether there should be the thought of the creation of the worlds) are above this thought. For the thought of the creation of the worlds, even as it is in the most elevated level, is like a person who thinks of something (a thought *to create worlds*), where the thought is outside of him, while Israel arose in an essential thought. It is explained there that the reason the consultation regarding the thought of the creation of the worlds was with Israel who arose in an essential thought (even though an essential thought is above the thought of the creation of the worlds) is because in an essential thought there are two modes: that He thinks about Himself, and that He thinks about a thing distinct from Him, and nevertheless the thought is not outside of Him because *He Himself* is the thinker. This is what "Israel arose in thought" means—the second mode of essential thought, that the *Atzmus* thinks, as it were, about the other [this is "You are children to Hashem your G-d," for since the root of the *neshamos* is in the essential thought, *that the Atzmus* thinks as it were *about the other*, therefore, even after they have come into *hameshah*, they are like children, where in a child (by way of example) there are the two aforementioned matters: the child is distinct from the father, and nevertheless he has within him the essence of the father]. Therefore, the consultation regarding the thought of the worlds was with Israel, because the purpose of the creation of the world [*that the lower ones* should be *a dwelling place for Him*] is completed by the *neshamos*.

It can be said that the connection of *neshamos* to *Malchus* as they are in their root in the essential thought is that the fact that the *Atzmus* thinks as it were about the other is like *Malchus*, for the matter of *Malchus* is specifically about the other; there is no king without a people. Based on this, one can explain the reason why through the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* (through the blowing of the shofar) the building of the *Malchus* is accomplished: because through the revelation of the root of the *neshamah* as it is in the essential thought, the *hameshah* of the *Malchus* is accomplished from its true root as it is in the essential thought.

8. We must understand that according to the above, the fact that through the blowing of the shofar the matter of "make Me King over you" is accomplished is because through the blowing of the shofar, the root of the *neshamah* is revealed as it is rooted in the *Atzmus* that is above the matter of the *mitzvos*. From the fact that the blowing of the shofar is a *mitzvah*, it is understood that the matter of "make Me King over you" also applies to the *mitzvah* of blowing the shofar. This will be understood based on what the Rebbe Rayatz explains in the aforementioned *Hemshech* of Rosh Hashanah, that in the *ratzon* of "I will reign," there are three levels: revealed *ratzon*, hidden *ratzon*, and the *ratzon* absolute in its essence. These three levels are divided into two types: the revealed and hidden *ratzon* are both in revelation within Him, and the difference between them is regarding the actor; the revealed *ratzon* is with an awakening that applies to the actor, and the hidden *ratzon* is only an agreement that does not apply to the actor (and therefore it is called hidden, because it is hidden and distant from the actor). The *ratzon* absolute in its essence is hidden entirely, meaning it is hidden even to itself. [Similar to the *ratzon* for a home: every person has (from the essence of his soul, even when he does not know it) a *ratzon* for a home, because it touches his very existence]. Another difference between the revealed *ratzon* and the hidden *ratzon* is that the awakening of the revealed *ratzon* is through *is'arusa d'lesata*, through the Jewish people accepting upon themselves the yoke of His kingship and requesting, "Reign over the entire world in Your glory." This matter (that the awakening of the revealed *ratzon* is through *is'arusa d'lesata*) existed also at the beginning of creation, but the awakening of the *ratzon* then was through the elevation of *MAN* from within them. As it says, "With whom did He consult? With the *neshamos* of the *tzaddikim*," that through the *taanug* that arose before Him to take pleasure in the avodah of the *tzaddikim*—"And your people are all *tzaddikim*"—through this, it was awakened in the revealed *ratzon* for kingship. [Similar to a human king, where the (revealed) *ratzon* for kingship is awakened by the crowning of the people]. The awakening of the hidden *ratzon* is not through *is'arusa d'lesata*, but it awakens by itself. He explains there that the fact that through *is'arusa d'lesata* the revealed *ratzon* is awakened is not because of the merit of the *is'arusa d'lesata* itself, for a created being is in no comparison to the Creator (even regarding the level of revealed *ratzon*, and especially since even the revealed *ratzon* is in the *Ohr Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum*), but because it so arose in His *ratzon* in the hidden *ratzon*, and higher still, because it was so decided in the *ratzon* absolute in its essence, that *is'arusa d'lesata* would awaken the revealed *ratzon*.

It can be said that the fact that it was decided in the absolute *ratzon* that the awakening of the revealed *ratzon* would be through *is'arusa d'lesata* specifically is so that His kingship over the worlds would be (also) from the perspective of the worlds. [Similar to what is in earthly kingship, where through the kingship being accomplished by the crowning of the people—"You shall surely set a king over you"—the nullification of the people to the king is from the perspective of *their* existence]. Based on this, one can understand the connection of "make Me King over you" (also) to the *mitzvah* of blowing the shofar, for the fulfillment of the *mitzvah* of blowing the shofar is the *is'arusa d'lesata* that awakens the revealed *ratzon*. The *bitul* that comes from the essence of the *neshamah* in the blowing of the shofar is not *is'arusa d'lesata* to awaken the revealed *ratzon*, for the matter of *is'arusa d'lesata* is that the avodah of the *lower one* (existence) awakens the *hameshah*. Therefore, both matters are needed: the *is'arusa d'lesata* that awakens the revealed *ratzon* is the fulfillment of the *mitzvah* of blowing the shofar, and the *hameshah* of the absolute *ratzon* [that the fact that through *is'arusa d'lesata* the revealed *ratzon* is awakened is because it was so decided in the absolute *ratzon*] is through the *bitul* that comes from the essence of the *neshamah* in the blowing of the shofar.

9. Now, there are also two aspects to the absolute *ratzon*. As it is in relation to the revealed *ratzon*, that through the *hameshah* of the absolute *ratzon*, the revealed *ratzon* is awakened through *is'arusa d'lesata* even though the *is'arusa d'lesata* is in no comparison—this matter is the *hafla'ah* of the *Atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* which is not defined by any definition, and it is in His power that the awakening should be also through a matter that is in no comparison [similar to what was explained above (Section 5) regarding the matter of *hafla'ah*, that the *hafla'ah* is that it is not defined and it has the ability to descend below]. And the absolute *ratzon* itself, which is from the perspective of the choice of the *Atzmus*. The fact that it was decided by Him to desire this way is not because through this His *hafla'ah* is revealed, but because He so chose in His free choice.

It can be said that this is why there are three aspects in the blowing of the shofar (in general): the *mitzvah* in it, the matter of *teshuvah*, and the internal *bitul* that comes from the essence of the *neshamah*. The fulfillment of the *mitzvah* of blowing the shofar is the *is'arusa d'lesata* that awakens the revealed *ratzon* (as above). Through the *teshuvah* of the blowing of the shofar—for the matter of *teshuvah* is that one exits his limitations—the *hafla'ah* (in the *Ohr Ein Sof*) is drawn down, that the awakening (of the revealed *ratzon*) is through a matter that is in no comparison, the absolute *ratzon* as it relates to the revealed *ratzon*. And through the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* through the blowing of the shofar, the absolute *ratzon* itself is drawn down—the choice of the *Atzmus*.

10. It can be said that the internal reason why on every Rosh Hashanah all things return to their origins is because through the *hameshah* of the *Malchus* (including the *hameshah* of the *Malchus* that is for the sake of the worlds) being through "Recite before Me *Malchiyos*," the *bitul* that comes from the essence of the *neshamah* which reaches and draws down the absolute *ratzon* itself—through this, a more elevated revelation is drawn down also into the world. For even though regarding the worlds it is (primarily) the *hameshah* of the revealed *ratzon*, nevertheless, since the awakening and *hameshah* of the revealed *ratzon* (through *is'arusa d'lesata*) is through the *hameshah* of the absolute *ratzon* which is through the *bitul* that comes from the essence of the *neshamah*, a semblance of this is drawn down also into the world. Through this, the purpose of the creation of the worlds is completed: that there should be a dwelling place for Him in the lower worlds.

11. This is "He chooses for us our heritage, the pride of Yaakov," that the request of this verse (and similarly in the psalm as a whole) that we recite before the blowing of the shofar is that through the avodah of the blowing of the shofar, there should be (in addition to the fulfillment of the *mitzvah* of blowing the shofar and the matter of *teshuvah* of the blowing of the shofar) also the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* as it is rooted in the *Atzmus*. The fact that the *neshamos* are rooted in the *Atzmus* is because He so chose in His free choice—"He chooses for us." And He draws down "our heritage, the pride of Yaakov," for the revelation of this matter is in the point of Yiddishkeit, "our heritage," which dwells in the point of the heart in every single Jew, the "Beis HaMikdash of Yaakov." According to what was explained above (Section 10) that through the revelation of the root of the *neshamos*, a semblance of this revelation is drawn down also into the world, through which the purpose of a dwelling place in the lower worlds is completed, one can explain the connection of the interpretation that "pride of Yaakov" refers to the Beis HaMikdash in every Jew with the interpretation that it refers to the Third Beis HaMikdash, for the primary matter of a dwelling place in the lower worlds will be in the Third Beis HaMikdash that will be built speedily in our days by Moshiach Tzidkeinu.

It can be said that in addition to the connection of the verse "He chooses for us..." to the general matter of the blowing of the shofar, it has a special connection to the verse (that follows it) "Hashem has ascended with a teruah, Hashem with the sound of the shofar," which our Sages interpreted that through the Jewish people taking their shofars and blowing before Hashem, He rises from the throne of judgment and sits on the throne of mercy. The matter is that the mercy drawn down through *is'arusa d'lesata* is limited according to the *is'arusa d'lesata*. The connection of the two verses is that the fact that the Jewish people take their shofars and blow is not only *is'arusa d'lesata*, but through this, the choice of the *Atzmus* in the Jewish people is revealed, and therefore the mercy drawn down through this is mercy without limit. And it is drawn from Hashem Himself (from the *Atzmus*) to every single Jew, a good writing and sealing for a good and sweet year, in revealed and manifest goodness.

## With the help of Hashem. The Second Day of Rosh Hashanah, 5728

"And it shall be on that day, a great shofar shall be sounded, and those who were lost in the land of Ashur and those who were cast away in the land of Mitzrayim shall come, and they shall bow to Hashem on the holy mountain in Yerushalayim." The Alter Rebbe (in the maamar entitled *Vehayah* in *Likutei Torah*) highlights the phrase "a great shofar," asking: what is the unique quality of this "great shofar"? It must also be understood (as he notes later in the maamar) why it says simply "shall be sounded" (*yitaka*), without specifying who is doing the sounding. The Mitteler Rebbe adds a further point: the grammar of *yitaka* (with a *kamatz* under the *tav*) indicates that it will sound of its own accord. The core of the explanation (in the *drushim*) is that the awakening caused by an ordinary shofar is only for those who are already somewhat close, whereas to awaken those who are "lost in the land of Ashur" and "cast away in the land of Mitzrayim" so that they, too, will be stirred to come to Yerushalayim and bow to Hashem, requires the "great shofar."

To explain the superiority of the great shofar that will be in the future, the maamar begins by explaining the concept of the shofar of Rosh Hashanah (now). Through the fact that Yisrael sound the shofar—which is the cry from the depths of the heart, transcending intellect—the depths of the heart of the "Above," so to speak, the Supreme Will that transcends Chochma, is drawn down. Regarding this it is said, "And Ad-noy Elokim shall sound the shofar," for just as the human sounding of the shofar is the cry of the inner heart, so too is it above: the sounding of the shofar is the drawing down and revelation of the inner essence. The difference between an ordinary shofar and a great shofar is that an ordinary shofar is the drawing down and revelation of the inner essence of Elokus that transcends the worlds but still relates to them—the *Sovev Kol Almin*—while the great shofar is the drawing down and revelation of the inner essence of Elokus that transcends any relation to the worlds, transcending even the *Sovev*. This is why, regarding the ordinary shofar, it is written, "And Ad-noy Elokim shall sound the shofar," for *Ad-noy Elokim* are names, albeit names that are extremely lofty. But regarding the great shofar, it says simply "shall be sounded," without specifying who is sounding it, because this sounding is a drawing down and revelation from the level of the "Hidden of all Hiddens," which transcends all names.

2. To explain the superiority of the great shofar even further, the Tzemach Tzedek brings (in his explanation of the maamar *Vehayah* in *Likutei Torah*) the teaching from *Likutei Torah* on the maamar *Lehavin HaMishnah* regarding a Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos, where they would sound the shofar in the Beis Hamikdash but not in the provinces. The reason is that the sounding of the shofar on Rosh Hashanah is intended to awaken the *taanug* (delight) in the creation of the worlds; since Shabbos possesses this *taanug* inherently, the shofar is not [as] necessary on a Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos. The reason they sounded it in the Beis Hamikdash even on such a Rosh Hashanah is that there are many levels of *taanug*, and the level of *taanug* drawn down by the shofar in the Beis Hamikdash is higher than the *taanug* of Shabbos. The *taanug* of Shabbos is from the external aspect of Keter, the root of the *Atzilim*, while the *taanug* drawn down by the shofar in the Beis Hamikdash is from the internal aspect of Keter, the lowest level within the *Maatzil*. The Tzemach Tzedek adds that even within this lowest level of the *Maatzil*, there are many degrees. The *taanug* drawn down in the First Beis Hamikdash via the shofar was higher than that of the Second. And within the First Beis Hamikdash itself, the shofar of Yom Kippur in the Yovel year [when the mitzvah of Yovel was practiced only in the First Beis Hamikdash] was higher than the shofar of Rosh Hashanah. Yet, even regarding the Yovel shofar, it is said, "And you shall sound the shofar of teruah," an ordinary shofar. From this it is understood that the great shofar is higher even than the revelation drawn down by the Yovel shofar.

3. To add: the great shofar of the future is higher even than the shofar of Matan Torah, for even regarding the shofar of Matan Torah it is said, "And the sound of the shofar was," an ordinary shofar. The fact that the verse continues, "and it grew very strong," refers to the *sound* of the shofar being very strong—a great sound—but regarding the shofar itself from which the sound was drawn, it is not said that it was a "great shofar." From this it is understood that the great shofar of the future is higher even than the shofar of Matan Torah. As it is brought in *Pirkei DeRebbi Eliezer* regarding the two horns of the ram of Yitzchak: the left horn's sound was heard at Har Sinai, as it is said, "And the sound of the shofar was," and the right horn, which is greater than the left, will be sounded in the future, as it is said, "And it shall be on that day, a great shofar shall be sounded." The reason the shofar of Matan Torah was an ordinary shofar while the shofar of the future will be a great shofar is explained by the Mitteler Rebbe: at Matan Torah, everyone was already in a state of closeness to Elokus (even before the sound of the shofar), and therefore even an ordinary shofar could awaken them. But to awaken the "lost and the cast away" requires specifically a great shofar. Based on this, he explains the phrase "shall be sounded (*yitaka*) with a great shofar," that the language of *yitaka* (with a *kamatz*) indicates it will sound of its own accord, for the revelation of the level of the "great shofar" to awaken the lost and cast away will be of its own accord, without any *is'arusa d'letata* (arousal from below) at all.

4. It is explained at the beginning of the maamar (after analyzing the verse "And it shall be on that day, a great shofar shall be sounded") that when we say on Rosh Hashanah, "This is the day, the beginning of Your works," it is because every Rosh Hashanah is the creation of all the worlds anew, just as it was the first time. And the phrase "a remembrance of the first day" (that Rosh Hashanah is only a remembrance of the first day) is because at the beginning of creation, the drawing down of the Will and *taanug* in the creation of the worlds was of its own accord, for "He desires kindness," whereas now, the matter depends on *is'arusa d'letata*. It explains that the *avodah* (*is'arusa d'letata*) through which the Will and *taanug* are drawn down into the creation of the worlds is as our Sages said: "Say before Me *Malchuyos*, *Zichronos*, and *Shofaros*—and with what? With the shofar." The reason the drawing down now is through the *avodah* of man is that because the drawing down is through the *avodah* of Yisrael—whose root is in the *Atzmus*—the Will and *taanug* drawn down now are higher than those drawn down of their own accord (at the beginning of creation). Based on this, one must understand that the shofar from Above (the inner essence of the Supreme Will) that is drawn down now, which is an ordinary shofar, is drawn down through the *avodah* of man, while the shofar of the future, which is a great shofar, will be drawn down of its own accord, without any *is'arusa d'letata* at all.

It is explained in the maamar that the fact that in the future "a great shofar shall be sounded" is similar to the shofar of Rosh Hashanah now, for the mitzvos will not be nullified in the future [only that then the shofar will be at a higher level, a "great shofar"]. One could say that the source for this (that sounding the great shofar relates to the sounding of the shofar of Rosh Hashanah) is what is found in the Gemara, that the reason for the opinion that "in Tishrei they are destined to be redeemed" is the verbal analogy (*gezeirah shavah*) of "shofar, shofar": it is written here, "Sound the shofar in the month," and it is written there, "On that day, a great shofar shall be sounded." Based on this, one must understand even more clearly what was brought above from the *drushim*, that the meaning of *yitaka* (with a *kamatz*) with a great shofar is that it will sound of its own accord without any *is'arusa d'letata* at all—yet one of the primary aspects of Rosh Hashanah is the *avodah* of man [as explained in many places that the reason Rosh Hashanah is on the first of Tishrei, even though the world was created on the 25th of Elul and the first of Tishrei is the sixth day of creation, is that on this day man was created]. Since the matter of "sounding the great shofar" is similar to "sound the shofar in the month" of Rosh Hashanah (Tishrei), it would seemingly be appropriate that the drawing down of this matter should be through the *avodah* of man. Yet it is explained in the *drushim* that the meaning of *yitaka* is that it will sound of its own accord without any *is'arusa d'letata* *at all*. Also, one must understand that all matters in the Torah (from the word *horaa*, instruction) are instructions for the *avodah* of man; since the sounding of the great shofar will be from Above without *is'arusa d'letata*, what is the instruction from this for the *avodah* of man?

5. This can be understood based on what is explained in the *drushim* of the Rebbe Maharash, that the fact that one side of the shofar is narrow and the other is wide is based on the verse, "From the narrow place I called to G-d; He answered me in the expanse of G-d." Just as the sound of the shofar, by exiting from the narrow place through its narrow side, is specifically then in the expanse and expansion of its wide side, so too is it in spirituality: through the cry from the narrow place and the constriction—"From the narrow place I called to G-d"—specifically through that, "He answered me in the expanse of G-d." He explains there that this is what our Sages meant when they said, "Any year that is poor at its beginning is enriched at its end." The "poor at its beginning" [that Yisrael make themselves poor on Rosh Hashanah to speak supplications and prayer, as it is said, "The poor man speaks supplications"] is the call from the narrow place, and through this, it is "enriched"—"He answered me in the expanse." As explained in the aforementioned maamar of the Rebbe Maharash, the superiority of the prayer of the poor over the prayer of the rich. One could say that the superiority of the prayer of the poor is in two aspects. In the prayer itself, according to the teaching of the Baal Shem Tov on the verse, "A prayer for the poor man when he faints and pours out his speech before Hashem," that the prayer of the poor is that he "pours out his speech before Hashem," and also that the prayer of the poor is more readily accepted. And another aspect: that even the *taanug* of the person from the influence drawn down to him through his prayer, the *taanug* of the poor man is a greater *taanug*, according to the teaching of the Maggid brought in the aforementioned maamar.

Based on this, one can explain the connection of "sounding the great shofar" to Rosh Hashanah, for the fact that in the future "a great shofar shall be sounded" to awaken the lost and cast away is because the lost and cast away are in the ultimate narrow place, and this awakens and draws down the sounding of the great shofar (the drawing down from the level of the "Hidden of all Hiddens"), the ultimate expanse. And this is the connection of "sounding the great shofar" to Rosh Hashanah ("Sound the shofar in the month"), for the essence of Rosh Hashanah is that through being "poor at its beginning" (*reishit* of the year, *reishit* missing the *alef*), it is enriched.

6. It can be said that through the sounding of the great shofar to awaken those lost in the land of Ashur and cast away in the land of Mitzrayim, an elevation is also effected in the land of Ashur and the land of Mitzrayim. For the intent in Yisrael being exiled to the land of Ashur and the land of Mitzrayim, to the point that they became "lost and cast away," is so that through the constriction of exile—and specifically the constriction of exile of those who, through the exile, reached the state of being lost and cast away—the great shofar will be awakened and drawn down, through which Yisrael will be elevated to a higher level than they were before they were exiled. Therefore, when the great shofar is sounded and the purpose for which the land of Ashur and the land of Mitzrayim were created is fulfilled, there will be an elevation in them as well.

Based on this, one can add another aspect to the connection of "sounding the great shofar" to Rosh Hashanah, for there are two aspects to Rosh Hashanah: that it is the day of the creation of man, and that through this, an elevation is effected in the entirety of creation (as follows). The matter is as the Rebbe (Rashab) explained in the maamar *Zeh HaYom Techilat Maasecha Zichron LeYom Rishon*, that one must understand: Rosh Hashanah (this day) is on the first of Tishrei, which is the sixth day of creation, so how is it a "remembrance of the first day"? He explains that the will for the creation of the world is the external aspect of the Will, and the internal aspect of the Will is in Yisrael. These two aspects in the times of the year are on the 25th of Elul and Rosh Hashanah. On the 25th of Elul, when the world was created, is the external aspect of the Will, and on Rosh Hashanah (the first of Tishrei), when man was created—"you are called man"—is the internal aspect of the Will. This is "This is the day, the beginning of Your works, a remembrance of the first day," for on Rosh Hashanah there are two aspects. The primary aspect of Rosh Hashanah is that it is "the beginning of Your works," the drawing down of the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* (which transcends the will for the worlds) through the *avodah* of man, and another aspect of Rosh Hashanah is that it is "a remembrance of the first day," that the drawing down of the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* is drawn down also into the will for the creation of the worlds, and through this, into the worlds. Based on this, the connection of "sounding the great shofar" to Rosh Hashanah is in the two aspects within it: the drawing down of the revelation of the great shofar to Yisrael relates to "the beginning of Your works," and the elevation that will be effected through this in the land of Ashur and the land of Mitzrayim relates to "a remembrance of the first day."

7. Based on what was brought above (section 5) from the *drush* of the Rebbe Maharash that the constriction of the shofar is the matter of "a year that is poor at its beginning," that the matter of "poor at its beginning" is that Yisrael *make* themselves poor on Rosh Hashanah, it is understood that the matter of the constriction through which the expanse is drawn down is also when the constriction is from the side of *bitul* (self-nullification)—that his *avodah* is in perfection, but from the side of his *bitul*, he feels he is in a narrow place; even through this constriction, the expanse is drawn down. Similarly, regarding the revelation of the great shofar that is awakened and drawn down through the constriction of the lost and cast away: even when his *avodah* is in perfection, but he contemplates that in comparison to the loftiness of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, even true *avodah* is considered a sin, and therefore he feels himself to be lost and cast away—through this, the revelation of the great shofar is awakened and drawn down. One could say that even then (when the drawing down of the revelation of the great shofar is through his *bitul*), the language of *yitaka* (with a *kamatz*)—*yitaka* of its own accord—is applicable, for when he makes himself poor and needy—the matter of the poor man being that he has nothing of his own and only receives what is given to him by way of charity—he recognizes and feels that the fact that the revelation is drawn down through his *avodah* is not due to his own superiority (that he has the power to draw it down), but due to the kindness of the Holy One, blessed be He; *yitaka* of its own accord, as if the drawing down is without any *is'arusa d'letata* at all.

To add: based on what the Rebbe Rayatz said (in the maamar *Vehayah*), that the great shofar will awaken the point of Yiddishkeit in every single one of Yisrael, it is understood that the fact that all of Yisrael (including the lost and cast away) will want to leave exile and go to Yerushalayim and bow to Hashem is because, through the revelation of the great shofar, the true will of Yisrael will be awakened. This is the difference between the redemption of the exodus from Mitzrayim and the future redemption: in the exodus from Mitzrayim, the desire of Yisrael to leave the impurity of Mitzrayim and cleave to Him was from the revelation from Above—"Draw me"—whereas in the future redemption, the desire to leave exile and come to Yerushalayim will be the will of Yisrael, and the revelation from Above (the great shofar) is only the cause through which the will of Yisrael will be revealed. Their *avodah* will be from themselves—the superiority of Rosh Hashanah, the *avodah* of *man*. However, regarding the lost and cast away in the literal sense, the superiority of the *avodah* of man is *after* the revelation of the great shofar, through which the true will of man is awakened, but the *drawing down* of the revelation of the great shofar is from Above. And there is another way regarding "sounding the great shofar," that even the drawing down of the revelation of the great shofar is through *avodah*, as mentioned above.

8. This is "And it shall be on that day, a great shofar shall be sounded." The instruction from this—that on that day a great shofar shall be sounded, *yitaka* of its own accord—is that at the end of the time of exile, when only a few moments remain before that day when the great shofar will be sounded [and furthermore, that regarding several matters, the matter of "a great shofar shall be sounded" has already begun, as understood from the aforementioned maamar of the Rebbe Rayatz, and especially since from the time the maamar was written until now, many years have passed, and all the more so in recent times, when we have seen tangibly that many who were initially in a state of being lost and cast away, G-d forbid, have been awakened to *teshuvah* through the sounding of the great shofar], the *avodah* must be in *bitul*—the recognition and feeling that all the matters achieved through his *avodah*, both regarding himself and regarding others, are not due to his own merits but due to the empowerment from Above. It should be noted that this feeling does not cause weakness in his *avodah*; on the contrary, through this feeling, his *avodah* is with greater intensity. For when his *avodah* is connected with his own existence, the *avodah* is with measurement and limitation. And even when his *avodah* is in the manner of "with all your might," it is *me'odecha*—your own might. Through the feeling that the matters achieved through his *avodah* are not due to the powers he possesses but due to Elokus, through this he exits his existence and limitations, and his *avodah* is beyond measurement and limitation.

9. It is explained in the maamar *Vehayah* regarding the shofar of Rosh Hashanah that even though the primary drawing down is through the cry of the inner heart, nevertheless, there must be the sounding of a physical shofar specifically, for "action is above," and also so that the revelation of the inner essence of the Supreme Will [the level of the shofar from Above] will be drawn down below into physicality. From this it is understood that it is the same regarding the sounding of the great shofar on that day: the matters that have existed until now are not sufficient, and the sounding of the great shofar must be in a manner that all of Yisrael, including the lost and cast away, will come and bow to Hashem on the holy mountain in Yerushalayim—in Yerushalayim in the literal sense—through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, who will lead us upright to our land, in our days, very soon.

## 29 Elul 5732, the night before Rosh Hashanah 5733

### 29 Elul 5732, the night before Rosh Hashanah 5733

On a Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos, the shofar was sounded in the Beis Hamikdash, but not in the rest of the country. The Gemara explains that this is a rabbinic decree, because while everyone is obligated in the mitzvah of shofar, not everyone is expert in blowing it; therefore, they decreed that one might carry it in his hand to go to an expert to learn, and in doing so, carry it four amos in a public domain. A well-known difficulty is raised here: what reason did Chazal have to set aside a Torah-mandated mitzvah—especially the mitzvah of shofar, which is a lofty and exalted mitzvah—merely because of a decree? [This is especially poignant now, when a public domain according to Torah law is not common, and the decree regarding carrying it is only to prevent a violation of a rabbinic prohibition.] Furthermore, the concern only applies to the unlearned and the light-headed; how could they prevent many great and righteous individuals from performing the mitzvah because of this? The explanation for this is known: the purpose of blowing the shofar on Rosh Hashanah is to awaken, through this, the *taanug* above regarding the creation of the worlds. Since on Shabbos this *taanug* is present by its own nature, the mitzvah of blowing the shofar on a Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos is not as intense (with all the *shturem*) as it is on a Rosh Hashanah that falls on a weekday, and therefore it is set aside due to the concern that one might carry it four amos in a public domain. The reason it was sounded in the Beis Hamikdash even on a Rosh Hashanah that fell on Shabbos is that there are many levels of *taanug*, and the level of *taanug* drawn down by the shofar in the Beis Hamikdash was higher than the level of *taanug* of Shabbos.

2) It must be understood: it is known that the reason we must awaken *taanug* on Rosh Hashanah through the *avodah* of man (blowing the shofar)—even though at the beginning of creation the drawing down occurred by itself—is because when the arousal of the *taanug* above (now) is through the *avodah* of Yisrael, whose root is in the *Atzmus*, the *taanug* that is drawn down now is higher than the *taanug* drawn down by itself (at the beginning of creation). Based on this, on a Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos, where the *taanug* is due to the time of Shabbos (*Shabbos mikdasha v'kayma*), it would seem that the excellence of the *taanug* awakened by the *avodah* of man is lacking. And even though on a Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos there is the *avodah* of reciting the *pesukim* of *Shofaros*, the recitation of the *pesukim* is only so that the drawing down should be in a revealed manner. As explained in the *hemshech* of *Yom Tov Shel Rosh Hashanah 5666*, the drawing down through *mitzvos* is not as revealed, and the revelation is through Torah, *Torah Ohr*. Therefore, for the drawing down of *taanug* through the fulfillment of the mitzvah of shofar to be revealed, it is done through the recitation of the *pesukim* of *Shofaros*. This is the reason that even on a Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos we recite the *pesukim* of *Shofaros*, even though the drawing down of *taanug* then is by its own nature; for the drawing down that comes from Shabbos itself is on a high level, and for there to be a revelation below, it is through the recitation of the *pesukim* of *Shofaros*. And since through the recitation of the *pesukim* there is only a revelation of the drawing down, and the drawing down itself is from Shabbos itself, it would seem that on a Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos, the elevation achieved through *avodah* is lacking.

3) This can be understood by prefacing what Chazal say: "They adjure him, 'Be a tzaddik...'" The Rebbe Rayatz said in the name of the Tzemach Tzedek that on the first Rosh Hashanah of his life (the year 5750), the Alter Rebbe said the *drush* "They adjure him, 'Be a tzaddik...'", which is the first three chapters of *Tanya*. One must explain the connection between the concept of "they adjure him" and Rosh Hashanah based on what is explained in *Likutei Torah*: the reason every Jew is called *Adam* ("You are called *Adam*") is that in the *neshamah* of every Jew there is a part of the *neshamah* of Adam HaRishon. From this it is understood that on Rosh Hashanah (the day Adam HaRishon was created), when the concept of drawing the *neshamah* of Adam HaRishon into his body is renewed (each year) ("And He breathed into his nostrils the *neshamah* of life"), it is also the birthday of every Jew. It is possible to say that this is the connection of "they adjure him" to Rosh Hashanah.

This can be linked to the interpretation of the Midrash on the verse "In the seventh month," that it is *yircha d'shvu'ata* (the month of the oath—*shvi'i* from the word *shvuah*), as explained in the *drushim* of the Tzemach Tzedek regarding the connection of *yircha d'shvu'ata* to "they adjure him, 'Be a tzaddik...'" Perhaps one can add that in the "seventh month" there are three interpretations: seventh in its simple sense; seventh from the word *shvuah* (*yircha d'shvu'ata*, as above); and seventh from the word *sova* (satiety/fullness), as it is in the Midrash that the interpretation of "in the seventh month" is that it is "satiated with everything" (*gisos* within it, blessings within it, etc.). Similarly, it is with "they adjure him, 'Be a tzaddik...'", as it is explained in many places and in the *drushim* of the Tzemach Tzedek that there are three interpretations for "they adjure him": adjure in its simple sense, and also from the word *sova* (satiety), and from the word *shivah* (seven), as follows.

4) The connection between these three concepts (oath, satiety, seven) is that the concept of an oath is that it binds one's *atzmus*. Just as in an oath in its simple sense, the reason a person swears to do something (even though at the time he swears he wants to do it, so why is the oath needed?) is so that even when something arises that would cause him not to want to do it, through having sworn to do it, he will do it even against his will. The reason for this is that the concept of an oath is that it binds one's *atzmus* to it—the essence of the soul that is above will. It is similar above: the concept of an oath is as it is written, "By Me I have sworn"—*bi* (in Me), in My *atzmus*. As explained in the *drushim*, *bi* refers to *Atik*, and it is known that *Atik* is the lowest level in the *Maatzil* that is above the root of the *Ne'etzalim*.

This is "they adjure him, 'Be a tzaddik...'"—the reason one must adjure the *neshamah* to "be a tzaddik," even though this (to be a tzaddik) is the will of the *neshamah*, is that through its enclothement in a body and *nefesh habehamis*, it is possible that the body and *nefesh habehamis* will conceal and hide the *neshamah*. Especially since the root of the *nefesh ha'elokis* is in *tikkun* and the root of the *nefesh habehamis* is in *tohu* that preceded *tikkun*. Therefore, they adjure the *neshamah* to "be a tzaddik," for the concept of the oath is the drawing down of the root of the *neshamah* as it is rooted in the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* (above the concept of *tohu* and *tikkun*), and through this it will be able to overcome the *nefesh habehamis*. This is why "they adjure him" is also from the word *sova* (satiety), for *sova* is the giving of strength. Through the oath—the drawing down of the root of the *neshamah* as it is rooted in the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* [which is analogous to the root of the son as he is in the mind of the father himself before he is drawn down]—they give strength to the *neshamah* as it is in revelation above [which is analogous to the son as he *is drawn down* from the mind of the father], and also to the *neshamah* as it is enclothed in the body and *nefesh habehamis*, to perform its *avodah* in "turning away from evil" ("do not be a rasha") and "doing good" ("be a tzaddik").

5) The Tzemach Tzedek questions the statement "they adjure him, 'Be a tzaddik...'"—how can an oath help the *neshamah* regarding the body and *nefesh habehamis*, which is something not in its domain? After all, a person cannot transfer something that is not in his domain. He explains that the source for "they adjure him, 'Be a tzaddik...'" is what Chazal learned from the verse: "For to Me every knee shall bow, every tongue shall swear." "For to Me every knee shall bow" is the day of death; "every tongue shall swear" is the day of birth. The beginning of this verse is "By Me I have sworn," where *bi* refers to *Atik* (as above). The fact that "every tongue shall swear" comes in continuation of "By Me I have sworn" is because through the oath that they adjure him "be a tzaddik," strength is drawn from the level of "By Me I have sworn." With this strength, the *neshamah* has the ability to rule over the *nefesh habehamis*. It can be said that the explanation of how the oath helps the *neshamah* over the body and *nefesh habehamis*, which is something not in its domain, is that from the level of "By Me I have sworn," the body and *nefesh habehamis* are also in the domain of the *neshamah*. In the level of revelations, the *neshamah* and the body and *nefesh habehamis* are two separate matters. The root of the *neshamah* is in *tikkun* and the root of the body and *nefesh habehamis* is in *tohu*. But in the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above *tohu* and *tikkun*, since the intention from the outset in the building and destruction of *tohu* was that there would be a clarification of *tohu* (the root of the body and *nefesh habehamis*) through *tikkun* (the root of the *nefesh ha'elokis*), therefore, through the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, the body and *nefesh habehamis* also become in the domain of the *neshamah*, because the intention of their creation from the outset is that they be clarified by the *neshamah*.

6) It can be said that the difference between the two concepts of "they adjure him"—adjure from the word *shvuah* (oath) and adjure from the word *sova* (satiety)—is that adjure from the word *shvuah* is the drawing down of the root of the *neshamah*, and adjure from the word *sova* is that this drawing down should be in revelation in the *neshamah* as it comes in the drawing down (including the *neshamah* as it is enclothed in the body and *nefesh habehamis*), analogous to satiety in its simple sense, where the food satiates the *person*. This is why "they adjure him" from the word *sova* is the giving of strength (as explained in many places): because the drawing down of the oath is in revelation in the *neshamah* that is in the body, it has the strength to overcome the *nefesh habehamis* (as in section 4 above), and also to effect in it the clarification and refinement (as in section 5 above), for which it descended below. As explained in *Tanya*, the ultimate intention in the descent of the *neshamah* below is (not for the sake of the *neshamah*, but) in order to clarify and refine the body and *nefesh habehamis* and its portion in the world.

This can be linked to what the Tzemach Tzedek says that an oath is in *Binah*, *Yesod*, and *Malchus*. That an oath is in *Binah* relates to the oath itself, the drawing down of *Atik* [and similarly the drawing down of the root of the *neshamah*], for in *Binah* is the revelation of *Atik*. As it is written, "And a river goes out from Eden"—the river is *Binah* and Eden is *Atik*, and the interpretation of "a river goes out from Eden" is that in *Binah* is the revelation of *Atik*. That an oath is in *Malchus* relates to the *sova* of the oath—that the oath influences and gives strength to the *neshamah* that is in the body is through the drawing down being in *Malchus*. And that an oath is in *Yesod* is that the drawing down of *Binah* (and the *Atik* within it) into *Malchus* is through *Yesod*. This is also the connection with the third interpretation of "they adjure him," which is from the word *shivah* (seven), for according to one explanation this refers to *Yesod*, which is the seventh from *Binah*.

7) Based on this, the connection of the oath that "they adjure him, 'Be a tzaddik...'" with the seventh month, *yircha d'shvu'ata*, is understood. For the fact that the creation of man was on the sixth day of the work of creation, the first day of the seventh month, is for two reasons: because it is "after" the work of creation—for the root of the *domem, tzomeiach, chai* is from *tohu* and the root of man is from *tikkun* which is below *tohu*—and because it is "before" the work of creation (prior in status), as it is in the Gemara, that man was created at the end so that he would enter the banquet immediately, which indicates the excellence of man, that the entire creation is for the sake of man. For even the creation of *tohu* that preceded *tikkun* is so that there would be a clarification of *tohu* through the *avodah* of man, as above. This is what we say on the day of Rosh Hashanah, "This is the day of the beginning of Your works", even though the creation of the world was on the 25th of Elul, because the intention in the creation of the world is that man, through his *avodah*, should make the world a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed, and this matter began on the day man was created. For in addition to the fact that it was then that the possibility to fulfill this intention was created, it was also done then in practice, as Chazal say that Adam HaRishon (on the day he was created) proclaimed to all the creatures, "Come, let us bow down and kneel, let us bend the knee before Hashem our Maker," and they all answered and said, "Hashem is King, He has donned majesty." This is why the seventh month (in which man was created) is *yircha d'shvu'ata*, for the strength that man has to act in the world, even though in revelation man is "after" the work of creation, is through the revelation of the true root of man, which is "before" the work of creation, and this revelation is through the oath (as discussed at length above), *yircha d'shvu'ata*.

Similarly, it is on Rosh Hashanah of every year, when Yisrael [who are called *Adam*, because in every Jew there is a spark of the *neshamah* of Adam HaRishon, as in section 3] crown the Holy One, Blessed be He, as King over them and over the entire world, through which they make the world a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed. And every year the coronation and the making of the dwelling place through it are in a more elevated manner, for the strength that every Jew has for this is because every Rosh Hashanah, the beginning of *yircha d'shvu'ata*, the oath that they adjure every Jew, "Be a tzaddik," is drawn down anew. It can be said that through the Alter Rebbe saying the *drush* "They adjure him, 'Be a tzaddik...'" on Rosh Hashanah, the saying of Torah (*Torah Ohr*), the drawing down of the oath on Rosh Hashanah is in even greater revelation.

8) Perhaps one can add that the reason Chazal give for man being created at the end—so that he would enter the banquet immediately—is intended not only for the world that man must make into a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed, but also for the giving of strength to man, "they adjure him" from the word *sova* (a matter of a banquet), so that he can make the world a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed. Based on this, it can be said that the fact that man is "before" the work of creation and everything was created for his sake includes the oath. For the intention of the oath, the drawing down of the level of *Atik* [and it is possible to say even more than this, also the intention in the level of *Atik* itself], is for the sake of the *avodah* of man. And through man fulfilling the oath, an elevation is achieved in it, that the intention of the oath is completed.

9) The fact that we stated above (section 7) that on Rosh Hashanah the giving of strength is drawn down to man to act in the world and make it a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed, should be linked with what is explained in many places, that at the beginning of creation, the drawing down of the will and *taanug* in the creation of the worlds was by its own nature, because "He desires kindness", and the drawing down of the will and *taanug* in the creation of the world now is through the *avodah* of man. It can be said that this difference is not only in the manner of the drawing down (whether the drawing down is by its own nature or through the *avodah* of man) but also in the will and *taanug* themselves. The will and *taanug* in the creation of the worlds that is from "He desires kindness" is *in the creation of the worlds*, through which His kindness, may He be blessed, is revealed, and the will and *taanug* in the creation of the worlds that is through the *avodah* of man is that which the Holy One, Blessed be He, desired—to have a dwelling place below, and that the making of the dwelling place should be through the *avodah* of man. Through the revelation of this *taanug* in the creation of the worlds, that the intention of the creation of the worlds is for the sake of Yisrael, man (you are called *Adam*) has the strength to act in the creation and make a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed. For the fact that we stated above that the giving of strength to man to act in the creation is the revelation of the root of the *neshamah* (the concept of the oath) is regarding *man*. And the action that is done *in the world* through the *avodah* of man (that through the *avodah* of man the world becomes a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed) is through the revelation of the intention of creation, that the creation of the world is for the sake of Yisrael.

10) Based on this, it can be explained that even on a Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos there is the excellence of *avodah*, because the intention in the drawing down of *taanug* [and also in the *taanug* itself] is for the sake of man, that through the *taanug* there should be a giving of strength to man to make the world a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed (similar to what we stated above in section 8 regarding the oath). For the giving of strength to man is specifically when the drawing down of *taanug* is in revelation. And since even on a Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos the revelation of *taanug* is through the recitation of the *pesukim* of *Shofaros* (as explained above in section 2 from the *hemshech* of 5666), it turns out that even the *avodah* of reciting the *pesukim* of *Shofaros* is regarding a fundamental matter. It should be added that the fact that through the mitzvah of blowing the shofar only the drawing down is achieved, and the revelation is through Torah (recitation of the *pesukim* of *Shofaros*), is because the root of Torah is above the root of the *mitzvos*. This should be linked with what is explained in the aforementioned *hemshech*, that there is another excellence in the recitation of the *pesukim* of *Shofaros*: that the drawing down through blowing the shofar is only the light and the revelation that is drawn from the *Atzmus*, and through the recitation of the *pesukim* the *Helem HaAtzmi* itself is drawn down, analogous to the elevation that will be in the future. Based on this, it is understood even more that even on a Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos there is the excellence of *avodah*, for through the *avodah* of reciting the *pesukim* of *Shofaros*, even the drawing down itself is a more elevated drawing down.

Since Shabbos is the concept of rest and *taanug*, the *avodah* on Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos, and similarly the *avodah* of the day before Rosh Hashanah, is from a place of rest and *taanug*. This also acts upon the entire year, that it should be a year of rest and *taanug*, and even to the true concept of rest—a day that is entirely Shabbos and rest for eternal life, and very soon, *b'agala di'dan*.

## Second Day of Rosh Hashanah 5740

**Premise**
"On the day of your gladness, and on your appointed seasons, and on your new moons, you shall blow with the trumpets... and they shall be for a remembrance for you before your G-d, I am Hashem your G-d." The Gemara states: "What is the teaching of 'I am Hashem your G-d'? This establishes a precedent for every place where *Zichronos* (remembrances) are mentioned, that *Malchios* (kingship) must accompany them." (For "I am Hashem your G-d" is *Malchios*, and from the fact that it says "I am Hashem your G-d" in the context of "they shall be for a remembrance," we learn that wherever *Zichronos* are mentioned, *Malchios* must also be present.) From this we learn that on Rosh Hashanah, one must recite *Malchios*, *Zichronos*, and *Shofaros*: "You shall blow" refers to *Shofaros*; "they shall be for a remembrance" refers to *Zichronos*; and "I am Hashem your G-d" refers to *Malchios*. These three concepts—*Malchios*, *Zichronos*, and *Shofaros*—are not merely matters of speech (reciting the verses of *Malchios*, *Zichronos*, and *Shofaros*, as our Sages said, "Recite before Me on Rosh Hashanah *Malchios*, *Zichronos*, and *Shofaros*"), but also matters of actual deed. As the Sages explain, *Malchios* is "so that you may crown Me," *Zichronos* is "so that your remembrance may ascend," and through what? Through the *Shofar*—by blowing the *Shofar* in actual deed. While the blessings (*Malchios*, *Zichronos*, and *Shofaros*) are mutually indispensable, the blessings and the blowing of the *Shofar* are not mutually indispensable. The discourses of our Rebbes on this subject explain that this is because the blessings and the blowing are two distinct matters: the blessings are speech, and the blowing is a deed. One must understand why both are necessary on Rosh Hashanah, especially since the simple meaning of the Sages' words, "And through what? Through the *Shofar*," refers to the *blowing* of the *Shofar*, implying that the crowning of Hashem and the ascending of our remembrance before Him for good are accomplished through the blowing of the *Shofar*, which is the primary element. This is also evident from the Sages' teaching that the *mitzvah* of the day is the *Shofar*, meaning that all the matters of this day (Rosh Hashanah) are drawn down through the blowing of the *Shofar*. This is also understood from the Rambam, who writes that although the blowing of the *Shofar* on Rosh Hashanah is a divine decree, it contains a hint—"Awaken, etc." The discourses of our Rebbes explain that the Rambam's elaborate language hints at the three concepts of *Malchios*, *Zichronos*, and *Shofaros*. From this, it is understood that all three are drawn down through the blowing of the *Shofar*. The (general) hint in the *Shofar*, "Awaken, etc.," is the concept of *teshuvah* (as explained in the Rambam there). This means that the *mitzvah* of blowing the *Shofar* pertains to the entirety of Rosh Hashanah (the *mitzvah* of the day is the *Shofar*), because on Rosh Hashanah all things return to their primordial state, and one must awaken a new *hemshech* (drawing down) from His *Atzmus* and *Mahus* (Essence and Being), and draw this down through the entire *Seder Hishtalshelus* (order of evolution) down to *Asiyah*. This *hemshech* is accomplished through the *Shofar*, because the *Shofar* hints at *teshuvah*, as mentioned above. The essence of *teshuvah* is unification with the Source—the primordial state—and this exists both from above to below and from below to above, as it is written, "And the spirit returns to G-d who gave it," as explained in *Likutei Torah* at the beginning of this *parsha* (*Parshas Ha'azinu*). Therefore, through this, we awaken a new *hemshech* from His *Atzmus* and *Mahus*, and through *Asiyah* (the act of blowing the *Shofar*), we draw this down into *Asiyah*.

b) Furthermore, it is not only necessary to have a physical act (and it is not enough to have the concept of *teshuvah* in spirituality, nor merely the recitation of the verses), but even within that, it is not enough for it to be the internal voice of the person; one must blow with the *Shofar* of an animal specifically, either a straight *Ya'el* (ibex) or curved rams' horns, according to the two opinions, both of which are the words of the living G-d. Even now, when we blow with curved rams' horns on Rosh Hashanah, these two concepts exist in the spiritual *avodah*. The straight *Ya'el* corresponds to "the more a person straightens his mind, the better," which is explained in *Chassidus* as the need for a person to strip himself of all his desires. This does not refer to undesirable desires—for what does that teach us? That matter (that one should not have undesirable desires) is already accomplished through the *avodah* of the month of *Elul* before Rosh Hashanah. The concept of "straightening one's mind" on Rosh Hashanah is that one should strip himself of all imagery, even imagery of *kedushah* (holiness). Because the *hemshech* of Rosh Hashanah comes from that which is above the category of place and the category of imagery, the *avodah* must be one of "straightening." Through this "straightness" in man, he draws down the "straightness" from above, up to the level of the ultimate simplicity. And we blow with curved rams' horns because "the more a person bows his mind, the better," which is explained in *Chassidus* as the *avodah* of *iskafya* (self-coercion)—which also has an advantage over the *avodah* of stripping away desires—and this *avodah* is through the category of "male" (*zecharim*—rams), for "it is the way of a man to conquer."

c) This is the *mitzvah* of the day—the *Shofar*. Through the *avodah* of the *Shofar* in both modes of "straightening" and "bowing" (each of which has a unique advantage), we draw down a new light. As explained in *Iggeres HaKodesh*, on every Rosh Hashanah a new light is drawn down that has never existed before, and through this, a renewal is effected in the entire *Seder Hishtalshelus*, even to the point that a renewal is effected in the externality of the worlds. Although the primary renewal on Rosh Hashanah is in the internality of the worlds, through this, a renewal is also effected in the externality of the worlds, as explained in the *Siddur* in the discourse "To understand the matter of blowing the *Shofar* according to the *kavanah* of the Baal Shem Tov." All of this is accomplished by "Israel, His near people," "You are children to Hashem your G-d," as the Maggid explains in *Likutei Amarim* and *Ohr Torah* at their beginning regarding the question, "With whom did He consult? With the souls of the *tzaddikim*." The *taanug* (delight) that He would derive from the *avodah* of Israel ascended before Him, and this determined the *ratzon* (will). From the absolute *ratzon*, it was drawn down to the revealed *ratzon*, and subsequently drawn down through the entire *Seder Hishtalshelus* to this lowest world, than which there is no lower, for specifically through this is the intent of a "dwelling place for Him in the lower realms" completed. This is the meaning of "Recite before Me *Malchios* so that you may crown Me, etc., and through what? Through the *Shofar*," which means the blowing of the *Shofar* in deed, which is the *mitzvah* of the day. However, it also includes (in continuation of the beginning of the *maamar*, "Recite before Me, etc., and *Shofaros*") the recitation of the *Shofaros* verses.

d) This verse ("On the day of your gladness... and you shall blow with the trumpets...") is a continuation and conclusion of the preceding matter: "Make for yourself two trumpets." The teaching of the Maggid is well known: "two trumpets" are "two half-forms," meaning that the Holy One, blessed be He, and the Community of Israel are, as it were, two half-forms. From this, the meaning of the verse "You shall blow with the trumpets... and they shall be for a remembrance for you before your G-d, I am Hashem your G-d" is understood. The matter of *Malchios* ("I am Hashem your G-d") and *Zichronos* ("they shall be for a remembrance")—which is accomplished through "you shall blow with the trumpets," "with trumpets and the sound of the *Shofar* shout before the King, Hashem"—is because the Holy One, blessed be He, and Israel are two half-forms. The matter is that the second soul in a Jew is truly a part of G-d above. It is known that there are two extremes to this. "Truly" refers to "a part of G-d above," meaning that the second soul in a Jew is literally a part of His Essence. The Baal Shem Tov's teaching is known: "The Essence—when you grasp a part of it, you grasp the whole of it." The word "truly" also points to the lowest extreme—literally that which is grasped by the sense of touch. This is the meaning of the second soul in a Jew being a part of G-d above *truly*: that even when it is clothed in a body—literally, from the word *mishush* (touch)—it is still a part of His Essence, one with His Essence. And as the Maggid's teaching (cited above) states, they are two half-forms. Since "you are called 'man' (*Adam*)," after the name "I will resemble the Most High" (*edameh l'elyon*), it is understood that the matter of two half-forms exists also within the soul itself. It is known that in every soul there are two general levels: the part of the soul that is above (the level of *mazal*), the "head" of the soul, and the part of the soul that is in the body (the revealed powers), the "foot" of the soul. These two levels are the two half-forms in every individual soul. The matter of "you shall blow with the trumpets... and they shall be for a remembrance for you before your G-d" is that one must connect his two half-forms and stand them before Hashem your G-d. Through the connection of these two half-forms (head and foot) in every individual soul, the connection of the head and foot in the entirety of Israel is also effected, and their elevation before Hashem your G-d: "You are standing today (Rosh Hashanah) all of you before Hashem your G-d, your heads... to your woodchopper and your water-drawer." All of Israel—the head of Israel (your heads) and the foot of Israel (your woodchopper and water-drawer)—are connected together, and they stand and are elevated before Hashem your G-d, and they become one with Him, as the verse continues, "to pass you into the covenant," for the matter of the covenant is that they become one thing.

e) This is "On the day of your gladness... and you shall blow with the trumpets... I am Hashem your G-d."
