And since everything Above is "from you" (as mentioned above from the teachings of the Maggid), it is in the power of every one of Yisroel to accomplish not only all the matters of the *Hishtalshelus* (even the highest matters), but it is in his power to accomplish even the simple *taanug*, and that this should be drawn down below as well, "Reign over the entire world in Your glory," etc., until "all that is made will know that You made it," and certainly that He will be the King of Yisroel (including) and his Redeemer, in the true and complete Redemption through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, when they will blow even on a Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos in the Beis Hamikdash and in Yavneh [and it can be said that also in every place (for then all the matters of the decrees will be nullified)], speedily and in our days, literally, and with great joy.

## Second Day of Rosh Hashanah 5743

**1.** "Master of the World, who reigned before any creature was created" — and my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, emphasizes this in his *maamar* on this topic [in the *hemshech* beginning "The Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos," where he explains (in the first *maamar*) that the coronation of the King is with great joy] — that one must understand: how is kingship possible before any creature was created? For kingship exists only over others, as the saying goes, "There is no king without a people." Furthermore, kingship is specifically over those who are in the king’s category—meaning the people must have some similarity to the king. It is well known that "people" (*am*) is related to the word *imo* (with him/like him). How, then, is the concept of kingship possible before any creature was created?

He explains this by prefacing what is stated in the *Shelah* (and in many other books) regarding the greatness of the praise of "Master of the World," to the point that *Adon Olam* (Master of the World) has the same *gematria* as *Ein Sof*. *Ein Sof* is the most exalted level in Elokus, and furthermore, it is above the concept of levels entirely, for the true essence of *Ein Sof* is in His Essence, blessed be He. Based on this, he explains the concept of "Master of the World, who reigned before any creature was created": since the Master of the World is (in *gematria*) *Ein Sof*, it is understood that the concept of "Master of the World, who reigned..." refers to the level of *Malchus* as it is rooted, so to speak, in His Essence, blessed be He (the true essence of *Ein Sof*). And since His Essence, blessed be He, is All-Powerful, it is possible for Him to be King even without a people, before any creature was created.

**2.** This *maamar* comes, as mentioned above, in continuation of the *maamar* beginning "The Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos," in which he explains at length the matter of the *shofar* blowing on Rosh Hashanah. The *avodah* of blowing the *shofar* is a general matter that includes all the details of the *avodos* of the entire year. For the matter of blowing the *shofar* is as our Sages said: "Say before Me on Rosh Hashanah... *Malchiyos* (verses of kingship), so that you may crown Me over you... and with what? With the *shofar*." Since all the commandments from Above (*mitzvos*) apply only after He, blessed be He, becomes King, as the saying goes, "Accept My kingship first, and then I will decree decrees upon you," it follows that the *avodah* of blowing the *shofar*, whose purpose is "so that you may crown Me over you," is the root of all the *mitzvos* (*avodos*) throughout the entire year.

He explains there that the matter of "so that you may crown Me over you" through the blowing of the *shofar* is the drawing down of the will for kingship (similar to the coronation of a king of flesh and blood, which comes with great joy and immense *bitul* to the king, through which the king is aroused with a will to desire kingship). The reason why the *avodah* of the Jewish people arouses in Him, blessed be He, the will for kingship is because even before this, He has a will for it, but this will is in a state of concealment, and the *avodah* of the Jewish people draws the will from concealment into revelation. The reason for this concealed will for kingship is that the concept of the will for kingship exists even before this (before the concealed will), and that is the absolute will in His Essence. In truth, it is not possible to say regarding this that the will for kingship exists beforehand (as we say regarding the concealed will, that even before it is aroused in the revealed will, the will already exists in concealment), for in His Essence, blessed be He, it is not possible to say that there is any matter that is in concealment; everything there is in a state of absolute unity in the ultimate simplicity. The matter of "so that you may crown Me over you" through the blowing of the *shofar* is not only to draw the will for kingship from concealment into revelation, from the level of will as it is included in concealment in the *Ohr Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum* (in the aspect of revelation to Himself), but rather, through this, it is drawn also from the absolute will in His Essence, for this will is, so to speak, the Essence itself (and it is not applicable to say of it that it is included in concealment). This drawing down on Rosh Hashanah is through "so that you may crown Me over you," a will for kingship, and from this is drawn afterwards the will for the *mitzvos* (decrees), for the essence of the *mitzvos* is the will Above. As is explained in many places, the reason why the performance of *mitzvos* must be specifically in detail and with precision (as we have been commanded) is because in the will, it matters that every single detail be exactly as the will dictates.

**3.** In the *maamar* (beginning "Master of the World"), it is explained that through the performance of the *mitzvos*, the building of the *keilim* (vessels) of *Ze'er Anpin* is accomplished, as well as the drawing of the light into the *keilim* of *Ze'er Anpin*. This matter also applies to (the beginning of the *hemshech*) "The Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos, in the Temple they would blow, but not in the provinces", as stated in the *maamar* of the *Tzemach Tzedek* (upon which the *maamar* said on the eve of Rosh Hashanah is based) regarding the reason why on Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos we do not blow in the provinces: because our *mitzvos* are (in order) that through *is'arusa d'lesata* (arousal from below) comes *is'arusa d'le'eila* (arousal from Above), that through the performance of the *mitzvos* by man, the *mitzvos* Above are accomplished (the 248 commandments are the 248 limbs of the King). It is the same regarding the *mitzvah* of blowing the *shofar*, whose purpose is to create thereby the aspect of the *shofar* Above. Therefore, on Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos, we do not blow (in the provinces), because on Shabbos there is the aspect of the *shofar* Above (the aspect of *taanug*) even without the *avodah* of man, due to the Shabbos, and "Shabbos is holy and established". The reason they would blow in the Temple even on Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos is because in the Temple, which is the "Gate of Heaven", there was the power to create a more exalted aspect in *taanug* (as explained at length in the *maamar* of the eve of Rosh Hashanah).

This matter, that the *mitzvos* (matters) Above are accomplished specifically through the performance of the *mitzvos* by man, applies to all aspects and all *partzufim* of the entire *Seder Hishtalshelus*, according to the teaching of the *Maggid* in his commentary on the *Mishnah*, "Know what is above you"—that everything that is above is from you. The aspect of *taanug* (*shofar*) Above is created (as mentioned above) through the performance of the *mitzvah* of blowing the *shofar*, and the aspect of *mochin* Above (which is the matter of *tefillin*, as is known that the four sections are the aspect of the four *mochin*) is created through the performance of the *mitzvah* of *tefillin*, for by man putting on *tefillin*, he causes the Holy One, blessed be He, to put on *tefillin*. And so it is also in the *midos* (of *Ze'er Anpin*), for by the man below performing the *mitzvah* of "And you shall love Hashem your G-d" and the *mitzvah* of "And Hashem your G-d you shall fear" (for *ahavah* and *yirah* are the root of all *mitzvos*), the building of *Ze'er Anpin* is accomplished thereby. And so it is also in the aspect of *Malchus*, for the building of the world of speech (*Malchus*) is accomplished through the speech of the lower man in words of Torah and words of *tefillah*. This is the content of the matter of the performance of *mitzvos* (according to what is explained in these *maamarim*), for the aspects Above are (in general) in the number of 248 (the 248 limbs of the King), and the building of all the aspects Above is through the performance of the 248 positive *mitzvos* (of man), as it is written, "And you shall perform them" (*ve'asitem otam*)—it is written "you" (*atem*), "I account it to you as if you had created Me".

**4.** One must understand: it is known that the elevation in the performance of *mitzvos* is when the performance is because it is the *command* of the Holy One, blessed be He (as in the formula of the blessing on the *mitzvos*, "Who has sanctified us with His *mitzvos* and commanded us"), and not because of the reason or the *segulah* within them, including also the reason that through the performance of the *mitzvos*, the *mitzvos* Above are accomplished. It can be said that the explanation for this is that in *mitzvos* (the will and the command from Above that the Jewish people should perform *mitzvos*), there are two matters. The cause and the reason for the command, and from this is created the existence of the *mitzvah* (the command), which is a consequence of the reason. And afterwards (after the command from Above) comes the performance of the *mitzvah* by the man who performs them. In this, there are two general modes. The first mode is that he performs it for the purpose of the command (so that the reason for the command is fulfilled), and the second mode is that he performs it in a more exalted way, that his performance of the *mitzvah* is in such a way that he thereby creates, so to speak, a new will, which is incomparably higher than the will that is a consequence of the reason. Since the Jewish people are rooted in the Essence (the Owner of the Will), "Israel and the King are one", therefore when a Jew performs a *mitzvah*, it is in his power to create a new will that did not exist before this.

It can be said that similarly, these are the two interpretations of the saying of our Sages, "There is satisfaction before Me, for I spoke and My will was done". The first interpretation is that through the performance of the *mitzvah*, the will (the command) that was at the beginning is fulfilled. And the second interpretation of "My will was done" is that through the performance of the *mitzvah* by the Jew (like the second mode), a new will is created (renewed) that did not exist before this (similar to what is explained in many places regarding the interpretation of "they do the will of the Omnipresent", that the interpretation of "they do" is that they create a new will). Based on this, it can be said that what is stated in the aforementioned *drushim* that the matter of *mitzvos* is in order to create thereby the *mitzvos* Above, this is in the first mode of the performance of *mitzvos*, in the aspect of fulfilling the will that is a consequence of the reason, so that there will be the building of the entire *Seder Hishtalshelus*, the 248 limbs of the King [and in this itself there are two matters: 1) the reason and the cause (of the will)—that the *mitzvos* (aspects) Above should come into being; 2) the consequence—the will that the Jewish people should perform the *mitzvos* below]. But the performance of *mitzvos* from the perspective that the Jewish people are rooted in the Essence is to create a new will. And this is what is explained in many *drushim* that the elevation in the performance of *mitzvos* is when the performance is because it is the will (command) of the Holy One, blessed be He, and not because of the reason (including also the reason that through the performance of the *mitzvos*, the *mitzvos* Above are accomplished), for this will is a will that is above the reason. And the performance of the *mitzvos* (from the perspective of this will) is not in order to affect matters Above, but rather, *mitzvah* is from the word *tzavta* (connection) and union, the union of the Jew with the Owner of the Will, and even to the union with the Essence. Similar to the saying of the Alter Rebbe: "I do not want anything, I do not want Your Gan Eden, I do not want Your Olam Haba... I want nothing but You alone."

**5.** Based on this, it will also be understood what is explained in the aforementioned *drush* of the *Tzemach Tzedek*, that the specific *taanug* in every *mitzvah*, that in every *mitzvah* there is a special *taanug*, is more exalted than the general *taanug* and will that is in all *mitzvos* equally, and its root is from the aspect of the Source of *Taanugim* (which is above the aspect of the revelation of *taanug*). Seemingly, it is not understood how this fits with what is explained in many *drushim* that the general intention in all *mitzvos* equally ("and He commanded us") is more exalted than the specific intention of each *mitzvah* in particular. The explanation for this can be said to be that in the aforementioned *drush* of the *Tzemach Tzedek*, it is speaking about the matter of the will in *mitzvos* as it is a consequence of the reason, in order that the *mitzvos* Above should come into being. And what is explained there that in *mitzvos* there are two aspects, the general matter that is in all *mitzvos* equally, and the specific matter that is in each *mitzvah* in particular—these two aspects are in the will that is from the reason. That at the beginning of its drawing down, it is a general will that the matters Above should be accomplished through the performance of the *mitzvos* by man, and from this is drawn afterwards (lower down) a specific will for each *mitzvah* in particular, so that through its performance, that same *mitzvah* Above that is its counterpart will be accomplished (through the *shofar* below, the *shofar* Above. And likewise in the other *mitzvos*). Therefore, he says there that there is an advantage in the specific *taanug* and intention (and even that the root of the specific *taanug* is specifically in the aspect of the Source of *Taanugim*), because the intention in this will is to create the *mitzvos* (specific matters) Above, and this is accomplished specifically through the specific action of each *mitzvah*. And what is explained in many *drushim* that the general intention in all *mitzvos* equally is more exalted is because there it is speaking about the will that the Jewish people create and make through their *avodah*, and from the perspective of this will, the mode of performance of the *mitzvos* is to create a will, a general intention.

**6.** The two aforementioned modes in the performance of *mitzvos* [because of and in the mode of the command from Above, and the innovation created by the Jewish people] are, of course, also in the *mitzvah* of blowing the *shofar*. Even though the specific matter of the *shofar* (which is from the command) is also above measurement and limitation. For although even in the *mitzvah* of blowing the *shofar* there is a measure and limit (as in most *mitzvos*) regarding the number of sounds and the modes of the sounds and the like, the matter (the content and intention) of this *mitzvah* is the *avodah* that is above measurement and limitation, as is known that the matter of blowing the *shofar* is the cry from the depths of the heart that is above the measurement and limitation of the intellect, the aspect of "with all your might." And similarly (and even more so) is the aspect of the *shofar* Above (which is created through the blowing of the *shofar* by man), which is above measurement and limitation, for the *shofar* Above is the aspect of the Source of *Taanugim* that is above specific *taanug*, as explained at length in the aforementioned *drush* of the *Tzemach Tzedek*. Thus, even though the matter of blowing the *shofar* as it is from the perspective of the command from Above is in the aspect of *bli gevul* [and the command from Above is the *is'arusa d'le'eila* and the giving of strength for this, that the *avodah* of blowing the *shofar* should be in the mode of "with all your might"], nevertheless, since the Jewish people are rooted in the Essence, they have the ability to create a new will and matter, the drawing down of the absolute will in His Essence, which is, of course, above even the aspect of the Source of *Taanugim* mentioned above. And as mentioned above (at the beginning of the *maamar*), the matter of "so that you may crown Me over you" through the blowing of the *shofar* is the drawing down (also) from the absolute will in His Essence.

**7.** It can be said that this is also the reason why the blowing of the *shofar* begins specifically with a *kol pashut* (simple sound), as explained at length in the *Siddur* in the *drush* "To understand the matter of blowing the *shofar* according to the intention of the Baal Shem Tov," that this *kol pashut* arouses the simplicity (and the plainness) of the Jewish people, and the simplicity of the Jewish people is one with the simplicity of the Essence. It can be said that this is also one of the reasons for the great effort of the Baal Shem Tov [even before his revelation as the Baal Shem Tov] that simple people, and even small children, should say "Baruch Hashem" and the like [and the telling of this detail (of the conduct of the Baal Shem Tov) was transmitted to us by our Rebbes, the *Nesi'im*, meaning that this is an instruction and a giving of strength to everyone], because when a simple person says "Baruch Hashem" and the like, his intention in this is to the Essence (as is known that what the Name of Heaven is common in everyone's mouth is the Essence). And similarly it is also in the performance of *mitzvos*, for the performance of *mitzvos* by a simple person is not because through his performance the *mitzvos* accomplish the matters Above, for a simple person does not (yet) know what is above and what is below and what is in the four directions, but because of the command, "Who has sanctified us with His *mitzvos* and commanded us." And this is the matter of the *kol pashut* of the blowing of the *shofar*, that after all the realizations of the elevation that is accomplished through the performance of *mitzvos*, that through the performance of the *mitzvos* by man the *mitzvos* Above are accomplished, and as in the *mitzvah* of blowing the *shofar* that thereby the *shofar* Above is created, the ultimate perfection is to perform the *mitzvah* in simplicity [it can be said that it is similar to "the end is rooted in the beginning". For just as the beginning of the *avodah* (before it is possible to have intentions) is in simplicity, so it is also in the conclusion and seal (perfection) of the *avodah], and through this specifically, one draws down and becomes one thing with the simplicity of the Essence.

**8.** Based on this, it will also be understood what is explained in the *drushim* of the Rebbe Maharash (whose matter is *lechatchilah ariber*) two parables about the blowing of the *shofar*. The first parable in the name of the Baal Shem Tov about the king's son who went on a long journey, etc., until he even forgot the language of his country and began to cry out with a *kol pashut* (in the parable there it says the language "with a loud voice," but in the *nimshal* it says the language "with a *kol pashut*") so that his father would recognize him by the instinctive recognition of the voice. And the second parable from the great Rabbi, the Chassid, Rabbi Levi Yitzchak of Berditchev regarding garments when the king was in the forest (which are not royal garments at all), as explained there at length the details of this. Seemingly, it is puzzling, for it is known that on Rosh Hashanah one must be careful not to mention undesirable matters and descriptions, which is why we do not say *viduy* (confession), etc., on Rosh Hashanah, and one must speak then only of the merit and praise of the Jewish people, and here he explains the blowing of the *shofar* of Rosh Hashanah with these parables, most of the details of which are seemingly matters that are the opposite of praise [and I was precise for this reason in the *maamar* of the eve of Rosh Hashanah to mention and speak only of the point of the content of these two parables, and not the details]. And even though the aforementioned *maamar* (in which these two parables were explained in detail) was said on *Shabbos Parshas Tavo* and not on Rosh Hashanah, nevertheless, these parables are an explanation of the matter of the blowing of the *shofar* of Rosh Hashanah. And the greatness of the puzzle in this, this itself proves that all the details in these parables are (in the inwardness of the matters) for praise.

This will be understood according to what is known that the reason why the blowing of the *shofar* is specifically with a *kol pashut* is because on Rosh Hashanah it is "to pass you into the covenant of Hashem your G-d", for this covenant is the essential connection of the Holy One, blessed be He, and the Jewish people (who pass together between the pieces) to be truly one thing. And since this connection is the connection of the essence of the soul with the Essence, the simplicity of the essence of the soul with the simplicity of the Essence, therefore also the remembrance of the covenant and the connection is through a *kol pashut* specifically (which is not clothed in letters), for letters (even the most exalted letters) are a limitation, and the remembrance of the covenant and the connection of the simplicity of the essence of the soul with the simplicity of the Essence is through a *kol pashut* specifically. And this is why the parable about the blowing of the *shofar* is of the king's son who even forgot the language, for this forgetting is for the better. For since the letters (the language) are limiting, therefore in order to come and reach the aspect of simplicity, one must forget the language. Similar to Rabbi Zera who fasted a hundred fasts to forget the Babylonian Talmud so that he could learn the Jerusalem Talmud, and similar to the pillar between *Gan Eden HaTachton* and *Gan Eden HaElyon* whose matter is (as explained in *Torah Ohr*) the forgetting and nullification of the realization and vision of *Gan Eden HaTachton*, for then specifically he can receive the revelation of *Gan Eden HaElyon*. And just as it is in *Gan Eden* that the realization of *Gan Eden HaTachton* confuses the realization of *Gan Eden HaElyon*, so it is (and even more so) regarding the matter of the revelation of the aspect of simplicity that is above realization entirely, for the totality of the matter of realization (even the most exalted realization) confuses the revelation of the aspect of simplicity. And similar to what is explained in *Ateres Rosh* regarding the reason for the opinion that *mitzvos* do not require intention (meaning that the performance of *mitzvos* must be with acceptance of the yoke without reason and intention), for seemingly one should wonder, what do we care if he also adds the intention? And he explains there that the intention causes a deficiency of light, so to speak, in the body of the acceptance of the yoke of the *mitzvos*, which is not the way of the yoke as much. For the intention (and reason) of the *mitzvah*, even in the most exalted level, is in the category of description, form, and limitation, whereas the *mitzvah* that is in the way of acceptance of the yoke, its root is in the simple will and *taanug*, and the reason weakens, so to speak, the strength and clarity of the essence of the *mitzvah*. And from this it is understood also regarding the matter of the *kol pashut* of the *shofar* blowing, that there must be a forgetting and nullification of the language, for through this specifically one arrives at the aspect of simplicity.

**9.** Similarly, it is also in the second parable, regarding the matter of the garments, for the garments, even though they are for honor and glory, like royal garments (and also expensive garments in general), are in measurement and limitation. For (in the *nimshal*) the garments are the matter of Torah and *mitzvos*, for *mitzvos* are garments as explained in *Tanya*, and as is understood also from what the Holy One, blessed be He, said to Abraham our father, "Come, put on My garments", and also the Torah is a garment as it is written, "Hashem has clothed Himself with strength, He has girded Himself," and there is no strength but Torah. And since the *avodah* of Rosh Hashanah is the connection of the Jewish people with His Essence, blessed be He, as He is above the aspect of King (for on Rosh Hashanah one must *create* that He should be King, "so that you may crown Me over you"), and even more so above the matter of the decrees (which come after He has already become King), therefore in order to reach this, there must be a nullification of the garments of the *mitzvos* (similar to what was explained above regarding the matter of the forgetting and nullification of the language). Therefore, the parable about the blowing of the *shofar* is of simple garments specifically, for these garments indicate the connection with His Essence, blessed be He, as He is above the aspect of King, as he elaborates in the parable that these garments are when the king was in the forest before he returned him to his throne of kingship, and also in the *nimshal* he explains that these garments are the matter of the garment that was at the time of the Giving of the Torah when we crowned Him, blessed be He, with the *shofar* before the Giving of the Torah itself, meaning before (above) He became King.

**10.** And even though the expressions that he says there (in the parable and also in the *nimshal*) as they are in simplicity do not speak of the praise of the Jewish people, he says in the *maamar* there that this parable is from the great Rabbi, the Chassid, Rabbi Levi Yitzchak of Berditchev, and it is known and famous in all the worlds that Rabbi Levi Yitzchak was a lover of the Jewish people and an advocate for every Jew, and he would judge every Jew to the scale of merit, and in the mode of *lechatchilah ariber*. Therefore, when he says in the *maamar* that this parable is from Rabbi Levi Yitzchak, it is understood in simplicity that all the expressions that he says there are for the better. And this is his method and way regarding the Jewish people, as is known the interpretation of Rabbi Levi Yitzchak of the verses at the beginning of the book of Isaiah, that there are there descriptions of the Jewish people that are seemingly, etc., and the Rabbi of Berditchev interprets that these descriptions indicate the praise of the Jewish people [and as is also proven from the fact that these descriptions were said in the book of Isaiah, for Isaiah is the prophet of salvation, as his name *Yeshayah* is from the word *yeshuah* (salvation)], and not only that, but they are the most exalted praises, for which there are no descriptions of praise like them. It can be said that according to what is known in the interpretation of the saying of our Sages, "They exact payment from a person with his knowledge and without his knowledge" (and similarly in the interpretation of the language of the *Mishnah* "judgment and accounting"), that when a person says the verdict of another person, he is also passing judgment on himself, then the verdict of the Rabbi of Berditchev regarding the aforementioned descriptions in Isaiah is also a verdict regarding the aforementioned descriptions in his own teachings and parables.

**11.** Especially since it has already happened in the world, and "a great act", as is told in the *Gemara* that they said expressions that appear on the outside, etc., and in truth they are all blessings, and what they blessed him with such language is as the *Tzemach Tzedek* says because the blessing in this mode is from the aspect of *satim* (hidden), for the blessings that are from the aspect of *satim* are more exalted blessings, and also their drawing down below is with great force (*mit a shturem*). And as it is in the *Gemara* there, that they blessed him "you will beget children," etc., the drawing down and revelation of the power of the *Ein Sof* "in a generation of the upright, he will be blessed." And this interpretation (that what they blessed him with such language is because the blessing in this mode is from the aspect of *satim*) the *Tzemach Tzedek* says is "more seen as the simple meaning." For before this, it is brought there (regarding the blessings in *Parshas Bechukosai*) that it is the matter of the opposite of the blessing, but that this refers to the *Sitra Achra*. And afterwards he brings a more exalted interpretation, that what the blessings were said in such language is in order to sharpen. That even according to this interpretation, the matter of the blessing that is in this language is not in simplicity and revelation, for in order to stand on the truth of the matter, one needs the sharpening of the intellect. But afterwards he concludes that what the blessings were said in such language is not a deficiency, G-d forbid, but on the contrary, because the blessings are more exalted. And on this interpretation he says that it is "more seen as the simple meaning," that what these expressions are blessings, this is not a sharpened interpretation but the simple interpretation, even in the world of *peshat*. And after the *Tzemach Tzedek* opened the way, and especially since he taught and gave this in the Torah as an instruction, this became an instruction and a giving of strength to everyone to interpret and reveal the blessing also in expressions and matters that in their externality appear, etc., and on the contrary, these blessings are the most exalted, and as the simple meaning, in the good that is seen and revealed. For this power (to draw down all matters in the good that is seen and revealed) is also in all the days of the year. And especially on Rosh Hashanah when they crown the King with great joy (as mentioned above from my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe), for joy breaks all boundaries. And all the more so on the Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos, for all the drawings down that are accomplished through the blowing of the *shofar* are accomplished on Shabbos by itself, for on Shabbos is drawn down the aspect of simple *taanug*, as explained at length in the *hemshech* "The Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos" by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe (Rashab), of blessed memory.

**12.** And this is "Master of the World, who reigned before any creature was created," and my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, explains in his *maamar* on this topic (as mentioned above at the beginning of the *maamar*) that *Adon O

## Second Day of Rosh Hashanah, 5747

– Discourse –

"Blow the shofar on the new moon, at the covering for our festival day". The Rebbe Rayatz, Nasi of our generation, explains this [in his maamar entitled *Tik'u*, 5707—forty years ago, for as our Sages say, "after forty years, one understands the mind of his Rebbe"] based on the teaching of our Sages regarding the verse, "Which is the festival on which the moon is covered? You must say, this is Rosh Hashanah." "At the covering" (*b'keseh*) refers to the "throne of Hashem" (*kis Hashem*), where *Heh* refers to the Sefirah of Malchus, and *kis* (throne) implies concealment. Rosh Hashanah is called *keseh* because on Rosh Hashanah, the Sefirah of Malchus is covered and hidden in an essential concealment. (Perhaps one might add the simple connection to the "festival on which the moon is covered," as the moon simply refers to the moon itself, and it is known that the moon represents the Sefirah of Malchus.) Therefore, the primary avodah of Rosh Hashanah is the construction of the Sefirah of Malchus, as our Sages say, "Recite before Me verses of sovereignty (*malchiyos*) so that you may make Me King over you." The drawing down of the aspect of Malchus is accomplished through the blowing of the shofar, as it is written, "Blow [the shofar]..."

It is well known that the Baal Shem Tov (in *Kesser Shem Tov*) notes a precision in this verse: it should have said, "Blow the shofar in the month." The Baal Shem Tov explains that a person must always see to it that he is "in the month" (*b'chodesh*)—meaning, to renew his good deeds so that they do not become a fixed, stale habit. This can be explained as follows: since this verse deals with essential and general matters—the essence of Rosh Hashanah and the concept of "make Me King over you"—it contains an essential and general instruction for the overall avodah of every Jew. The avodah must be in a manner of renewal, such that every time, every moment, and in every single thing one does, one performs it as if it were something entirely new. [Perhaps one could add that this is also the precision of the word *tik'u* (blow), which also relates to strength and force (*tokef*); one should "stake" himself with the greatest strength and force without any changes in his avodah, until he stakes himself in an eternal manner, whereby every moment of his avodah is something new.] 

This explains the connection to what was said previously in the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov there: that the nature of a miracle is that a thing is renewed for the first time, and when it happens again, it is called "nature"—meaning that in truth, everything is a miracle. This should be connected with the Baal Shem Tov’s teaching that everything in the world is renewed every moment, and what is called "nature" is only because the renewal already happened previously (and is not being renewed now for the first time). Through contemplation of this very fact—that in truth everything is a miracle and a new thing—one is given the strength for every aspect of avodah to perform it in a manner of something entirely new.

The maamar (of 5707) continues that *keseh* also means a time and a set season (*zman u'mo'ed*), implying there is a limited time and season to draw down the aspect of Malchus. The connection of this (that *keseh* means time and season) to Rosh Hashanah can be explained based on the well-known teaching of the Maggid of Mezeritch [brought in the discourse of the Alter Rebbe printed in the *Siddur im Dach*, written by his son and successor, the Mitteler Rebbe] that time is a created thing. From this, it is understood that just as a renewal in the creation of the entire world is effected on Rosh Hashanah, as the saying goes, "This day is the beginning of Your works, a remembrance of the first day," so too, Rosh Hashanah is the renewal of the entire creation of the parameters of time and space and their existence (for time and space are the parameters of the world). 

The matter is as follows: it is known (in *Likkutei Torah*, etc.) that the world was created on the 25th of Elul, and Rosh Hashanah is the sixth day of creation, the day of the creation of the first man. What, then, is "the beginning of Your works, a remembrance of the first day"? It is explained there that although the creation of the entire world took place in the first moment of the six days of creation, as it is written, "In the beginning G-d created the heavens and the earth," and certainly and *a fortiori* the parameters of time and space and their existence (which are the parameters of the world, as mentioned) were created then, nevertheless, the perfection of creation was specifically on the sixth day, the day of the creation of man. Therefore, this day specifically is called "the beginning of Your works" and "a remembrance of the first day." 

With this, we can also understand why this day is called *keseh* (covering), which is a time and season: even though the formation of the parameters of time and space and their existence occurred in the first moment, nevertheless, this day specifically is the perfection of the formation of the parameter and existence of time (and space), because it is the day of the creation of the first man. For the first man brought about perfection in all of creation through the drawing down of Elokus (His sovereignty) into creation. This is what is meant by *Adam* (man), from the expression *adameh l'elyon* (I will be like the Most High)—that he drew down His Elokus below to make for Him a dwelling place in the lower worlds within the parameters of time and space.

Just as this happened the first time—that the first man then brought about perfection in all of creation, including the matter of time—so too it is every single year through the avodah of every single Jew, who is also called *Adam* from the expression *adameh l'elyon*. This means that every single Jew, through his avodah, draws down His Elokus below. On the contrary, man was created as a single individual to teach you that whoever sustains one soul of Israel is as if he sustained an entire world. That is, the fact that the first man was created as a single individual—*adameh* (I will be like) the Unique One of the world (the aspect of *Yachid* which is above the aspect of *Echad*)—is because of one soul of Israel.

The explanation of the matter (why the drawing down of His sovereignty on Rosh Hashanah is connected specifically to a certain time and specifically to the avodah of Israel) is as follows: it is known that the ultimate purpose of creation is to make for Him a dwelling place in the lower worlds. This means that the revelation of Elokus below (the dwelling place for Him) must be specifically within the parameters of the lower worlds—within the parameters of time and space—and the making of this dwelling is through Israel. For this reason, the drawing down of Malchus on Rosh Hashanah is specifically within the parameters of time and specifically through the avodah of Israel. 

This is what is written, "You have affirmed Hashem this day..." The Tzemach Tzedek and the Rebbe Maharash explain this (in the discourse *Zeh* of the year 5630) with the concept of *l'chatchila ariber*. *Ha'amarta* (you have affirmed) has three interpretations, and the first (the primary) is that *ha'amarta* is an expression of praise and *taanug* (delight). "This day" refers to Rosh Hashanah, meaning that "You have affirmed Hashem this day" refers to the drawing down of *taanug* on Rosh Hashanah. As explained in other places, this is also why the shofar of Rosh Hashanah is the matter of *taanug*; *shofar* is from the expression *shafru maaseichem* (improve your deeds). From this, it is understood that the primary avodah of Rosh Hashanah is to complete the *taanug* and desire Above to make for Him a dwelling place in the lower worlds, and this is done through the avodah of Israel—specifically *ha'amarta*.

The maamarim continue that the construction of Malchus—which is on Rosh Hashanah, as explained in the writings of the Arizal—is accomplished through the avodah of "make Me King over you." For this, there must be the removal of all concealments and obscurations, etc. We can explain the sequence of these matters with what was explained earlier: since on Rosh Hashanah Malchus is covered and hidden, the avodah of "make Me King over you" is through the prerequisite of nullifying the covering, concealment, and obscuration. This includes nullifying all distracting matters [including nullifying the distractions of the body, so that there will be a swift and complete healing, etc.], and on the contrary, that it should be in a manner of "double the wisdom". 

After the prerequisite of removing the concealments and obscurations (which is the matter of refining the vessel), there must be avodah with *ratzon* (will)—which creates a strength of light much greater than the drawing down that comes through refining the vessel—as explained in the maamar. It can be said that this is the matter of accepting His sovereignty in simplicity, which is specifically with *ratzon*, as in "His sovereignty they accepted upon themselves with *ratzon*". Through the avodah of nullifying the concealments and obscurations, and the avodah of "His sovereignty they accepted with *ratzon*," the construction of Malchus is accomplished, leading to the perfection therein: the unification of *Havayah* and *Elokim*, the unification of light and abundance, until even the concealment of the name *Elokim* (the aspect of Malchus) itself shines. This is what is meant by Malchus being built as a *partzuf*; the advantage of the *partzuf* of Malchus (over the mere point within it) is that it is in a state of revealed light, as explained there at length.

The maamar continues that this is also the explanation of the text of the *Shofaros* blessing: "Who hears the sound of the *teruah* of Your people Israel with mercy." The *teruah* is the matter of breaking the materiality and the *yeshus* (selfhood), through which one causes Malchus to be in a state of revealed light. Therefore, on a Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos (as is the case with the first day of Rosh Hashanah in this year's calendar), we do not blow the shofar, because on Shabbos the avodah is not in a manner of breaking and refining, for "on Shabbos, refining is forbidden". The reason for this is that on Shabbos, there is the perfection and truth of the matter of physicality, and in that state, there is no need for the avodah of refinement, etc. 

The maamar continues that, nevertheless, in the Beis Hamikdash they would blow even on Shabbos, because in the Beis Hamikdash, the *yesh* (existence) is in its rectified state, as it is in the most select place. This means that the physical is as it should be; therefore, in the Beis Hamikdash, they would blow even on Shabbos, for this is not a matter of refinement, but an act of higher perfection in the perfected physical. Perhaps one might add to this, based on what is known that in the Holy of Holies, the place of the Ark is "not of the measurement"—meaning that there was in it a matter of measurement and limitation, "measuring ten cubits to every side", and similarly, the Ark itself had measurement and limitation, "two and a half cubits its length, and a cubit and a half its width, and a cubit and a half its height"—and together with this, it is "not of the measurement," because the *yesh* is in its rectified state, where the measurement itself is without limit.

It can be said that in the inwardness of the matter, the same drawing down that was drawn by the blowing in the Beis Hamikdash (on Shabbos) is also drawn in every place and at every time. Just as even when we do not blow when Rosh Hashanah falls on Shabbos, nevertheless, through the recitation of the verses of *Malchiyos*, *Zichronos*, and *Shofaros*, the matter that was drawn in the Beis Hamikdash is drawn down. That is, the recitation of the verses in every place and at every time draws down that same drawing of the construction of Malchus, as is also understood from the fact that the verses one recites (in every place and at every time) are specifically ten in number, corresponding to the ten Sefiros from which the construction of Malchus is made. This means that through the recitation, one effects a complete construction, etc. 

It can be said that even more than this: in every place and at every time, the aforementioned drawing that comes through the blowing in the Beis Hamikdash is also drawn down. The explanation for this is that it is known from our Sages on the verse, "And I will dwell within them" (*b'socham*)—it does not say "within it" (*b'socho*), but "within them" (*b'socham*), within every single Jew. Thus, within every single Jew there is the aspect of the aforementioned Beis Hamikdash (in which they would blow even on Shabbos). Furthermore, this verse ("And I will dwell within them") is said (simply) regarding the Tabernacle that Moses made, and this Tabernacle is eternal, like all the works of Moses’ hands, which are eternal. Especially based on what is explained in *Tanya* that there is an aspect of Moses within every single Jew, which permeates him (*un nemt im durch*). And especially since there is in every single Jew also the aspect of the aforementioned Ark ("not of the measurement"), which is the aspect of the inwardness of the heart, as explained in *Likkutei Torah* and the discourses of the Mitteler Rebbe. 

Thus, it is in the power of every single Jew to effect, on a Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos, the elevation of physicality even above the ultimate perfection that was in the Beis Hamikdash, just as through the blowing of the shofar in the Beis Hamikdash. Especially based on what is brought in the continuation of the maamarim from the *Zohar* that on Shabbos there occurs the "spreading of this point," which is the complete construction of the Sefirah of Malchus so that it will be in a state of revealed light. And especially based on what is explained in the well-known continuation that every Shabbos is a foretaste of the future. From all this, it is understood that through the avodah of Israel regarding "Blow the shofar on the new moon, at the covering for our festival day" on Rosh Hashanah, they effect Above the construction of Malchus in ultimate perfection, until it effects below also in this physical world. As explained in the aforementioned continuation of the maamarim, the true avodah is that even the physical *yesh* is in the state of a vessel for the *Ohr Ein Sof*, and this is "G-d ascended with a *teruah*"—the ascent of the name *Elokim*, i.e., the measurement and limitation of the world that was created by the name *Elokim* (as it is written, "In the beginning G-d (*Elokim*) created," specifically), and the measure of the ascent is that it is included in the name *Havayah*, and the name *Havayah* also ascends with the sound of the shofar, which is the matter of the unification of *Havayah* and *Elokim*—a new action in the name *Havayah* and in the name *Elokim*.

May it be His will that this should be so for us, that all the aforementioned matters be drawn down, and included in this, in simplicity, is that there be a good inscription and sealing immediately for a good life in the book of the perfectly righteous, and in the future, "He will cause the righteous to return in repentance", in the true and complete Redemption through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, as explained in the continuation there, that the *teruah* is Jacob and David—David the King Mashiach, Mashiach Tzidkeinu, speedily in our days, truly.

## Haazinu, Shabbos Shuvah 5723

"And no man shall be in the Ohel Moed when he enters to make atonement in the Kodesh, etc." It is stated in the Yerushalmi that this includes even those of whom it is written, "and the likeness of their faces was the face of a man." The Tzemach Tzedek explains in his maamar on this subject that the revelation drawn down when the Kohen Gadol entered the Kodesh HaKodashim is a revelation that transcends Seder Hishtalshelus. As it is stated in the Midrash regarding the verse, "With this shall Aharon come," by what merit did Aharon enter the Kodesh HaKodashim? The merit of the mitzvah of milah entered with him, and milah was given on the eighth day, for the eighth is above Seder Hishtalshelus. As the Midrash states regarding the verse, "The sod of Hashem is for those who fear Him," what is the sod of Hashem? It is milah. The revelations of Seder Hishtalshelus are from the aspect of the "revealed" of the Ribono shel Olam, while the revelation drawn down through milah is from the aspect of the "hidden" of the Ribono shel Olam; therefore, it is called the "sod of Hashem," for a sod is that which is hidden and concealed. At the time the Kohen Gadol would enter the Kodesh HaKodashim, the revelation of the "sod of Hashem" was drawn down. Therefore, even those of whom it is written "and the likeness of their faces was the face of a man" were not there, for in the face of this revelation, there is no place for created beings—not even for the angels whose likeness is the face of a man.

It must be understood: from the fact that the Kohen Gadol’s ability to enter the Kodesh HaKodashim is through the merit of the milah given on the eighth day, it is proven that the revelation transcending Seder Hishtalshelus that existed in the Kodesh HaKodashim was due to the inherent quality of the Kodesh HaKodashim itself (and not that the revelation was drawn down by the Kohen Gadol’s entry). Nevertheless, the fact that "no man shall be... even those of whom it is written 'and the likeness of their faces was the face of a man'" applies specifically when the Kohen Gadol enters the Kodesh HaKodashim to make atonement. From this, it is understood that the revelation drawn down through the avodah of the Kohen Gadol in the Kodesh HaKodashim is higher than the revelation that existed in the Kodesh HaKodashim by its own nature.

2) This can be understood based on what is explained in the maamar that teshuvah (and kapparah) are in the category of the "eighth" which is above Seder Hishtalshelus. For "great is teshuvah, for it brings healing to the world," and healing was established on the eighth day; this establishment of healing on the eighth day is analogous to milah, which was given on the eighth day. This is the reason that seven days before Yom Kippur, the Kohen Gadol is separated from his house, and for all seven days, he is accustomed to the avodah, for the seven days are the category of Seder Hishtalshelus, and Yom Kippur—the eighth day (following the seven days)—is above Seder Hishtalshelus. This is also the concept of the eight sprinklings on Yom Kippur, one above and seven below, where the "one" that is above the seven is the aspect of "one thing I have asked," "one is my dove." From this, it is understood that within the "eighth" that is above Seder Hishtalshelus, there are several levels: the eighth of milah; similarly, the eighth of the Kodesh HaKodashim, for the entry into the Kodesh HaKodashim is through the merit of milah; the eighth of teshuvah, which is higher than the eighth of milah (for milah is a mitzvah, and teshuvah, which atones for matters that damaged Torah and mitzvos, is above Torah and mitzvos, as will be explained in section 8); and the eighth of Yom Kippur, for the teshuvah of Yom Kippur is higher than the teshuvah of the entire year (even higher than the teshuvah of the Aseres Yemei Teshuvah), which is why Yom Kippur is a time of teshuvah for all. And on Yom Kippur itself—the eight sprinklings in the Kodesh HaKodashim, one above and seven below, where the one is before the seven.

It can be said that the "eighth" that follows the seven is a revelation that transcends Seder Hishtalshelus that is drawn down after and through the matters of Seder Hishtalshelus; from the fact that the drawing down of this revelation is through the matters of Seder Hishtalshelus, it is proven that this revelation belongs to Seder Hishtalshelus. The "one" that is before the seven is a revelation that transcends Seder Hishtalshelus that is above any connection to Seder Hishtalshelus [though *afterwards* the revelation is drawn down into Seder Hishtalshelus—the seven that follow the one, as will be explained in section 7]. Based on this, one can understand the reason why "no man shall be... etc." applies specifically when he comes to make atonement in the Kodesh: because in relation to the revelation that existed in the Kodesh HaKodashim by its own nature [which is analogous to the eighth of milah, the eighth that follows the seven], which is above Seder Hishtalshelus but still connected to Seder Hishtalshelus, there is a place for existence. But through the avodah of the Kohen Gadol on Yom Kippur in the Kodesh HaKodashim, the revelation that transcends Seder Hishtalshelus that is above any connection to Seder Hishtalshelus is drawn down, and in the face of this revelation, "no man shall be," not even those whose likeness is the face of a man.

3) This can be understood by prefacing what is written, "For this mitzvah which I command you today," and it is explained in Likutei Torah that all mitzvos are called the mitzvos of Hashem, but "this mitzvah," which is the mitzvah of teshuvah, is "I command you"—*Anchi* (I), He who I am (which is above Hashem). The concept is that the purpose of the mitzvos is to draw down a revelation of the name Hashem into the world. For the creation of the world is from the name Elokim, and Elokim in gematria is *hateva* (nature), and through the mitzvos, a revelation of the name Hashem, which is above nature, is drawn into the world. The primary revelation of Hashem through the performance of mitzvos is in the person who performs the mitzvos (and through this, the revelation is drawn into the world as well). This is why the first utterance of the Giving of the Torah is "Anchi Hashem Elokecha"—the purpose of the mitzvos given at the Giving of the Torah is that Hashem should be *your* Elokim.

Now, the fact that through the mitzvos it is possible to draw down a revelation of the name Hashem into the world, even though the formation of the world is through the name Elokim, which conceals and hides the name Hashem, is because the intent of the concealment of the name Elokim (from the outset) is so that through the performance of Torah and mitzvos, the revelation of Hashem will be drawn down. As it is written, "For Elokim made man upright"—the intent in the formation from the name Elokim (Elokim made) is that man, through the performance of mitzvos, should continue to be "upright," that he should behold His countenance, so that below there will be a revelation just as it is above. And when "they sought many calculations" (the opposite of the intent of creation), then in order for the revelation of the name Hashem to be drawn into man, and through him into the world, the drawing down must be from *Anchi*, which is above Hashem; this drawing down is through teshuvah, which is above the mitzvos. As it is written, "Return, O Yisrael, unto Hashem your Elokim, for you have stumbled in your iniquity"—in order for Hashem to be *your* Elokim after you have stumbled in your iniquity, the performance of mitzvos from this point forward is not sufficient, and there must be "Return, O Yisrael."

4) The explanation of this in greater detail can be understood by prefacing what our Sages said: They asked Wisdom, "What is the atonement for a sinful soul?" and it replied, "The sinful soul shall die." They asked Prophecy, and it replied, "Sins pursue evil." They asked Torah, and it replied, "He shall bring a guilt offering and he will be atoned for." They asked the Ribono shel Olam, and He said, "He shall do teshuvah and he will be atoned for." This is what is written, "Good and upright is Hashem, therefore He instructs sinners in the way." It can be said that the fact that Wisdom said that through death there will be atonement for the sinful soul is because Wisdom, too, is above the light that is enclothed in the world. From the perspective of the light that is enclothed in the world, since sin is the opposite of the intent of the world's creation, it is impossible for there to be atonement for it; the fact that from the perspective of Wisdom there is an atonement for the sinful soul is because Wisdom is above the world. [Even though the intent of "Wisdom" here is *Chochma Tatta'ah*, the Chochma within Malchus, even the Chochma within Malchus is above Malchus itself.] However, from the perspective of Wisdom, the atonement is through death—the separation of the soul from the body. [It can be said that this is *middah k'neged middah*. For the purpose of the mitzvos is to draw Hashem into Elokim, for Elokim is the root of the *keilim* (vessels), which are analogous to the body, and the light of Hashem drawn into them is analogous to the life-force of the soul that vivifies the body. And since through sins he caused that the revelation of Hashem should not be drawn into Elokim, therefore his punishment (which is his atonement) is the separation of the soul from the body, *middah k'neged middah*.] And from the perspective of Prophecy, which is in Netzach and Hod (above *Chochma Tatta'ah*), the sinful soul is atoned for through suffering as well. The innovation in the answer of Torah, and all the more so in the answer of the Ribono shel Olam, is that the atonement is through a sacrifice and teshuvah, and there is no need for suffering. The difference between the answer of Torah and the answer of the Ribono shel Olam—even though the answer of Torah, "he shall bring a guilt offering and he will be atoned for," is also the concept of teshuvah (for only then does the sacrifice atone)—is explained by the Tzemach Tzedek in the aforementioned maamar: from the perspective of Torah, the wisdom of the Ribono shel Olam, through teshuvah, the willful sins become (only) like unintentional errors (and through the sacrifice, this unintentional error is atoned for), whereas from the perspective of the Ribono shel Olam, who is above Torah, through teshuvah the willful sins are *atoned for*, and furthermore, they are transformed into merits.

Based on what was brought above (section 3) that teshuvah is in the Ohr Ein Sof that is above Seder Hishtalshelus, it can be said that the reason why teshuvah is effective even from the perspective of Torah (that willful sins become like unintentional errors), even though Torah is in Chochma (Seder Hishtalshelus), is because in Chochma resides the Ohr Ein Sof that is above Chochma; and it is similar regarding Torah as it is drawn down into the *middos* of Chesed, Gevurah, and Tiferes, for Torah is in Tiferes, the middle line, and Tiferes ascends to Keter. However, the light that is above Seder Hishtalshelus that is in Torah, since it is drawn down into Seder Hishtalshelus (in Chochma and Tiferes), is in a state of measurement and limitation. Therefore, the power in teshuvah from the Ohr Ein Sof within Torah is only that they become like unintentional errors. But the answer of the Ribono shel Olam is the drawing down of the Ohr Ein Sof that is above Seder Hishtalshelus as it is in its essence (which is above drawing down into Seder Hishtalshelus), and this drawing down is above measurement and limitation. Therefore, through the teshuvah that is from the answer of the Ribono shel Olam, the willful sins are *atoned for* (and not just made like unintentional errors), and furthermore, they are transformed into merits.

5) It can be said that the fact that the answer of the Ribono shel Olam, which is above Torah, is revealed through Torah is because the fact that Torah is in the middle line is because its root is in the essence of the Ohr Ein Sof that is above the lines, but afterwards it was drawn down into the middle line. Therefore, in addition to the fact that it contains the light that is above Seder Hishtalshelus as it is drawn down into the middle line (which is why even from the perspective of the answer of Torah, teshuvah is effective so that willful sins become like unintentional errors), through it is also revealed the essence of the Ohr Ein Sof that is above the lines—the answer of the Ribono shel Olam, "He shall do teshuvah and he will be atoned for." That is to say, the fact that Torah said, "He shall bring a guilt offering and he will be atoned for" (that through teshuvah willful sins become only like unintentional errors) is at the level of Torah as it is drawn down into the middle line, and the fact that the answer of the Ribono shel Olam, "He shall do teshuvah and he will be atoned for," is also a part of Torah (the Oral Torah) is from the perspective of the root of Torah in the essence of the Ohr Ein Sof that is above the lines.

Based on this, one can explain what our Sages said: " 'He shall come'—this is Moshe... 'and he shall receive'—this is Torah... 'from the Good'—this is the Ribono shel Olam, as it is written, 'Hashem is good to all,' 'to the good'—these are Yisrael, as it is written, 'Hashem has done good to the good.' " It must be understood, for it is stated in the Gemara Sanhedrin: It is written, "Hashem is good to all," and it is written, "Hashem is good to those who wait for Him." It is like a man who has an orchard; when he waters, he waters the whole thing, but when he weeds, he only weeds the good ones among them. And since the Giving of the Torah is (only) to "the good"—these are Yisrael—it should seemingly have brought the verse, "Hashem is good to those who wait for Him." [And based on the known principle that "Hashem is good to all" is an external influence, and "Hashem is good to those who wait for Him" is an internal influence, the fact that it brings the verse "Hashem is good to all" regarding the Giving of the Torah, which is an internal influence, requires further explanation.] The explanation for this can be said to be that the fact that regarding teshuvah it is written "Good and upright is Hashem," the intent is to the aspect of "Hashem is good to all," for teshuvah atones even for willful sins. Therefore, it says, "and he shall receive 'the Good'—this is Torah... from 'the Good'—this is the Ribono shel Olam, as it is written, 'Hashem is good to all,' " because the "Good" of Torah, from its root, is the aspect of "Good and upright is Hashem," "Hashem is good to all."

6) Now, even though the fact that teshuvah atones is because the answer that comes through teshuvah is from above Seder Hishtalshelus, the atonement through teshuvah is (not in the manner of a waiver, where one waives the sins, but) an internal action within the person who returns, that his flaws are repaired. And this is "Good and upright is Hashem, therefore He instructs sinners in the way," for "Good" is that He influences all. As is explained elsewhere, the difference between "Good" and "Chesed": Chesed belongs to the recipient, and therefore the influence of Chesed is specifically to one who is worthy [though from the perspective of the attribute of Chesed, it is not meticulous, and when the recipient has some merit, he is already worthy of being influenced], whereas "Good" is from the perspective of the influencer, and therefore the influence is to all (even to those who are not worthy). And "upright" is that below is like above. And this is that the drawing down through teshuvah is from the aspect of "Good and upright," for the fact that teshuvah atones for willful sins is not because the person is worthy of this, but because of the "Good" of the Ribono shel Olam—the drawing down of the Ohr Ein Sof that is above Seder Hishtalshelus. And the fact that through this drawing down his flaws are repaired, and the repair is in the internal aspect, is because the drawing down of the "Good" is in the manner of Chesed, so that the person who returns is like a vessel for the influence.

7) Now, the fact that through teshuvah willful sins are atoned for, and even transformed into merits, is true throughout the entire year. And all the more so during the Aseres Yemei Teshuvah, when teshuvah is higher. And the quality of the teshuvah of Yom Kippur is that the atonement of Yom Kippur is from the *essence of the day*, and this atonement is higher than the atonement through teshuvah. The fact that Yom Kippur only atones for those who return is because the drawing down of the matter of the "essence of the day" *to the person* is through teshuvah. The concept is that the fact that through teshuvah the matters that were done contrary to the Will are atoned for is because through teshuvah, one draws from the essence of the Ohr Ein Sof, the "Master of Will," who is not compelled by the Will. For the fact that the "Master of Will" is not compelled by the Will is the aspect of the *wonder* of the Ohr Ein Sof, and not the essence of the Ohr Ein Sof itself. And since the concept of the wonder of the "Master of Will" is that He is wondrous *from the Will*, this proves that the Will has a connection to Him [as is also proven from the fact that He is called by the name "Master of *Will*"], therefore there must be forgiveness and atonement for the fact that they acted contrary to the Will. This is not the case regarding the essence of the Ohr Ein Sof, who is above even the "Master of Will," for regarding this level, there is no flaw from the outset. And this is the concept of "the essence of the day atones"—that the atonement from the essence of the day is (not in the manner of an action, but) that on this day, the connection of the essence of the soul with the Essence (that is above the "Master of Will") is revealed, and regarding this level, there is no flaw from the outset. The reason that the drawing down of the matter of the "essence of the day" is through teshuvah is because the repair of flaws through the revelation of the essence of the soul (for from the perspective of the essence of the soul, no flaw is possible from the outset) does not (so much) relate to the matters of the revealed powers themselves, and in order for the atonement through the revelation of the essence of the soul to be in the internal aspect, it is through teshuvah.

[It should be added that the drawing down of the atonement of the "essence of the day" into the revealed powers through teshuvah—since the concept of teshuvah is that the person *exits* his limitations—is why the drawing down through teshuvah is into the powers as they are in the movement of *ascension*. And in order for the atonement of the "essence of the day" to be drawn down also into the revealed powers as they are in their configuration, it is through the *mitzvah* of teshuvah, for on Yom Kippur everyone is obligated to do teshuvah. It should be noted that these three matters on Yom Kippur are analogous to the three matters in *Teki'as Shofar*: the mitzvah of *Teki'as Shofar*, the concept of teshuvah hinted at in *Teki'as Shofar*, and the internal *bitul* that is from the essence of the soul.] It can be said that this is the concept of the eight sprinklings that were in the Kodesh HaKodashim, one above and seven below; the "one above" is the revelation of the *Yechidah*, which is unified with the Essence, and the "seven below" (*after* the one) is that the revelation of the *Yechidah* (above Seder Hishtalshelus in man) should be drawn down into the revealed powers, the Seder Hishtalshelus in man.

8) Based on this, one can explain the difference between the three matters of the "eighth": the eighth of milah, the eighth of teshuvah, and the eighth of Yom Kippur. Milah is a mitzvah, and since the intent of creation from the outset is that Yisrael should perform all the mitzvos and draw down the revelations that are drawn down through them, therefore the revelations drawn down through the mitzvos (including the mitzvah of milah) are not a truly "new light," since the intent of creation from the outset is that Yisrael should draw down these revelations. And even though the revelation drawn down through all the mitzvos is the revelation of Hashem, and the revelation drawn down through milah is above Hashem—as is explained in Torah Or that "who shall ascend for us to heaven" is an acronym for *milah* and the final letters are *Hashem*, which indicates that milah is above Hashem—nevertheless, since the creation of the worlds from the outset is that through the mitzvah of milah they should draw down this light, it is a light that has a connection to the worlds. [It can be said that, in general, even this light is included in Hashem. For Hashem, from the root *mehaveh* (bringing into being), has two levels: the source from which the formation of the world is made, and the will to bring the world into being. And since the will to bring into being is that the revelation through milah should also be drawn down, this revelation is included in Hashem. And the fact that the revelation through milah is above Hashem means that this revelation is above the source of formation.] The eighth of teshuvah is the revelation of a new light. For through the fact that he sinned and blemished and went off the path (the opposite of the intent of the creation of man, which was in the manner of "For Elokim made man upright") and afterwards repairs this through teshuvah, through this a new light is drawn down that did not (so to speak) exist even in the intent of creation. However, this light is from the "Master of Will," who has a connection to the Will and the intent of creation. The eighth of Yom Kippur, that "the essence of the day atones," is the revelation of the essence of the Ohr Ein Sof that is above even the "Master of Will." However, the revelation of this matter is primarily at the time of the Kohen Gadol's entry into the Kodesh HaKodashim. Therefore, even the eighth of Yom Kippur is an eighth that follows the seven days before Yom Kippur, for in general, even the eighth of Yom Kippur is above Seder Hishtalshelus that is connected to Seder Hishtalshelus (the "Master of Will"), unlike the order of the eight sprinklings in the Kodesh HaKodashim, where the one is before the seven.

9) And this is "And no man shall be in the Ohel Moed when he enters to make atonement in the Kodesh"—the fact that "no man shall be... even those whose likeness is the face of a man" applies specifically when the Kohen Gadol enters the Kodesh HaKodashim to make atonement, because the revelation that transcends Seder Hishtalshelus that existed in the Kodesh HaKodashim by its own nature has a connection to Seder Hishtalshelus, and in relation to this revelation, there is a place for angels. But when he enters (the Kohen Gadol into the Kodesh HaKodashim) to make atonement, the fact that through the avodah of the Kohen Gadol in the Kodesh HaKodashim atonement is drawn down for all Yisrael is because through his avodah, the connection of the essence of the soul with the Essence is revealed, and in the essence of the Ohr Ein Sof, there is no place for angels—"Yisrael and the King alone." Therefore, at that time, "no man shall be," not even those whose likeness is the face of a man.

To add, in the prayer of *Ne'ilah*, this revelation shines even more than in the *Musaf* of Yom Kippur, in which we recite the *Seder HaAvodah* of the Kohen Gadol on Yom Kippur. For the five prayers of Yom Kippur correspond to the five levels of the soul: *Nefesh, Ruach, Neshamah, Chayah,* and *Yechidah*. The first three prayers are in *Nefesh, Ruach, Neshamah*, the *Musaf* prayer of Yom Kippur is in *Chayah*, and the revelation of *Yechidah* is (primarily) in the *Ne'ilah* prayer. Therefore, it is called by the name *Ne'ilah* (locking), for then all the gates are locked, and even the highest created beings (and even *Atzilut*, etc.) cannot enter—"Yisrael and the King alone."

This is also what is written in the Zohar regarding the verse, "And no man stood with him when Yosef made himself known to his brothers"—at the time when the Ribono shel Olam is connected with them, Yisrael, etc., therefore, "on the eighth day, it shall be a *Atzeres* (gathering) for you," for it can be said that the matter of the Ribono shel Olam being connected with them, with Yisrael, is the revelation of *Yechidah* and the essential connection of the Essence with the essence of the soul. [It can be said that the connection of this matter to Yosef is that the name Yosef is from the phrase, "Hashem will add (*yosef*) to me another son," that even an "other" becomes a son, for through the revelation of the essential connection of the Essence in Yisrael (when Yosef made himself known to his brothers), it is revealed that even an "other" is a son.] And this revelation is on Shemini Atzeres, and as is known, on Shemini Atzeres there are all the matters of Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, but on Shemini Atzeres they are in the line of joy, and therefore on the eighth day, "it shall be *for you*—it shall be for you alone, and no stranger with you."

## B’s”D. Shabbos Parshas Haazinu, Shabbos Shuvah, 5736

“Return, O Israel, to Hashem your G-d, for you have stumbled in your iniquity. Take with you words... say to Him: ‘Forgive all iniquity... and we will pay [with] our lips [the] bulls [that we owe].’” 

It is well known that there is a specific point of inquiry in the discourses of our Rebbes regarding the phrase “Return, O Israel.” Seemingly, since the subject is *teshuvah*, it should have said “Return, O Yaakov,” rather than “Return, O Yisrael,” which is the name denoting a higher level. *Yisrael* is derived from the expression, “For you have contended (*sarisa*) with G-d and with men, and you have prevailed,” and the reason for the name *Yisrael* is this *sarisa* (contention/mastery). By contrast, *Yaakov* is derived from the word *eikev* (heel), and the reason for the name *Yaakov* is because he held onto the *eikev* (heel) —and not just any heel, but the heel of Esav. If so, it should have said “Return, O Yaakov,” so why does it say “Return, O Yisrael”? This is especially true in light of the statement, “He did not see... in Yisrael,” (which is not the case regarding *Yaakov*).

Furthermore, it is even more difficult to understand the continuation of the verse, “for you have stumbled in your iniquity.” *Avon* (iniquity) refers to *zedonos* (intentional sins). Yet, even regarding *shegagos* (unintentional sins), it is stated in *Iggeres HaKodesh* —citing *Likutei Torah* from the Arizal —that *shegagos* stem from the strengthening of the *nefesh habahamit* (which is the reason for what Rashi says in *Maseches Shevuos* that even one who sins unintentionally requires atonement). If so, even *shegagos* are not applicable to the level of *Yisrael*, and certainly not the matter of “you have stumbled in your iniquity,” which refers to *zedonos*. Why, then, does it say “Return, O Yisrael” and not “Return, O Yaakov”?

We must also understand the phrase “Take with you words.” Seemingly, when it says “Return, O Yisrael,” the intention is *teshuvah* in its proper form. Since it is a *mitzvah* to confess (when one performs *teshuvah*), “Return, O Yisrael” already includes the matter of confession. Even though the essence of *teshuvah* is in the heart, confession is also one of the essentials of *teshuvah*. If so, what is added by “Take with you words”?

Furthermore, we must understand the phrase “Say to Him: ‘Forgive all iniquity.’” Seemingly, since he has already performed *teshuvah* (“Return, O Yisrael”), it is certain that his sins have been forgiven. Why is it necessary to say and ask, “Forgive all iniquity”?

Finally, we must understand the phrase that follows, “and we will pay [with] our lips [the] bulls [that we owe].” The verse lists here several exalted levels (first “Return, O Yisrael,” then “Take with you words,” then “Say to Him,” until it arrives at “and we will pay [with] our lips [the] bulls”), yet after all the preceding matters, it concludes only with “we will pay [with] our lips [the] bulls,” which is merely in place of the *korbanos* (sacrifices), not the *korbanos* themselves.

The matter is as follows: The general concept of *teshuvah* is a matter of *dilug* (leaping), as explained previously (in the *sichos*), above the order of *seder, hadragah* (gradation), and *hishtalshelus* (chain-like descent). This is why *teshuvah* can be accomplished in a single hour and a single moment. Matters that follow a *hadragah* require time, and the more exalted the matter, the more time is required—as in the saying, “A person does not grasp the mind of his teacher until forty years have passed,” because due to the great loftiness of the matter (the teacher’s mind), a vast amount of time is needed. However, the matter of *teshuvah* (despite being an exalted matter) is accomplished in a single hour and a single moment, because the *avodah* of *teshuvah* is with *chaila yatir* (extra strength), in a manner of *dilug* that is not according to *hadragah*.

The *dilug* in *teshuvah* is that it leaps from the opposite state. This means it is not merely an incomparable ascent like the ascents within *kedushah* itself; rather, it leaps from an opposite state. This is true even in ordinary *teshuvah*, where one was initially on the side of the opposite, and in a single moment becomes a *baal teshuvah*. This is especially true in *teshuvah* from *ahavah* (love), where intentional sins are transformed into merits. This is a leap from the lowest extremity to the highest extremity: initially, he had not just ordinary sins but *zedonos*—the lowest extremity—and through *teshuvah*, they become merits, and these merits (transformed from *zedonos*) are even more exalted than the merits of the *tzaddikim*—the highest extremity.

It is known how the Alter Rebbe interprets the Sages’ statement, “The ultimate goal of wisdom is *teshuvah* and good deeds.” The reason *teshuvah* precedes good deeds is that through the priority of *teshuvah*, the deeds become “good deeds” that radiate light. The *avodah* of *teshuvah* also impacts the *avodah* of *Torah* and *mitzvos*. Therefore, since the *avodah* of *teshuvah* is by way of *dilug*—not according to *hadragah*—it causes the *avodah* of *Torah* and *mitzvos*, and even the *avodah* of “let all your deeds be for the sake of Heaven,” to also be in a manner that is above *seder* and *hadragah*, similar to the *dilug* in *teshuvah*.

This is explained at length in the *Hemshech* 5666 in the *maamar* preceding the *d.h. Shuvah Yisrael*. It was also explained in the previous *maamar* (*d.h. Zeh HaYom*) that Torah study must be preceded by *bitul*, as it is written, “And let my soul be as dust to all,” and (only through this) “open my heart in Your Torah.” Even within the gradations of Torah study itself (when the levels are incomparable), one must first nullify the grasp of the previous level, just as Rabbi Zeira fasted one hundred fasts to forget the Babylonian Talmud in order to grasp the Jerusalem Talmud.

The same applies to the performance of *mitzvos*: there must also be an *avodah* that is above measurement and limitation, as it is written, “And you shall love Hashem your G-d with all your heart, and with all your soul, and with all your *me’odecha* (might).” It is not enough for the performance of *mitzvos* to be with the love of “with all your heart” and “with all your soul,” which is a love within limitation; rather, there must also be the love of “with all your *me’odecha*,” which is above measurement and limitation. This matter is incomparably higher than the ordinary performance of *mitzvos*, for the *avodah* of “with all your *me’odecha*” is the matter of “doing the will of the Omnipresent,” as is known from the teaching of the Maggid (meaning that he *makes* the will). From this, it is understood that it is incomparably higher than the (ordinary) performance of *mitzvos*, for in the (ordinary) performance of *mitzvos*, the will already exists and the person merely fulfills that will; whereas in the love of “with all your *me’odecha*,” he *makes* the will of the Omnipresent.
