This matter (of “with all your *me’odecha*”) must be present not only in the performance of *mitzvos* but also in the *avodah* of “let all your deeds be for the sake of Heaven.” For there is another interpretation of “with all your *me’odecha*,” as the Sages said, “with all your *me’odecha*—with all your wealth,” which is the matter of “all your deeds.” This interpretation of “with all your *me’odecha*” also relates to the aforementioned interpretation—namely, that even with all your wealth and all your deeds, you must love Hashem your G-d not only with all your heart and not only with all your soul, but also with all your *me’odecha*—above measurement and limitation. However, the *dilug* that is not according to *hadragah* in *Torah* and *mitzvos* and “all your deeds for the sake of Heaven” (which is brought about by the *dilug* of *teshuvah*) is that within *kedushah* itself, the ascent is in an incomparable manner, whereas in *teshuvah*, the *dilug* is from the side of the opposite. The *dilug* is from the lowest extremity to the highest extremity, as mentioned above.

The power for the *avodah* of *teshuvah*, which is by way of a complete *dilug* as mentioned above, comes from the level of *Yisrael*. Even though *Yisrael* are the letters of *li rosh* (to Me is the head), and the head has a connection to the body and the feet, nevertheless, *Yisrael* are the letters of *li rosh* (the head is *Mine*—nullified *to Him*—and according to the simple meaning of the word and the matter of *sarisa*—mastery and victory in battle). From this level, the *dilug* that is above measurement can occur. This is “Return, O Yisrael,” for the power to return comes specifically from the level of *Yisrael*. Since this level exists in every single Jew, even in one who “has stumbled in his iniquity,” as the Sages said, “A Jew, even if he has sinned, remains a Jew.” Indeed, it exists even in one who is like the person from whom we learn “A Jew, even if he has sinned, remains a Jew.” Therefore, everyone has the power for *teshuvah*. From this level, the matter of *teshuvah* can also occur in the level of *Yaakov*, for the essential and primary name of every Jew is *Yisrael*—meaning that this level is his essence and core—and therefore it also acts upon the level of *Yaakov*.

This is also why the verse continues, “for you have stumbled in your iniquity.” It is known from the teaching of our Rebbes that just as one must know his own shortcomings, he must also know his own virtues. Therefore, it says “for you have stumbled,” which is only a matter of a stumble—that because his foot tripped on a tree or a stone, this causes a stumble in the head, while the level of *Yisrael* is in perfection. Nevertheless, the matter of *teshuvah* must be in its full strength, for since he is of the level of *Yisrael*, he requires *teshuvah* even regarding this matter relative to his level. On the contrary, because of his exalted level, even a slight matter affects him more than a great defect in the level of *Yaakov*, similar to what is said in *Tanya* and in *Shabbos* regarding a speck on the garment of a *talmid chacham*, who is called *banayich* (your builders), and similar to what is explained in *Tanya* regarding a thought in the mind, which affects one much more than the matter of speech, etc.

The verse continues, “Take with you words.” In order to properly rectify the stumble (the fact that he tripped with his foot), the *teshuvah* of *bitul hametzius* (nullification of existence) that is accomplished thereby is not enough—which is achieved through the confession of words, the matter of the letters, as explained in *Sefer HaMitzvos* of the Tzemach Tzedek. For the ultimate goal of *teshuvah* is the *avodah* of *Torah* and *mitzvos* (as mentioned above regarding *teshuvah* and good deeds), and for this, it adds “Take with you words” of *Torah* and *mitzvos*, as explained in *Likutei Torah* on this verse. These words are specifically within measurement. This is not only regarding the Oral Torah—since the study of the Oral Torah must be specifically with understanding and grasp, it must necessarily be within measurement—but even regarding the Written Torah. Although the matter of grasp is not as relevant in the Written Torah, nevertheless, there must also be measurement in the Written Torah. On the contrary, the measurement of the Written Torah is greater than the measurement of the Oral Torah, for in the Written Torah, the letters, the crowns, and the cantillation marks must be with the utmost precision.

The verse continues, “Say to Him: ‘Forgive all iniquity.’” The matter is that the ultimate goal of *teshuvah* is not only that the sins be forgiven, but that the *zedonos* themselves be transformed into merits. This is why even after “Return, O Yisrael,” one must say, “Forgive all iniquity.” *Tisa avon* is the matter of *nosei avon* (bearing/lifting iniquity) —that He does not merely “subdue our iniquities and cast all their sins into the depths of the sea,” but that He is also *nosei avon*, that He carries and elevates the iniquity, so that the iniquities become merits, as explained at length in *Ohr HaTorah*. This matter (*tisa avon*) is accomplished by “Say to Him: ‘All.’” *Kol* (all) is what He, may He be blessed, is—He is *Kol* and includes everything. Therefore, *tisa avon*—that He bears even the iniquity and transforms it into merit.

The verse continues, “and we will pay [with] our lips [the] bulls [that we owe].” This means that through the perfection of *teshuvah* of “Return, O Yisrael,” etc., one achieves perfection in the matter of the *korbanos* after “you have stumbled in your iniquity.” The meaning of “we will pay [with] our lips [the] bulls” is not only that our lips are a payment for the bulls, but that our lips effect perfection in the “bulls.” Our lips are the matter of *tefillah* (prayer)—the matter of *korbanos* in spirituality, which is higher than the matter of *korbanos* in physicality. This is similar to the fact that even at the time of the Beis HaMikdash, when *korbanos* were offered literally, there still had to be the matter of the *Yisrael* in their standing, the *Leviim* in their platform, and the *Kohanim* in their *avodah*—and especially the *avodah* of the *Kohanim*, which is “in silence and with the desire of the heart.” This is what is meant by “and we will pay [with] our lips [the] bulls” after “Return, O Yisrael,” etc., for this matter (the payment of lips) is the elevation achieved through *teshuvah*, reaching elevation after elevation, higher even than the matter of *korbanos*, until one reaches the level of the “Face of Hashem.” As it is written, “Seek My face; Your face, Hashem, I will seek.” Through “Seek My face,” which is the matter of *teshuvah*, one reaches “Your face, Hashem, I will seek.” For in the manner of drawing down from above to below, it is written, “And you will see My back, but My face shall not be seen,” whereas in the manner of ascent, one reaches the level of “Your face, Hashem.” 

As it is regarding *avodah*—that although the *avodah* of *Torah* and *mitzvos* (in their own right) is within limitation, it also contains the matter of being above *hadragah* (which is effected by the *avodah* of *teshuvah*), and there is also the *dilug* of *teshuvah* which is higher even than the matter of being above *hadragah* in the *avodah* of *Torah* and *mitzvos*—so too is it regarding the reward. In addition to the three levels of *Gan Eden* (which are levels of *hishtalshelus*), there is the reward of “they shall go from strength to strength,” which are incomparable ascents. But even these ascents are only a radiance and a revelation, and afterwards there will be the drawing down of the *Etzem* (the Essence), which is higher than the matter of ascents and descents, to the point that it is not applicable to say of it that it is higher than the matter of ascents, for it is not in the category of ascent and descent, but rather “a day that is entirely Shabbos.” 

This is “Return, O Yisrael,” etc., which we say in the *Haftorah* of Shabbos Shuvah, for the *avodah* of Shabbos Shuvah rectifies all the Shabbosos of the past year and also acts upon all the Shabbosos of the coming year. As explained in the writings of the Arizal, the first day (of the week) among the seven days between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur rectifies all the first days of the past year, and the same applies to the second day, etc., until the day of Shabbos. Just as it is regarding the rectification of the past year, so too is it regarding the coming year. For the *mitzvah* of *teshuvah* from the Torah is that he should not return to folly, etc., and not transgress again, etc. —meaning a good resolution for the future, until (in the language of the Rambam) the Knower of Mysteries will testify concerning him that he will never return to this sin again. Just as it is known regarding the matter of *yirah* (fear/awe) that although there are many levels—fear of punishment, fear of G-d, fear of majesty, etc., up to the level of *yirah* as it is in the level of the *kotzo shel yud*—nevertheless, all the levels of *yirah* are connected and bound to one another, so too is it regarding *teshuvah*. Even *teshuvah* in the lowest manner is connected and bound to the highest level of *teshuvah*, up to the level of the *teshuvah* of “and the spirit returns to G-d who gave it,” which is explained in *Likutei Torah* on this *parsha*. This is why on Shabbos Shuvah we say “Return, O Yisrael,” etc., “and we will pay [with] our lips [the] bulls,” for through the *teshuvah* of Shabbos Shuvah, which acts upon all the Shabbosos of the year, we reach the level of “and we will pay [with] our lips [the] bulls,” which is an extremely exalted level, as mentioned above. And in the words of the Rambam, “*Yisrael* perform *teshuvah* and *immediately* they are redeemed,” and the Third Beis HaMikdash will be built, and there “we will perform before You the *korbanos* of our obligations according to the *mitzvah* of Your will,” speedily and truly.

## B’s”D. Thursday, 6 Tishrei, 5737

"Return, O Israel, to Hashem your Elokecha." It is well known that the precision of this phrasing—"to Hashem your Elokecha"—requires explanation, for seemingly it should have said "to Hashem, your G-d" (leHashem Elokecha). Furthermore, we must understand why both names, Hashem and Elokim, are mentioned here. Additionally, we must clarify why the verse continues, "for you have stumbled in your iniquity." It is self-evident that *teshuvah* (return) is for sin and iniquity; what, then, does the verse add by stating "for you have stumbled in your iniquity"?

2) The matter is as follows: In the *avodah* of man, there are two general categories: "Turn from evil" and "Do good." It is well known that the *avodah* of "turn from evil" pertains (primarily) to the name Elokim, while the *avodah* of "do good" pertains to the name Hashem. The reason for this is as stated in the *Shulchan Aruch*: when mentioning the name Hashem, one must intend that He is "He was, He is, and He will be," and when mentioning the name Elokim, one must intend that He is "mighty and powerful, possessing the ability in the upper and lower realms." The difference between these two is that "possessing the ability in the upper and lower realms" refers to Elokus as it relates to the worlds—which, in general, is *memale kol almin* (filling all worlds)—whereas "He was, He is, and He will be as one" refers to Elokus that is above the worlds, *sovev kol almin* (surrounding all worlds).

The definition of a "world" is space and time; this is true even in the spiritual worlds, which are defined by the parameters of space and time, albeit spiritual space and time. Since the light of *memale* is a light that relates to the worlds, it is understood that even in the light of *memale* there is a semblance of space and time (as is known, in the light of the *Kav*, which is generally the light of *memale*, there are the concepts of "above and below" [space] and "before and after" [time]), which is the root of the space and time that exist within the worlds. The fact that Hashem is "He was, He is, and He will be as one," above the division of time, is because Hashem is the light that is above the worlds. Nevertheless, from the fact that we say Hashem is "He was, He is, and He will be as one" [for "was, is, and will be" is the concept of time, only that He is above its division], it is understood that Hashem also relates to time (worlds). This is also the meaning of Hashem being a term of *mehaveh* (bringing into being), for the *hithavut* (coming into being) of all worlds is from the name Hashem. However, the worlds, as they are brought into being from the name Hashem, are not so limited, and therefore the past, present, and future are as one.

This is the meaning of Hashem and Elokim being *sovev kol almin* and *memale kol almin*: the *hithavut* from the light of *memale*, since it is in relation to the worlds and is their source, is by way of *hitlabshut* (enclothement). Therefore, the worlds that come into being from it are a *metzius* (an independent existence), for the concept of *hitlabshut* is that it enclothes itself to bring *the worlds* into being. The *hithavut* from the light of *sovev*, since the worlds have no relation to it, is by way of *mimila* (automatically), similar to an action resulting from a *ratzon* (will)—for *ratzon* is a metaphor for *sovev*—where the *ratzon* is merely the cause of the action and does not enclothe itself within it. Therefore, the worlds that come into being from the light of *sovev* are not in the category of *metzius*, for just as the *ratzon* itself is not a *metzius* and is only a revelation of the *Etzem* [similar to the *ratzon* in the human soul, which is only an inclination and extension of the soul], so too is it regarding the *hithavut* from the *ratzon*, whose purpose is (not that worlds are brought into being, but) that through this *hithavut*, the *ratzon* is fulfilled.

This is the connection of the light of *sovev* and the light of *memale* to the names Hashem and Elokim. The light of *memale* is called by the name Elokim, from the word *koach* (power), for it is like a power whose purpose is to act. However, since the existence of the worlds is through the fact that the Divine power brings them into being at every moment from *ayin* to *yesh*, the intention of the name Elokim is that He possesses the ability in the upper and lower realms, meaning His governance and dominion (Elokim from the word *serarah*—rule) over the upper and lower realms exists even after they have been brought into being, and His control over them pertains even to the very essence of their existence (whether to bring them into being or not). The light of *sovev* is called by the name Hashem, "He was, He is, and He will be as one," for since the *hithavut* from the light of *sovev* is that through the *hithavut* the *ratzon* is fulfilled, there is no difference between past, present, and future; "He was, He is, and He will be as one."

This is the connection between the two *avodos* of "turn from evil" and "do good" to the two names Elokim and Hashem. It is known that "turn from evil" also includes the caution not to transgress a positive commandment (for the nullification of a positive commandment is a prohibition, an evil). However, in "turn from evil," since one's caution in fulfilling the *mitzvos* is because one is afraid to transgress the King's command, one's fulfillment of the *mitzvos* is only to the extent that one is obligated. The innovation in the *avodah* of "do good" is that one's fulfillment of the *mitzvos* is (not only because one is compelled to do so, in order not to transgress the command, but) in the manner of *asiyah*—"and *do* good." As stated in *Likutei Torah*, the concept of "do good" is that one exerts oneself in Torah and *mitzvos*, even to the point of exertion beyond one's nature and habit. This is why the *avodah* of "turn from evil" pertains to the name Elokim, and the *avodah* of "do good" pertains to the name Hashem: because the one who is afraid to transgress the King's command, to the point that because of his fear he conquers his *yetzer* (even when his *yetzer* burns like fire) because he is afraid to transgress the King's command, does so because he feels that He, blessed be He, rules and governs over him—Elokim. And the *avodah* of "do good," where one exerts oneself in Torah and *mitzvos* beyond one's nature and habit, is through the revelation of the light of *sovev* that is above the worlds—Hashem, who is above nature.

3) Regarding the explanation of the connection of the *avodah* of "do good" to the light of *sovev* in greater detail: it was explained above that even the light of *sovev* has a connection to the worlds, but the purpose of the worlds in relation to the light of *sovev* is (not their existence, but) that through them the *ratzon* is fulfilled. Similarly, it is in the *avodah* of "do good": since the *avodah* of "do good" is because one loves Hashem and desires to cleave to Him, one's *avodah* of Hashem is bound up with the person's existence, his love, and his *ratzon*. However, his love for Hashem is in such a way that this (the love of Hashem) becomes his entire being, which is why his *avodah* of Hashem is in a manner that does not take into account the limitations of his nature.

This is the superiority of the *avodah* of "seek peace"—which is the study of Torah—over the *avodah* of "do good," for Torah is called the "ancient metaphor," which is a metaphor and garment for the "Ancient of the world," meaning the Essence of the *Ohr Ein Sof* which is not in the category of worlds at all. Therefore, the *bitul* of Torah study is absolute *bitul*. As explained in *Torah Or*, the difference between Torah and *mitzvos* is that in the fulfillment of *mitzvos*, the person who fulfills the *mitzvos* is like a servant fulfilling the command of a king; the existence of the servant is not the existence of the master. And even when his fulfillment of the *mitzvos* is with love and delight, there is still "one who loves." This is not the case in the study of Torah; it is the King Himself. For when the study of Torah is in *bitul*, then "I have placed My words in your mouth"—My words literally. Meaning (that it is not the person who is speaking, but) that the word of Hashem, which is *halacha*, is speaking from his throat.

4) Similarly, it is in the *avodah* of the love of Hashem, which has three levels: "With all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your might." It is explained in many places that the love of "with all your heart" is through contemplation of the Divine light that is enclothed in the created beings to give them life, which in general is the light of *memale*. By contemplating that the vitality of every thing is Elokus, and his contemplation is in such a way that it becomes understood even in the intellect of the *nefesh habehamis*, through this he will come to love Hashem "with all your heart," with both your inclinations. The love of "with all your soul" is through contemplation that the light that gives life to the worlds is merely an illumination, and that this illumination is incomparable to the *Ohr Ein Sof*, which in general is the light of *sovev*. Through this, he will come to the love of "with all your soul," where his love for Hashem is in a manner of *mesiras nefesh*, even if He takes your soul. The love of "with all your might" is through the revelation of the Essence of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, which is not in the category of worlds at all; through this revelation, the love is beyond measure and limitation.

And although the love of "with all your soul" is also "even if He takes your soul," and the concept of *mesiras nefesh* is beyond measure and limitation, the *mesiras nefesh* in the love of "with all your soul"—which is generally in the category of *ruach*—is in a manner that he *wants* to surrender his soul [and more than that, this *ratzon* is like the *ratzon* that is based on reason, from the fact that all things are incomparable to the *Ohr Ein Sof*]. But the love of "with all your might," which is generally in the category of *yechidah*, is not from his *ratzon*, but because he cannot be separated, G-d forbid, from His unity, blessed be He. *Mesiras nefesh* in this manner is the true concept of *bitul*, since the *mesiras nefesh* is not from his *ratzon* (existence) but from Elokus. This is similar to what was mentioned above (Section 3) regarding the superiority of the *avodah* of "seek peace" over the *avodah* of "do good."

It can be said that this is the reason why in the second paragraph of *Krias Shema* it says only "with all your heart and with all your soul," because the love of "with all your might," since it comes through the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* which is not in the category of worlds at all, is not for everyone. That which is demanded of everyone is the love that comes from the light of *memale* and the light of *sovev*. Similarly, regarding the three concepts of "turn from evil," "do good," and "seek peace," in many places only the two concepts of "turn from evil" and "do good" are mentioned.

5) It is known that the reason why forgiveness of sins is achieved through *teshuvah*—the true concept of forgiveness being that the person is acceptable before Him, blessed be He, as he was before the sin—is because through *teshuvah* one draws down a new *ratzon* from the *Ohr Ein Sof*, the Master of Will, which is above the *ratzon* of Torah and *mitzvos*. This drawing down is through the fact that his performance of *teshuvah* is in the manner of "with all your might," beyond measure and limitation. As is known, through the love of "with all your might," one "does the will of the Place," and the meaning of "doing the will of the Place" is that one *makes* the will. For through the love of "with all your might," one reaches the *Ohr Ein Sof* which is above *ratzon*, and through this, one draws down from there a new *ratzon*. Based on the principle that the *avodah* of man through which the drawing down is effected must be akin to the drawing down, it can be said that the two aspects of "doing the will of the Place"—that the drawing down is from the *Ohr Ein Sof* which is above the category of *ratzon*, and that the *ratzon* drawn down from there is higher than the *ratzon* as it is in itself—are accomplished through the two aspects of the love of "with all your might." Because the love of "with all your might" is above *ratzon* (as mentioned in Section 4, that the *mesiras nefesh* of "with all your might" is not that he *wants* to surrender his soul, but that he cannot be separated, G-d forbid, from His unity, blessed be He), therefore it reaches the Master of Will. And the fact that through the love of "with all your might" an elevation is also effected in the love of "with all your heart" and "with all your soul," through this, the *ratzon* drawn down from the Master of Will is also higher.

6) This is "Return, O Israel, to Hashem your Elokecha." The reason both names, Hashem and Elokim, are mentioned here is that the essence and *avodah* of every Jew is in these two names. And since the concept of *teshuvah* is that one returns and goes back to his place, therefore both names, Hashem and Elokim, are mentioned here. And the fact that it says "to Hashem your Elokecha" (and not "to Hashem, your G-d") is, as explained by the Alter Rebbe, that the measure of *teshuvah* is until "Hashem will be your Elokecha." That is, the *teshuvah* must be in the manner of "with all your might," beyond measure and limitation, through which the drawing down is from the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above Hashem. This concept (that the drawing down is from the *Ohr Ein Sof* above Hashem) is revealed in the fact that Hashem is your Elokecha—your power and your vitality. For the connection of *sovev* (Hashem) and *memale* (Elokim) is through the drawing down from the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above Hashem. This is why the verse continues, "for you have stumbled in your iniquity," which is the reason why the *teshuvah* must be in this manner: because you have stumbled in your iniquity, the *avodah* of "with all your heart" and "with all your soul," which is from the names Elokim and Hashem, is not sufficient; there must be the *avodah* of "with all your might," so that the drawing down will be from the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above Hashem.

7) There is another interpretation of "Return, O Israel, to Hashem your Elokecha": that the *teshuvah* must be until Hashem is only a category of Elokim and *tzimtzum* in relation to the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above Hashem. The difference between the two interpretations is that according to the interpretation of "to Hashem your Elokecha" meaning "Hashem will be your Elokecha, your power and your vitality," what is drawn down in revelation is the names Hashem and Elokim, but the connection of the two names is through the drawing down from the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above Hashem and Elokim. According to the interpretation that "Hashem will be only a category of Elokim and *tzimtzum* in relation to the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above Hashem," the light itself (that is above Hashem) is drawn down in revelation. For the fact that Hashem becomes a category of Elokim and *tzimtzum* in relation to the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above Hashem occurs specifically when this light shines in revelation.

It can be said that the difference between the two interpretations is similar to the difference between the blowing of the *shofar* and the recitation of the *shofaros* verses. As explained in *Hemshech 5666*, *Malchiyos* and *Zichronos* are in the light of *memale* and the light of *sovev*, and the drawing down through the *shofar* is from the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above *memale* and *sovev*, up to the drawing down from the "Essential Concealment," through which the unification of *sovev* and *memale* is effected. The drawing down through the recitation of the *shofaros* verses is higher even than the drawing down through the blowing of the *shofar*. The drawing down through the blowing of the *shofar* is only the light of *sovev* and the light of *memale*, but because the drawing down is from the *Ohr Ein Sof* [and higher than the Essential Concealment], they connect and unite. Through the recitation of the *shofaros* verses, the Essential Concealment itself is drawn down, similar to the revelation that will be in the future. This is why in the *shofaros* verses we recite the *shofaros* verses of the Giving of the Torah and conclude with the concept of the *shofar* of the future, for in the future there will be the revelation of the Essential Concealment. Similarly, there was the revelation of the Giving of the Torah.

8) The *shofar* of the future is higher than the *shofar* of the Giving of the Torah, as stated in *Pirkei D'Rabbi Eliezer* regarding the two horns of the ram of Isaac: the right horn is greater than the left, for with the left the Holy One, blessed be He, blew on Mount Sinai, and with the right He will blow in the future to come. And even in the *shofar* of the future itself there are several levels: "The L-rd Hashem will blow on the *shofar*," and "He will blow with a great *shofar*." "The L-rd Hashem" are names, but these names (the L-rd Hashem with the vowels of Elokim) are most exalted, as explained at length in *Likutei Torah*. This revelation will be at the beginning of the *Geulah*. Afterwards, "He will blow with a great *shofar*," it says "He will blow" without specifying who is blowing, for this blowing is a drawing down and revelation from the level of "the Concealed of all Concealed." It can be said that the reason we also recite the verse "He will blow with a great *shofar*" in the *shofaros* verses of *Rosh Hashanah* is because through the *avodah* of *Rosh Hashanah* we draw down this concept as well. Based on this, it can be said that when we say in the *Haftorah* of *Shabbos Teshuvah*, "Return, O Israel, to Hashem your Elokecha," in this "Hashem" is included the concept of "the L-rd Hashem" with the vowels of Elokim, which is a most exalted level, and yet we say that the *teshuvah* must be until Hashem is only a category of Elokim and *tzimtzum* in relation to the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above Hashem, because through the *avodah* of *Rosh Hashanah* we draw down the concept of "He will blow with a great *shofar*," and in relation to this level, even the name "the L-rd Hashem" is considered as Elokim.

9) It is known what is written in *Iggeret HaTeshuvah* that *teshuvah ila'ah* (higher repentance) is with great joy. From this it is understood *a fortiori* regarding the *teshuvah* of *Shabbos Teshuvah*—that in *teshuvah ila'ah* itself, which is a most exalted level, to the point that even "the L-rd Hashem" is considered as Elokim—it must be with great and immense joy. And since "joy breaks through barriers," through the joy of the Ten Days of Repentance, and especially on *Shabbos Teshuvah*, we break through all measurements and limitations, "He will answer me in the expanse of *Y-ah*," and this is drawn down to the entire year, in spiritual matters, so that the *avodah* in Torah and *mitzvos* will be with expansion and in a manner of "breaking through," and also in physical matters with abundant children, life, and sustenance, up to the true expansion that will be with the coming of *Mashiach Tzidkeinu*, "And it will be wide... Jerusalem is destined to be expanded," "Jerusalem is destined to spread throughout all of *Eretz Yisrael*, and *Eretz Yisrael* is destined to spread throughout all the lands," with the coming of *Mashiach Tzidkeinu* immediately.

## With the help of Heaven. Shabbos Parshas Haazinu, Shabbos Shuvah, 5737

"Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d..." and "Take with you words and return unto Hashem." One must understand: when it says "Return, O Israel," the intent is clearly to perform *teshuvah* as it should be performed. Since it is a *mitzvah* to confess (when one performs *teshuvah*)—to the point that confession is one of the foundations of *teshuvah*—"Return, O Israel" already includes the confession of words. What, then, is added by "Take with you words"? Furthermore, we must understand the continuation, "and return unto Hashem," which implies that after the *teshuvah* of "Return, O Israel," there must be yet another *teshuvah*.

2) This can be understood by prefacing what was explained previously: the verse "Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d" (even though, on the face of it, it should have said "unto Hashem your G-d") is interpreted by the Alter Rebbe to mean that *teshuvah* must reach the point where "Hashem will be your G-d." Another interpretation of "Return, O Israel, unto (*ad*)" is that *ad* refers to a level above that is called *Ad*. Regarding this, there are two levels: *ad v’ad bichlal* (up to and including) and *ad v’lo ad bichlal* (up to, but not including). *Ad v’ad bichlal* generally refers to the levels within *Seder Hishtalshelus*, where all levels are relative to one another; therefore, even when one is at a lower level, one already has a connection to the levels above it, reaching up to the highest level—*ad v’ad bichlal*. *Ad v’lo ad bichlal* refers to that which is above *Hishtalshelus*; even when one is at the highest level of *Hishtalshelus*, one does not thereby reach the level above *Hishtalshelus*—*ad v’lo ad bichlal*.

Based on this, we can explain the connection between *teshuvah* and the level of *Ad* ("Return, O Israel, unto *Ad*"). *Teshuvah* involves two matters. First, through *teshuvah*, one repairs and fulfills what one was supposed to do; this pertains (primarily) to the levels of *Hishtalshelus* (since a person is a configuration of *mochin* and *midos*), which is *ad v’ad bichlal*. Second, in order to be able to repair and fulfill these matters, one must reach the level above *Hishtalshelus* (the "not-man" within man), which is *ad v’lo ad bichlal*. It can be said that this is also what the verse continues with (after saying "Return, O Israel, unto *Ad*"): "Hashem your G-d," for *Havayah* and *Elokim* are *sovev* and *memale*. By "Returning, O Israel, unto *Ad*," where one’s *teshuvah* is in a manner that awakens the "not-man" within him (*ad v’lo ad bichlal*) and draws this down into a revelation within the configuration of the "man" within him, through this, "Hashem will be your G-d"—your strength and your life-force—so that the *sovev* light that is above *Hishtalshelus* will shine within him in a revealed, internal way.

3) It was explained above that the interpretation of "Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d"—that *teshuvah* must reach the point where "Hashem will be your G-d"—involves two matters: that Hashem will be your strength and life-force, and that Hashem will be merely a level of *Elokim* and *tzimtzum* relative to the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above *Havayah*. It can be said that the two matters in (*Return, O Israel*) *Ad*—*ad v’ad bichlal* and *ad v’lo ad bichlal*—correspond to these two interpretations (matters) of "Hashem your G-d." The point is that while the explanation above—that the two matters of *Ad* are *Hishtalshelus* and above *Hishtalshelus*—is general, in particular, even within that which is above *Hishtalshelus* there are these two matters of *Ad*. As stated in *Likutei Torah*, the two matters of *Ad* are in *Keter* [for *Keter*, in general, is above *Hishtalshelus*]. *Ad v’ad bichlal* is in *Arich*, the root of the *ne'etzalim*. *Ad v’lo ad bichlal* is in *Atik*, the lowest level of the *Ma'atzil*.

This can be understood based on the known principle that through the apprehension of the *memale* light that is enclothed in the worlds—which is merely an illumination (as is proven by the very fact that it is in a state of enclothement)—one comes to the knowledge that there is a light that is transcendent of the worlds, from which the illumination that is enclothed is drawn. Since through the apprehension of the illumination enclothed in the worlds one comes to the knowledge of the light transcendent of the worlds—even though the knowledge of this light is only a "knowledge of negation," knowing that this light is transcendent of the worlds and the illumination enclothed in them—nevertheless, since even a knowledge of negation is a form of knowledge and apprehension, therefore the light transcendent of the worlds is also included in *ad v’ad bichlal*. [And similarly in the *avodah* of man: since the intellect itself forces one to realize that "with intellect alone one cannot pass through," and for *avodah* to be as it should, it must be specifically through *kabbalas ol* that is above intellect, therefore this *kabbalas ol* (which comes from the fact that the intellect forces it) is also included in *ad v’ad bichlal*.] The matter of *ad v’lo ad bichlal* is in the light to which even a knowledge of negation does not apply [and in the *avodah* of man—in the *kabbalas ol* that comes from the *neshamah*].

Based on this, we can explain the connection between the two interpretations of "Return, O Israel, unto *Ad*" (whether it refers to *ad v’ad bichlal* or *ad v’lo ad bichlal*) and the two interpretations of "Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d" (that Hashem will be your strength and life-force, or that Hashem will be merely a level of *Elokim* and *tzimtzum* relative to the *Ohr Ein Sof* above *Havayah*). The interpretation that "Return, O Israel, unto *Ad*" is *ad v’ad bichlal*—the light to which a knowledge of negation applies, which in general is the *sovev* light—corresponds to the interpretation that "Hashem will be your G-d" means your strength and life-force, for the light revealed through *teshuvah* is the *sovev* light (*Havayah*). The interpretation that "Return, O Israel, unto *Ad*" is *ad v’lo ad bichlal*—the light to which even a knowledge of negation does not apply—corresponds to the interpretation that "Hashem will be your G-d" means that Hashem will be merely a level of *Elokim* and *tzimtzum* relative to the *Ohr Ein Sof* above *Havayah*, for the light revealed through *teshuvah* is the light that is above even the *sovev* light.

4) More deeply, it can be said that the verse adds "Hashem your G-d" after already saying "Return, O Israel, unto *Ad*," because the matter of "Hashem your G-d"—that *Havayah* is merely a level of *Elokim* and *tzimtzum* relative to the *Ohr Ein Sof* above *Havayah*—is higher even than *ad v’lo ad bichlal*. This can be understood based on what was explained above: that the *Havayah* in the verse "Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d" also includes *Havayah* with the vowels of *Elokim*, which is a most exalted level. As explained in *Likutei Torah* (on the verse "So shall *Adonai Havayah* cause righteousness to sprout..."), the greatness of the exaltation of the two names *Adonai* and *Havayah* (with the vowels of *Elokim*). And yet, it is written "unto Hashem your G-d," for even this *Havayah* is merely a level of *Elokim* and *tzimtzum* relative to the *Ohr Ein Sof* above *Havayah*, because even *Havayah* (with the vowels of *Elokim*) is only a name. As explained in *Likutei Torah* regarding the two levels of the *shofar* that will be in the future, and "*Adonai Havayah* shall blow the *shofar*..." and "it shall be blown with a great *shofar*," the *shofar* of "*Adonai Havayah* shall blow the *shofar*"—since even *Adonai Havayah* (with the vowels of *Elokim*) are only names—is a *shofar* in the general sense. Not so regarding the "great *shofar*," where it is written "it shall be blown with a great *shofar*," simply "it shall be blown," without stating who is blowing, because this blowing is an extension and revelation from the level of the "Hidden of all Hidden." It is explained in *Likutei Torah* there that this is the difference between *Rosh Hashanah* and *Yom Kippur*: the *shofar* of *Rosh Hashanah* is like "*Adonai Havayah* shall blow the *shofar*," while on *Yom Kippur* it is written, "*Before* Hashem you shall be purified,"—above *Havayah*. And more than that, "For on this day He shall atone for you,"—"He shall atone" simply, without stating who is the One who atones, similar to "it shall be blown with a great *shofar*," simply "it shall be blown." This is what we say in the *Haftorah* of *Shabbos Shuvah*: "Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d," where this *Havayah* refers also to *Havayah* with the vowels of *Elokim*, because on *Shabbos Shuvah* there is already a semblance of the exaltation of *Yom Kippur*, and relative to this exaltation, even *Havayah* with the vowels of *Elokim* is only a level of *Elokim* and *tzimtzum*.

Based on this, we can explain why, after saying "Return, O Israel, unto *Ad*"—which includes *ad v’lo ad bichlal*—it adds "Hashem your G-d." For even the matter of *ad v’lo ad bichlal*, although a knowledge of negation does not apply to it (as mentioned in section 3), nevertheless, from the fact that one must negate and say *ad v’lo ad bichlal*, it is understood that it has some connection. This is similar to the matter of the "Master of Will" from whom forgiveness is drawn (as explained above), which in general is the matter of the names *Adonai* and *Havayah* with the vowels of *Elokim*, through which all flaws are filled. Even though the forgiveness drawn from Him for having acted against the Will is because He is not "forced" by the Will, nevertheless, the Will has a connection to Him (as is proven by the fact that He is called the "Master of *Will*"), and therefore there must be forgiveness for having acted against the Will. This is not the case regarding the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* which is above even the Master of Will, where a flaw is not applicable to begin with. As explained in other places regarding the concept that "the essence of the day atones," the atonement that comes from the essence of the day is (not in the manner of an action, but) that on this day the connection of the essence of the *neshamah* with the *Atzmus* (which is above even the Master of Will) is revealed, and relative to this level, a flaw is not applicable to begin with. This is why, after saying "Return, O Israel, unto *Ad*," it adds "Hashem your G-d"—because since *Shabbos Shuvah* is a preparation for *Yom Kippur*, the *teshuvah* of *Shabbos Shuvah* must be not only *Ad*—*ad v’lo ad bichlal*—through which all flaws are *nullified*, but one must also awaken and reveal the point of Jewishness, the connection of the essence of the *neshamah* with the *Atzmus* (which is above even the Master of Will), relative to which even *Havayah* with the vowels of *Elokim* is only a level of *Elokim* and *tzimtzum*. Through this, it is revealed that the undesirable matters are only in the externality, but in the internality (from the perspective of the connection between Israel and the Holy One, blessed be He), there were no undesirable matters to begin with.

5) It can be said that even on *Yom Kippur* there are two matters: the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* that is connected with the *Atzmus*, and the fact that this matter is revealed in the individual levels of the *neshamah*, down to the level of *nefesh* within it, through which the atonement (in the aforementioned manner) is revealed also in the levels of the *neshamah* where a flaw is applicable. This is why *Yom Kippur* is a day obligated in five prayers; the five prayers correspond to the five names of the *neshamah*: *nefesh, ruach, neshamah, chayah,* and *yechidah*. The matter of a day obligated in five prayers is that the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah*, which comes from the essence of the day, is revealed in its individual levels. It can be said that the difference between the five prayers is not only in the place (the level) where the essence of the *neshamah* is revealed, but also in the revelation itself. In the *Ne'ilah* prayer, the level of *yechidah*, the revelation is greater than in the four prayers preceding it. And so it is regarding the four prayers themselves, where the difference is also in the manner of the revelation. And so it is regarding the levels above; the fact that *Yom Kippur* is a day obligated in five prayers is because on *Yom Kippur* there is the ascent of *Malchus* into *Atik* (as explained in the *Siddur*), a matter that is present throughout the entire day, yet there are many levels within it, and the main ascent is in *Ne'ilah* (as explained in the *Siddur*).

The levels of *Yom Kippur* are divided into two general levels: *Shabbos Shabbaton* is (feminine) and *Shabbos Shabbaton* is (masculine). Both levels are in *Atik*, but there are two interpretations of *Atik*. In general, the highest level in *Keter* (and specifically the three heads within it) is called *Atik*, but the essential matter of *Atik* is the *Ohr Ein Sof* within the three heads. The difference between the two aforementioned levels is that *Shabbos Shabbaton* (feminine) is in *Atik*, in the three heads within it (which also include *Radla*), while *Shabbos Shabbaton* (masculine) is in the *Ohr Ein Sof* within *Radla*. Based on this, we can add an explanation as to why, after saying "Return, O Israel, unto *Ad*," it adds "Hashem your G-d," for *ad v’lo ad bichlal* is in *Atik* (as mentioned above in section 3 from *Likutei Torah*), and the fact that *Havayah* is considered as *Elokim* relative to the *Ohr Ein Sof* above *Havayah* can be said to be relative to the *Ohr Ein Sof* in *Radla*.

6) It can be said that the fact that we say the greatness of *Yom Kippur* is that it is a day obligated in five prayers—even though the day itself (the revelation of the connection of the essence of the *neshamah* with the *Atzmus*) is higher than the fact that it is obligated in five prayers (the revelation in the individual levels of the *neshamah*)—is because the fact that the essence of the *neshamah* is revealed in its individual levels, through which the atonement is in the place where the flaw was initially, has an advantage over the level of the essence of the *neshamah* itself. For even though in the levels of *Neshama-Ruach-Nefesh*, and especially the level of *nefesh* (as they are from their own perspective), flaws are applicable, nevertheless they are atoned for. [And more than that, the atonement that comes from the essence of the day is in a manner that reveals that in the internality, there was no flaw in them to begin with, as mentioned above.] And so it is also regarding the matter of the atonement itself, for the main innovation in the atonement is the atonement for sins in their literal sense. The atonement for the sins of the *tzaddikim*—the matter of "there is one who loves" and the like, which relative to their level is like a shedding of the yoke—since regarding these sins it is not applicable to say they are against the Will of the Holy One, blessed be He [for this *yeshus* is because of the nature that the Holy One, blessed be He, implanted], therefore the atonement for this is not such an innovation. The main innovation and (consequently) the greatness is in the atonement for sins in their literal sense, for therein is revealed the strength of the unlimited connection between Israel and the Holy One, blessed be He, that even when they have done against His Will, nevertheless (through *teshuvah* and atonement) they are as pleasing before Him, may He be blessed, as if they had never sinned. This is what our Sages said: "In the place where *baalei teshuvah* stand, perfect *tzaddikim* cannot stand." It can be said that this statement of our Sages does not depart from its literal meaning, and the main greatness of *baalei teshuvah* is in *baalei teshuvah* in the literal sense. Based on this, one can understand what the *Tzemach Tzedek* brings (from the book *Taamei HaMitzvos* by Rabbi Chaim Vital): that the reason the Arizal used to recite the specific confessions is because all of Israel is one body. On the face of it, one could say that the confessions of the Arizal were for matters of the utmost subtlety that were applicable to him as well [similar to what was explained above regarding the *yeshus* of "there is one who loves"]. The fact that it is explained that the confessions of the Arizal were (also) for sins in their literal sense because all of Israel is one body, it can be said, is because according to this explanation, it is precisely understood that the Arizal also possessed the greatness of *baalei teshuvah* in the literal sense.

7) It should be added that the main innovation and greatness in *baalei teshuvah* is in the *avodah* of Torah and *mitzvos* after the *teshuvah*, for the *avodah* of *teshuvah* is to "leap over the wall" in a manner that is not according to the order and path of *Hishtalshelus*. Therefore, the revelation of the essential connection of the essence of the *neshamah* with the *Atzmus* through *teshuvah* and atonement—even though the revelation is in those levels where the flaw was, which in general are the levels of *Neshama-Ruach-Nefesh*—is as if the *Neshama-Ruach-Nefesh* are in a manner of ascent to above (to the levels of *chayah* and *yechidah*, and up to the essence of the *neshamah*). The main innovation and greatness of *teshuvah* is that through *teshuvah*, the *avodah* of Torah and *mitzvos* is also with "greater strength." The *avodah* in Torah and *mitzvos* is in an orderly sequence [for (most) *mitzvos* have a limited measure, as explained in *Tanya*, and all the more so the study of Torah, which is in understanding and apprehension], and through the fact that the *avodah* of Torah and *mitzvos* after *teshuvah* is with greater strength, the revelation of *chayah* and *yechidah* (and it can be said, also of the essence of the *neshamah*) in the *Neshama-Ruach-Nefesh* is as if the *Neshama-Ruach-Nefesh* are below. And more than that, through the *avodah* of Torah and *mitzvos*, the world also becomes a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed, and through this the intent of a "dwelling place in the lower worlds" is completed. As is known regarding the matter of "With whom did He consult? With the souls of the *tzaddikim*," that this was said regarding the souls of the *tzaddikim* (even though "in the place where *baalei teshuvah* stand, perfect *tzaddikim* cannot stand"), because the ultimate intent of creation is that there be a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed, in the lower worlds, and this is accomplished through the *avodah* of the *tzaddikim*, the *avodah* of Torah and *mitzvos*. However, the perfection in the *avodah* of Torah and *mitzvos* is when it comes after the precedence of *teshuvah*, for then the *avodah* of Torah and *mitzvos* is with greater strength, and through this, there is an addition to the revelation of *Elokus* (within man, and through this) also in the world, and through this, the intent of a "dwelling place in the lower worlds" is more fully completed.

8) This is "Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d... Take with you words and return unto Hashem." The fact that it adds "Take with you words" after saying "Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d" is because, from the perspective of the great exaltation in the *avodah* of *teshuvah*—and especially in the *teshuvah* of "Return... unto Hashem your G-d," where the *teshuvah* is at the level of *ad v’lo ad bichlal*, and more than that, "Hashem your G-d," through which the essence of the *neshamah* that is connected with the *Atzmus* (which is above even the Master of Will) is revealed—even though one is at a most exalted level, and on the face of it, it is not applicable to repair flaws [and especially since to repair this one must descend from his level], nevertheless it says "Take with you words," for there must be a confession of words, in order to wipe away the body of the *kelipos*.

Another interpretation of "Take with you words" is that "words" are Torah and *mitzvos*. Even regarding the matter of repairing sins, although it has the advantage that through this the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* is drawn (also) into the lower levels where a flaw is applicable, nevertheless, even this is not enough, and one must "Take with you words," Torah and *mitzvos*, through which there will be an exaltation also in the levels of the *neshamah* as they are below (and not in a manner of ascent), and more than that, through this, there is also a clarification and refinement of the world, and it becomes a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed, in the lower worlds.

This is also what the verse continues with: "and return unto Hashem," for after the *teshuvah* of "Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d," which is an ascent from below to above, there must be "and return unto Hashem," to draw the light from above to below through Torah and *mitzvos*, which are the "way of Hashem." Through this, all the influences are drawn down, not only in spiritual matters but also in physical matters—in children, life, and ample sustenance. As the Alter Rebbe said: "The Almighty gives Jews physicality, and Jews make spirituality out of that physicality," and they make a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed, in the lower worlds, and through this—"And the kingdom shall be Hashem's."

## Shuva Yisrael Ad Havayah Elokecha, Shabbos Shuvah 5740

"Return, O Israel, unto Havayah your G-d." It is well known that our Rebbes have questioned why the verse says "unto (*ad*) Havayah your G-d," when seemingly it should have said "to (*le*) Havayah your G-d." Furthermore, we must understand why both names, Havayah and Elokecha, are used. This is especially pertinent since the verse says "Return, O Israel," and the name "Israel" describes the higher level within the Jewish people. The Jewish people are generally divided into two levels: Yaakov and Yisrael. The name Yisrael is derived from "for you have striven with Elokim and with men and have prevailed," which implies that the verse is speaking of the perfection of teshuvah. This is also understood from the fact that the Haftorah for Shabbos Shuvah is "Return, O Israel," for the teshuvah of Shabbos Shuvah is a higher level of *teshuvah ila'ah* (higher teshuvah). For "Shabbos" (Shabbos) is an anagram of "tashuv" (you shall return), which is the level of *teshuvah ila'ah*. Within *teshuvah ila'ah* itself (which contains several levels), there is a superior teshuvah, for one must be in a state of *teshuvah ila'ah* all his days (even on weekdays). From this, it is understood that Shabbos is a higher level within *teshuvah ila'ah* itself. This is especially true on Shabbos Shuvah, which rectifies and elevates all the Shabbosos of the year—as explained in the writings of the Arizal regarding the seven days between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, which contain all seven days of the week. On the first day of the week within these days, one rectifies and elevates the Sundays of the entire year, and similarly for the second day, and so on. On Shabbos Shuvah, one rectifies and elevates the Shabbosos of the entire year, because the teshuvah of Shabbos Shuvah is superior even to the teshuvah of all other Shabbosos of the year. This teshuvah is akin to Yom Kippur, regarding which it is written, "For on this day He shall provide atonement for you... before Havayah you shall be purified." "Before Havayah" specifically means above the name Havayah. Moreover, it says "He shall provide atonement for you" in an absolute sense—He who has no known name and is not grasped by any name. Why, then, does it say here "unto Havayah your G-d"?

The explanation for this is known: the phrase "Return, O Israel, unto Havayah your G-d" refers to the unification of Havayah and Elokim, as it is written, "And you shall know... that Havayah is the G-d (Ha-Elokim)," which is the highest level of knowledge, as explained in the Zohar. Therefore, it says "Return, O Israel, unto Havayah your G-d," because the concept of teshuvah relates to this knowledge, as it is written (regarding the teshuvah that took place on Mount Carmel), "And they said, 'Havayah is the G-d, Havayah is the G-d.'" The repetition of "Havayah is the G-d" twice is because through the avodah of teshuvah—which is higher than the avodah of tzaddikim—the concept of "Havayah is the G-d" is drawn down twice. As it is stated in the Midrash, at Sinai it was said, "I am Havayah your G-d," while at the Carmel it was said twice, "Havayah is the G-d, Havayah is the G-d." It is explained in Likutei Torah that at Sinai, the Jewish people were at the level of tzaddikim, and through the avodah of tzaddikim (Torah and mitzvos), the concept of "Havayah is the G-d" was drawn down once. This is not the case at Mount Carmel, where through teshuvah they drew down "Havayah is the G-d" twice. This is similar to the fact that at the Giving of the Torah it is written "I am (Anochi) Havayah your G-d"—*Anochi* once—whereas regarding teshuvah it is written, "I, I (Anochi Anochi) am He... for My own sake," *Anochi* twice. This is because through the avodah of Torah and mitzvos, the level of *Anochi* is drawn down once, whereas the drawing down through the avodah of teshuvah is higher than the drawing down through the avodah of Torah and mitzvos. This is also the meaning of "I, I am He... for My own sake," which the Targum translates as "for the sake of My name," reaching the level of His Great Name, and even higher, to the level of "He is and His name alone," before the Tzimtzum.

The matter is as follows: There are two interpretations and aspects to "Havayah is the G-d." The first is that even the Tzimtzum of Elokim is Havayah. Havayah is a term denoting "bringing into being" (*mehaveh*), for all existence comes from the name Havayah. However, in order for existence to be in actuality as a separate entity, this occurs through the name Elokim, which conceals and hides the name Havayah, as it is written, "For Havayah Elokim is a sun and a shield." This is the meaning of "Havayah is the G-d"—that even the name Elokim, which restricts and conceals, is Havayah. Havayah and Elokim are entirely one, and therefore, in truth (as it is before the Ribono shel Olam), it does not conceal at all. Through the avodah of Torah and mitzvos, one draws down the revelation of the name Havayah into the world. This is the meaning of "which Elokim created to do"—"to do" means to rectify—for one must rectify the concealment of the name Elokim and draw down the revelation of the name Havayah into the world, as it is written (subsequently), "On the day that Havayah Elokim made earth and heaven," where the name Havayah is also mentioned. For through the avodah of man, "to work it and to guard it" (Torah and mitzvos), the revelation of the name Havayah is drawn into the world, and it becomes "these are the generations of the heavens and the earth," and even "these are the generations of Peretz," which is higher still than "these are the generations of the heavens and the earth," because through the avodah of Torah and mitzvos, one breaks through all the veils and concealments of the name Elokim, making a dwelling place for Him in the lower realms. The second aspect of "Havayah is the G-d" is that even the name Havayah (as it is before it is clothed in the name Elokim) is only a level of Elokim and a Tzimtzum relative to His Essence and Being, as it were. As it is written, "I am Havayah, that is My name," meaning that the level of Havayah is merely a name, a radiance and illumination, which is a great and immense Tzimtzum relative to the Ohr Ein Sof itself, as it were.

These two aspects are drawn down through the two types of avodah: that of tzaddikim and that of *baalei teshuvah*. When a person is in the state of "Elokim made man upright," through his avodah in Torah and mitzvos, he draws down the revelation of the name Havayah into the name Elokim (the first aspect of "Havayah is the G-d"). But when he has sinned and caused a blemish, he cannot draw down from the level of Havayah (since he has blemished the name Havayah), and he must draw down from a level that is above the name Havayah. This drawing down is achieved through the avodah of teshuvah. For all higher matters are drawn down specifically through avodah. Even the drawing down of the Ohr Ein Sof that is above the *seder hishtalshelus*—where *is'arusa d'letata* (arousal from below) does not reach—does not dwell and reveal itself (in a state of inwardness) unless there is perfection in the deeds of the lower beings, for the Holy One, blessed be He, only dwells in a complete place. This drawing down (of the Ohr Ein Sof that is above the *seder hishtalshelus*) is achieved through the avodah that is above the *seder hishtalshelus* (within man), which is the avodah of teshuvah. When one does teshuvah—especially such that his willful sins are transformed into merits—which is teshuvah out of love from the depths of the heart, with great love, as explained in Tanya—he thereby awakens the level that is above the name Havayah, relative to which even Havayah is considered as Elokim (the second aspect of "Havayah is the G-d"). [This is the superiority of simple teshuvah over the teshuvah of Shabbos, which is an anagram of "tashuv," because the teshuvah (and elevation) of Shabbos is only the matter of "and Elokim finished," where the measure of Tzimtzum and concealment of the name Elokim is finished and the revelation of the name Havayah is drawn down. But in order to awaken the level above the name Havayah, this is done through simple teshuvah, and especially through teshuvah where willful sins are transformed into merits, for through the clarification and transformation of the sins, the advantage of the light is created. This is a fortiori from what is stated in the Zohar that the Giving of the Torah was achieved specifically through the admission of Yisro, "Now I know that Havayah is greater than all the Elokim," even though at the Giving of the Torah it was said "I am" (only) once. How much more so is this true for the "I, I" (twice) of teshuvah, the drawing down of which is achieved specifically through the clarification and transformation of darkness.] After the awakening from above Havayah (through teshuvah), one must draw it down from above to below into the level of Havayah, the term denoting "bringing into being," and even into the level of Havayah as it is clothed in the name Elokim—the first aspect of "Havayah is the G-d." This is why in teshuvah it says "Havayah is the G-d" twice: first, the ascent from below to above until, relative to it, Havayah is considered as Elokim (the second interpretation), and afterwards, the drawing down from above to below in Havayah, the term denoting "bringing into being," as it is clothed in the name Elokim (the first interpretation), from which actual existence is created, "In the beginning Elokim created the heavens and the earth," and not only the drawing down into the *eser sefiros* (as the Zohar interprets "In the beginning" as referring to the *eser sefiros*), but also into the heavens and the earth and all their hosts in their literal sense.

These are also the two aspects of the blowing of the shofar (which alludes to the concept of teshuvah): that the sound of the shofar is an inner voice (the inwardness of the heart), and together with this, one must blow into a physical shofar specifically. For on Rosh Hashanah, all things return to their source, and through the shofar, one awakens a new drawing down from the Essence. The shofar is the level of Binah, which is above the *seder hishtalshelus*, the level of Will that is above even the level of Binah, and higher still, a new drawing down from the inwardness and Essence of the Blessed One. From there, it is drawn down into the inwardness of the Will, the inwardness of Binah, etc., until it reaches the inwardness of the worlds, and from there it is subsequently drawn down into the externality of the worlds as well, as explained in the Siddur in the discourse "To Understand the Concept of the Blowing of the Shofar" based on the intent of the Baal Shem Tov. Therefore, the shofar has the two aforementioned aspects: through the inner voice in the shofar, the inwardness of the heart, one awakens the Essence; and through the blowing into a physical shofar—specifically the horn of an animal, and furthermore, the horn must be detached (not attached to the animal), a level of "inanimate"—one draws it down below into *Asiyah*, even to the level of the inanimate. As we say in the Rosh Hashanah prayer, "And all that is made will know that You made it," meaning that even in *Asiyah* (as stated in the writings of the Arizal that "all that is made" refers to the world of *Asiyah*), and even the inanimate within *Asiyah*, will know that You made it.

This is "Return, O Israel, unto Havayah your G-d." "Unto" (*ad*) is a term of eternity, for the concept of eternity relates specifically to the category of time (for that which is above the category of time is above even "He was, He is, and He will be" as one), yet within the category of time, it is in a state of eternity. Therefore, *ad* is the level of Malchus, which is in the state and category of time. Yisrael is the level of Ze'er Anpin. The meaning of "Return, O Israel, unto Havayah your G-d" is that one must return Yisrael until Malchus is in the state of *ad*, and the levels of Yisrael and *ad* (Ze'er Anpin and Malchus, the levels of *hishtalshelus*) are unto Havayah your G-d—that is, unto the Essence of the Ohr Ein Sof, relative to which Havayah is considered as Elokim. From there, one must draw down into the level of Havayah your G-d—Havayah, the term denoting "bringing into being"—and within that, as it is clothed in the name Elokim, becoming "your G-d," your strength and your life-force. This is also why we say "Havayah is the G-d" seven times at the Ne'ilah of Yom Kippur. For within the Ten Days of Teshuvah itself [when teshuvah is most beautiful and is accepted immediately], the essence of teshuvah is on Yom Kippur, as the Rambam writes: "Yom Kippur is the time of teshuvah for all... and it is the end of pardon and forgiveness for the Jewish people." On Yom Kippur itself, the essence of teshuvah is in the Ne'ilah prayer (the end of the day), for then the gates are locked and the Jewish people and the King are alone together. Therefore, we say "Havayah is the G-d" seven times, for it contains the two aforementioned aspects: the aspect of ascent (from below to above) into the Essence of the Ohr Ein Sof, and as is known, the concept of "Havayah is the G-d" (which we say at Ne'ilah) is that Ze'er Anpin (Havayah) upon ascending to Atik is considered as Elokim; and the aspect of drawing down from above to below (in Havayah, the term denoting "bringing into being," and even as it is clothed in the name Elokim). We say this seven times in order to draw it down into the seven lower *sefiros*. It can be said that this is also why we say "Shema Yisrael Havayah Elokeinu Havayah Echad" once, "Baruch Shem Kvod Malchuso" three times, and "Havayah is the G-d" seven times. "Shema Yisrael..." once is the ascent from below to above, and "Baruch Shem..." three times and "Havayah is the G-d" seven times is the drawing down from above to below into all the *eser sefiros* (three *mochin* and seven *midos*).

One can connect this to the verse, "And on the day of your gladness... you shall blow with the trumpets." The teaching of the Maggid is known: two trumpets (*chatzotzros*) are two half-forms (*chatzi tzuros*). It is explained in the *maamar* that the concept of two half-forms is in the realm of revelations (a form indicates revelation), where it is appropriate to say that the Holy One, blessed be He, and the Congregation of Israel are two half-forms that complete each other. This is not the case regarding the Essence, where Israel and the Holy One, blessed be He, are literally one (and it is not appropriate to say they are two halves). It is the same regarding the two half-forms in the soul: the part of the soul that is above, the "head" of the soul, and the part of the soul that is in the body (*nefesh, ruach, neshamah*), the "foot" of the soul. This is specifically in the realm of the revelations within the soul, for even the "head" of the soul is only up to the level of *chayah*, which has a connection to the level of the "foot" of the soul (the *neshamah* in the body), which is why it is called *mazal*, because it drips and drops the illumination of the *neshamah* clothed in the body. This is not the case for the level of *yechidah*, and especially the essence of the soul, which, while including all the powers and even more so carrying all the powers, is above the level of source and connection to the level of revelations. It is (specifically) present in all the levels of the soul, even in the "heel" of the soul, and also in the "heel" in the body, and even in the four cubits (outside the human body), which is why a person's four cubits acquire for him, as explained in many places. This is the superiority of the shofar over the trumpets, as our Sages say, "The shofar is long and the trumpets are short," and the mitzvah of the day is with the shofar, because the avodah of the shofar is in the essence of the soul, which is literally one with the Essence, above the concept of two half-forms.

This is also "the shofar is long," for the concept of length is both above and below, because the avodah of the shofar reaches the Essence (the essence of the soul and the Essence of the Blessed One) which is above all revelations (length above), and it is present, as mentioned, even in the "heel" (length below). Thereby, the intent of a dwelling place for Him in the lower realms is completed in both aspects: for Him, for His Essence, and in the lower realms. However, one also needs the level of revelations, just as in the dwelling of a king of flesh and blood, there must be (in addition to the main thing, a dwelling for the essence of the king) a beautiful dwelling and beautiful vessels. Therefore, there must also be trumpets, and because of the affection in this, both sounds (trumpets and shofar) are heard, but the mitzvah of the day is with the shofar, because the essence of the avodah on Rosh Hashanah is the avodah that comes from the essence of the soul, which is one with the Essence.

This is why during the Ten Days of Teshuvah (the ten days between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur) we read the Haftorah "Return, O Israel, unto Havayah your G-d," for these days between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur are akin to Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. Therefore, just as Rosh Hashanah is the level of "head" from which life is drawn for all the days of the year, and even subsequently the conduct throughout all the days of the year is according to the manner of conduct and the decision of Rosh Hashanah (as explained at length in *Ateres Rosh B'Rosho*), and similarly it is for Yom Kippur, which is also called Rosh Hashanah—and not only that, but Yom Kippur is the level of *neshamah* and inwardness—similarly, the seven days between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur are the level of "head" from which it is drawn to all the days of the year. Similar to what is found in the writings of the Arizal (as mentioned in section 1), it can be said that from the first day of the week within these seven days, it is drawn to every Sunday throughout the year, and from the second day of these seven days to every Monday, etc., and from Shabbos Shuvah to all the Shabbosos of the year. Therefore, similar to the two aspects of ascent and drawing down in the shofar of Rosh Hashanah and in saying "Havayah is the G-d" seven times at the Ne'ilah of Yom Kippur, so too it is with the teshuvah during the days of teshuvah between them [and especially on Shabbos Shuvah, the head of all the Shabbosos of the year, Shabbos being an anagram of *tashuv*], which contains these two aspects. Therefore, we say "Return, O Israel, unto Havayah your G-d," which includes both aspects: the ascent of the level of Yisrael (Ze'er Anpin) and the level of *ad* (Malchus) unto Havayah your G-d—that is, unto the Essence of the Ohr Ein Sof, relative to which Havayah is considered as Elokim—and afterwards the drawing down from the Essence of the Ohr Ein Sof unto the level of Havayah your G-d—that is, unto the level of Havayah as it becomes "your G-d," your strength and your life-force. All this is achieved through "Return" in a general sense, the teshuvah that comes from the essence of the soul, the core of *yechidah* [and from this is drawn teshuvah in the level of Yisrael, the head of the soul, and unto the foot of the soul], and thereby the redemption from exile is achieved, the individual redemption, and afterwards also the general redemption with the coming of Mashiach Tzidkeinu, who comes to return the tzaddikim in teshuvah. This too relates to the level of *yechidah*, as it is known that Mashiach is the level of *yechidah*, that it will be "He has redeemed my soul in peace... redeem me and my children from among the nations," in the true and complete redemption through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, very soon and in our days.

## Parshas Haazinu, Shabbos Shuvah, 5741

"Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d, for you have stumbled in your iniquity." The Alter Rebbe explains that the phrasing "Return... unto Hashem your G-d" (even though, seemingly, it should have said "to Hashem your G-d") implies that the measure of *teshuvah* is until Hashem becomes *your* G-d. 

One must understand: someone who is capable of (and from whom is demanded) such a lofty *teshuvah* is already a person of high standing even before performing this *teshuvah*. This is understood from the term "Israel," which is named for "for you have striven with Elokus and with men and have prevailed"—meaning he is a prince and ruler over the concealment of the name *Elokus* and the forces that spread from it ("men"). How, then, is the matter of "iniquity" applicable to him, such that he requires *teshuvah*? 

Even though it is known that *teshuvah* is not only for sins, but to return one's soul to its source and root ("and the spirit shall return unto the G-d who gave it"), and that this *teshuvah* applies even to *tzaddikim gemurim*, the verse explicitly states, "Return, O Israel... for you have stumbled in your iniquity." How can the concept of iniquities apply to one who is at this level? 

Furthermore, one must understand what the verse continues: "Take with you words and return unto Hashem." After it has already said "Return, O Israel," what is added by "Take with you words and return unto Hashem"? Also, one must understand what it says later: "Say unto Him, 'Take away all iniquity,'" for even the speech and the request to "take away iniquity" is connected to the matter of *teshuvah*. This implies that after the *teshuvah* of "Return, O Israel" and the *teshuvah* of "Return unto Hashem," there must be yet another *teshuvah*. Additionally, we must explain why the first and third *teshuvah* mention iniquity ("for you have stumbled in your iniquity," "take away iniquity"), whereas the second *teshuvah* does not.

### 2

The point of the explanation is based on what is explained in *Likutei Torah*: there are three categories of *teshuvah*. *Teshuvah* of *sur meira* (turning from evil), *teshuvah* of *va'aseh tov* (doing good), and *teshuvah* of *bakash shalom* (seeking peace), which refers to Torah—for anyone who engages in Torah brings peace to the heavenly entourage and the earthly entourage, and the entire Torah was given to make peace in the world. 

The three *teshuvahs* in these verses ("Return, O Israel," "Return unto Hashem," "Say unto Him, 'Take away all iniquity'") are the three *teshuvahs* of *sur meira*, *va'aseh tov*, and *bakash shalom*. This is also why these verses are the beginning of the *Haftorah* for Shabbos Shuvah, because on Shabbos Shuvah, all three categories must be present. As the Rebbe Rayatz related, the Tzemach Tzedek had three candles of *teshuvah* on Shabbos Shuvah and said, "There are three *teshuvahs*," and the Rebbe Rayatz explained that these are the three *teshuvahs* explained in *Likutei Torah*. 

This is why the first *teshuvah* says, "Return... for you have stumbled in your iniquity," whereas in the second *teshuvah* ("Return unto Hashem"), the matter of iniquity is not mentioned, because the first is the *teshuvah* of *sur meira* (stumbling in iniquity), while the second is the *teshuvah* of *va'aseh tov*. This is also why the first *teshuvah* mentions the name *Elokus* ("unto Hashem your G-d"), the second mentions only the name *Havayah* ("Return unto Hashem"), and the third says "Say unto Him," because the *teshuvah* of *sur meira* is that *Havayah* should be *our* G-d; the *teshuvah* of *va'aseh tov* is to connect and cleave to the name *Havayah* as it is above the name *Elokus*; and the *teshuvah* of *bakash shalom* is to cleave and be included in the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above the name *Havayah*.

### 3

The difference between the *teshuvah* of *sur meira* and the *teshuvah* of *va'aseh tov* and *bakash shalom* is that the *teshuvah* of *sur meira* is that there should be no iniquities that separate between you and your G-d, while the *teshuvah* of *va'aseh tov* and *bakash shalom* is that the *avodah* should be to draw down a loftier light. The drawing down that comes from Above by itself—because He is the essence of good and it is the nature of the good to do good—is measured and limited, whereas through *avodah*, the drawing down is without limit. 

As is known regarding "these are the generations of Peretz," "generations" is written fully, which is higher even than "these are the generations of the heavens and the earth when they were created" (where "created" is written defectively), for the world was created in its fullness. The "full" generations mentioned regarding the creation of heaven and earth (creation from Above) the Creator intended to be measured and limited, whereas the "full" generations of Peretz—which will be (in the coming of Moshiach) through our deeds and *avodah*—is that Moshiach (from the children of Peretz, and called the *Poretz*) will break all boundaries. 

Within this itself (the drawing down through *avodah*), the drawing down through engagement in Torah (*bakash shalom*) is loftier than the drawing down through the performance of *mitzvos* (*va'aseh tov*). For in addition to the fact that through *mitzvos* the drawing down is in the category of *makif* (encompassing) and through Torah it is *penimiyus* (internal), the drawing down itself is loftier. Through the performance of *mitzvos*, the *Ohr HaSovev* (encompassing light) is drawn down, and through engagement in Torah, the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* which is above even the *Sovev* is drawn down.

### 4

Based on the above, that "Return, O Israel... for you have stumbled in your iniquity" is the *teshuvah* of *sur meira*, it is understood that for one who is at the level of "Israel," the concept of "you have stumbled in your iniquity" does not mean (only) that he has some deficiency (which is true even of *tzaddikim*), but that iniquities that separate are also applicable to him (albeit in the most subtle of subtleties), and therefore he requires the *teshuvah* of *sur meira*. 

This matter [that even in *tzaddikim* (Israel) there is the *teshuvah* of *sur meira*] is also understood from the fact that *teshuvah* is a *mitzvah* [as the Alter Rebbe writes in *Igeres HaTeshuvah*, "the *mitzvah* of *teshuvah*"]. From this, it is understood that the *mitzvah* of *teshuvah* is (like all *mitzvos*) for every Jew, including *tzaddikim gemurim*. And since the *mitzvah* of *teshuvah* is (as the Alter Rebbe writes there) the abandonment of sin (*sur meira*), it is understood that in addition to the fact that even in *tzaddikim gemurim* there must be the *teshuvah* of "and the spirit shall return unto the G-d who gave it" [as explained in *Likutei Torah* (in our *parsha*), that the root of the soul is the category of *Elokus* (who gave it), which is the category of *Tzur* that is above *Havayah*—a concealment that is not in existence, and the drawing down of the soul from this category is in the manner of "who gave it," for anyone who gives, gives with a generous eye; and the *teshuvah* of "and the spirit shall return unto the G-d who gave it" is the returning and elevating of the soul to its root and source, so that even while the soul is clothed in the body (for the *mitzvah* of *teshuvah* is, like all *mitzvos*, for souls in bodies specifically), it should be in a state of complete *bitul* as it was in its root, in the category of *Elokus* who gave it], there must also be for them a *teshuvah* in the simple sense. 

One must understand: in *Atzilus* (even in the individual *Atzilus*), it is written, "No evil shall dwell with You." Since the *teshuvah* of *tzaddikim* is (as mentioned from *Likutei Torah*) unto the G-d who gave it—above the category of "He created it, He formed it, He breathed it into me" (*Beriah, Yetzirah, Asiyah*) and even above the category of "that You gave into me" (*Atzilus*)—how is the *teshuvah* of *sur meira* possible for them?

### 5

Seemingly, one could explain this based on what is written in *Likutei Torah*: that for one who, due to the loftiness of his soul, is capable of the *avodah* of the category of *ruach* (*va'aseh tov*), and certainly for one capable of the *avodah* of the category of *neshamah* (*bakash shalom*), when he is lacking in this *avodah*, it is a blemish. Furthermore, because he is at a lofty level, his blemish (even though it is subtle) is greater than the actual iniquity of a simple person.
