As the Maggid said, the *machshavos zaros* (alien thoughts) of *tzaddikim gemurim* blemish more than the severe iniquities of an *am ha'aretz*. Like a speck on a scholar's garment. 

Based on this, one could seemingly say that the *mitzvah* of *teshuvah* (which is the abandonment of sin) in *tzaddikim gemurim* is the *teshuvah* for the fact that there was a deficiency in their *avodah* of *va'aseh tov* or at least in the *avodah* of *bakash shalom*, and that this *teshuvah* is also the abandonment of sin, since the blemish caused by this is even greater than the iniquity (in the simple sense) of a simple person. However, from the very fact that we say there are three matters (*sur meira*, *va'aseh tov*, and *bakash shalom*), it is proven that the *avodah* of *va'aseh tov* and *bakash shalom* is an addition and an ascent in holiness itself, and even when there is a lack in this *avodah*, it is not a matter of evil (not even subtly). Based on this, one must understand what the *mitzvah* of *teshuvah* (abandonment of sin) is in *tzaddikim*, regarding which it says, "Return, O Israel... for you have stumbled in your iniquity," that even one who is at the level of "Israel" must also have the *teshuvah* of *sur meira*.

### 6

This will be understood based on the teaching of our Sages, "One who gestures with his hand before the King..." For before the King, there must be absolute *bitul*, and when one gestures before the King, he presents himself as an existence before the King. From this, it is understood that even when one serves Hashem, if his *avodah* is in the category of "existence," it is like "gesturing before the King," G-d forbid. 

Based on this, it is understood that even in *tzaddikim gemurim* there must be (also) the *teshuvah* of *sur meira*, because since even when he is a *tzaddik gamur* serving Hashem with *yirah* and *ahavah* in *taanug*, he is not completely *batel* in existence, but is a thing unto himself—a "fearer of Hashem" and an "lover of Hashem" ("there is one who loves and there is one who fears") as explained in *Tanya*—it turns out that his acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven is not in absolute perfection, and relative to his level, it is like a casting off of the yoke. Therefore, even in *tzaddikim gemurim* there must be the *teshuvah* of *sur meira*. 

This is "Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d, for you have stumbled in your iniquity"—that the *teshuvah* for the stumbling in iniquity that is at the level of "Israel" (one who loves) is specifically through the revelation of *Havayah*. For relative to the name *Elokus*, since the creation from the name *Elokus* is in the manner of "clothing" ("In the beginning, *Elokus* created"), the creatures that are created from it occupy space, and their *bitul* is only the *bitul* of "existence." Whereas relative to the name *Havayah*, where the creation from it is in the manner of "automatically" ("Let them praise the name of *Havayah*, for He commanded and they were created"), the creatures do not occupy space—*bitul* in existence. This is that the *teshuvah* for "you have stumbled in your iniquity" at the level of "Israel" (one who loves) is that *Havayah* should be *your* G-d; by having the revelation of *Havayah* shine within him, until this (*Havayah*) becomes *your* G-d—your strength and your vitality—he arrives at *bitul* in existence.

### 7

It is explained in *Likutei Torah* that the meaning of "Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d" is that the *teshuvah* must be until *Havayah* is only a category of *Elokus* and *tzimtzum* relative to the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above *Havayah*. This is similar to the interpretation of "*Havayah* is the G-d" that we say at the *Ne'ilah* of Yom Kippur, that *Ze'er Anpin* (*Havayah*), upon ascending to *Atika*, is considered as *Elokus*. 

This is understood based on the known fact that even relative to the name *Havayah*, since it is a source for creation—for *Havayah* is related to the word *mehoveh* (bringing into being) (even though the creation is "automatically")—it is necessary that there be some occupation of space for the creatures. True *bitul* is specifically relative to the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above *Havayah*, which is not in the category of a source for worlds. This is that the *teshuvah* for "you have stumbled in your iniquity" (one who loves) is "unto Hashem your G-d" (as interpreted in *Likutei Torah*), that *Havayah* is *Elokus* and *tzimtzum* relative to the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, for through the revelation of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* [the *Atika*, which is separated and removed and distinct from the existence of the worlds], one reaches the ultimate *bitul*. 

It should be added that the matter of "existence" (of "one who loves") in the simple sense was already rectified through the *avodah* of *teshuvah* of the month of Elul, "And she wept for her father and her mother for a month of days," which refers to the month of Elul (as it is in the writings of the Arizal), the month of mercy, when the King is in the field. Especially in the days after the 18th of Elul, which infuses vitality into all the matters of the month of Elul, and especially in the days of *Selichos* when we say, "Yours, Hashem, is the righteousness, and ours is the shame of face"—the *bitul* of the *yirah* of shame, which is the *yirah* of transcendence at the highest level. Especially on Erev Rosh Hashanah, whose purpose is (as its name, Erev Rosh Hashanah, implies) preparation for Rosh Hashanah, and especially on Rosh Hashanah itself, when the *avodah* is the acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven, "that you shall make Me King over you." The *teshuvah* of Shabbos Shuvah is for "existence" as it is in the most subtle of states. Therefore, *Likutei Torah* explains that the meaning of "Return, O Israel, unto Hashem your G-d" [which we say on Shabbos Shuvah] is that *Havayah* should be only *Elokus* and *tzimtzum* relative to the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, because in order to negate "existence" in the most subtle state, it is specifically through the revelation of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above *Havayah*.

### 8

After saying "Return, O Israel... for you have stumbled in your iniquity," which is the *teshuvah* of *sur meira* [as it is at the level of "Israel"], he adds, "Take with you words and return unto Hashem, say unto Him, 'Take away all iniquity,'" for these two *teshuvahs* are the *teshuvah* of *va'aseh tov* and the *teshuvah* of *bakash shalom*. 

This is understood by prefacing what is explained in *Likutei Torah* that the three *teshuvahs* of *sur meira*, *va'aseh tov*, and *bakash shalom* are in the three categories of *nefesh, ruach, neshamah*. The *teshuvah* of *sur meira* (which is for sin and iniquity) is in the category of *nefesh*, for the matter of sin and iniquity is only applicable to the category of *nefesh*, as it is written, "The soul (*nefesh*) that sins." Also, the *teshuvah* (of *sur meira*) is in the category of *nefesh*, because the *teshuvah* of *sur meira* is that one accepts upon himself the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven and the yoke of the *mitzvos* [not to rebel against His Kingdom, may He be blessed, and not to transgress the King's command, G-d forbid], and the matter of accepting the yoke is in the category of *nefesh*. 

The *teshuvah* of *va'aseh tov*, which is for the lack of arousal of love and fear [for the drawing down through the performance of *mitzvos* (*va'aseh tov*) is through love and fear, which are the wings through which it flies Above, and the perfection of the performance of *mitzvos* itself is specifically through love], is in the category of *ruach*, for the emotions of the heart (love and fear) are the category of *ruach*. The *teshuvah* of *bakash shalom*, which is for the lack of engagement in Torah with understanding and comprehension—and more specifically, the *teshuvah* for the lack of comprehension of *Elokus*, and primarily for the fact that the Divine matter is not felt in his mind (for even when he contemplates a Divine matter, the matter is not felt in his mind)—is in the category of *neshamah*, "the soul of the Almighty gives them understanding." 

This is why in the *teshuvah* of *va'aseh tov* it is written, "Take with you words," and in the *teshuvah* of *bakash shalom* it is written, "Say unto Him," for speech ("words") belongs to the emotions (for speech is the breath that comes from the heart), and therefore it is the category of *ruach*, "the speaking spirit." And "saying," which is in the heart—thought—is the category of *neshamah*. This is also what is meant by "Say unto Him 'all,'" *kol* (all) from the word *hishtalelus* (inclusion), for (seeking) peace is the matter of inclusion.

### 9

What is said in this *teshuvah* (of the category of *neshamah*), "Take away iniquity," is understood based on what is said in the *Zohar* (which we say in *Ma'aneh Lashon*): "Their *nefesh* is holy, their *ruach* is holy, their *neshamah* is holy of holies." The fact that the *neshamah* is called "holy of holies" is because it includes within it also the holiness of *nefesh* and *ruach*. Since *nefesh* and *ruach* as they are included in the *neshamah* are at a loftier level [similar to the *domem, tzomeiach, chai* (inanimate, vegetative, animate) in man, which are higher than the *domem, tzomeiach, chai* (of the world), and similarly regarding the *Beriah, Yetzirah, Asiyah* that are in *Atzilus*, and similarly in the *Sefiros* of *Atzilus* as they are hidden in what is above *Atzilus*], therefore, through the *teshuvah* of *neshamah*, an elevation is also effected in *nefesh* and *ruach*, not only as they are included in the *neshamah*, but also as they are in their own place. Therefore, it says in the *teshuvah* of the category of *neshamah*, "Take away iniquity," for through this *teshuvah*, an elevation is also effected in the *teshuvah* of *sur meira*. 

From this, it is understood that in order for the *avodah* of *sur meira* (category of *nefesh*) and *va'aseh tov* (category of *ruach*) to be as they should, it is through engagement in Torah (category of *neshamah*), and especially through engagement in the *penimiyus* (inner dimension) of Torah, in the secrets of the Torah and the secrets of secrets. For the category of the *penimiyus* of Torah, called the "hidden of the Torah," is what connects and joins the "hidden" of Israel to the "hidden" of the Holy One, blessed be He. Even though the drawing down of the *Atzmus* that is in the Torah is in all parts of the Torah—even in the revealed part of the Torah, and even in reading the letters of the Torah (even when he does not know what he is saying), as explained in *Hemshech Ta'arav*—it is explained there that the revelation of the *Atzmus* is specifically in the *penimiyus* of Torah [for the *penimiyus* of *Abba* is the *penimiyus* of *Atik*, and therefore the revelation of the *penimiyus* of *Atik* is specifically in the *penimiyus* of *Abba*]. Especially in the *penimiyus* of Torah as it was revealed in the teachings of Chassidus by the Baal Shem Tov, and all the more so as it was revealed by the Alter Rebbe in the teachings of Chassidus Chabad, in a manner that "they will be sustained." Through engagement in Torah, and especially in the *penimiyus* of Torah, an elevation is also effected in the *avodah* of *sur meira* and *va'aseh tov*.

### 10

Just as the *teshuvah* of "Return... for you have stumbled in your iniquity" is not for iniquities in the simple sense, G-d forbid—for all Israel are presumed to be righteous, and especially after the *teshuvah* of the month of Elul and the days of *Selichos*, and especially after Rosh Hashanah when all Israel are judged favorably, and even on Erev Rosh Hashanah they already know clearly that they will be judged favorably, for "Your people are all righteous," and especially after *Teki'as Shofar* when the matter of wealth is effected [as is known that what our Sages said, "it becomes enriched at its end," is immediately after *Teki'as Shofar*]—and the *teshuvah* of Shabbos Shuvah (after Rosh Hashanah) is only for the "existence" of "one who loves" (and within this itself—in the most subtle of states), as mentioned above (section 7)—similarly, it is also regarding the *teshuvah* of *va'aseh tov* and *bakash shalom*. Even though they performed these matters before, the *teshuvah* of Shabbos Shuvah is that the performance of *mitzvos* should be with even greater *hiddur* (beautification), and similarly in engagement in Torah, and especially in the *penimiyus* of Torah as it was revealed in the teachings of Chassidus, and especially in the teachings of Chassidus Chabad, including also the matter of "your wellsprings will spread outward," until "the earth will be full of the knowledge of Hashem as the waters cover the sea." May it be a year of Torah, a year of *teshuvah*, and a year of *tefillah*, and likewise all the blessings in all the letters: a year of light, a year of blessing, a year of redemption, etc., and especially the primary blessing: the complete redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, immediately.

## Parshas Haazinu, Shabbos Shuvah 5748

### – Second Farbrengen – Continuation of the Farbrengen of the Second Day of Rosh Hashanah –

"A Song of Ascents; from the depths I have called You, Hashem". This psalm is ordained to be recited every day during the Ten Days of Repentance, beginning from Rosh Hashanah (which is the first day of the Ten Days of Repentance), and many discourses on Rosh Hashanah begin with this verse. It is brought in the discourses regarding this that there are two opinions in the Zohar—that of Rabbi Yitzchak and that of Rabbi Abba—regarding the interpretation of "from the depths." Rabbi Yitzchak explains it as the depths of man, meaning that this prayer comes from the depths of the soul of man—from the depths of the soul of King David, and likewise from the depths of the soul of every individual Jew who recites this psalm. Rabbi Abba, however, explains it as the depths of Above, a place that is hidden on high, and so on. The discourses continue by stating that the interpretation of "from the depths" encompasses two depths, corresponding to these two aforementioned opinions. Based on this, it is explained that according to Rabbi Yitzchak, the "depth within depth" is the *omeka d’liba* (depth of the heart), while according to Rabbi Abba, it is the *amika d’amikta* (the deepest of the deep) within the levels of Above. The discourse continues that the primary interpretation refers to the levels of Above—meaning that through "I have called You," one reaches the level of "depth within depth," until one ascends to a level of depth that is beyond the division of levels, and from there, "I have called You"—meaning, one draws down—Hashem. The connection between these two interpretations (for "one said one thing and the other said another, yet they do not disagree") is that the ability to draw down from this level of depth, which is above any definition of division, is through the *omeka d’liba* that comes through the *avodah* of *teshuvah*, as explained in the discourses, and as is understood simply: this is the content of this psalm ("A Song of Ascents; from the depths"), the prayer of *teshuvah*.

To understand this—how through the *avodah* of *teshuvah* one draws down from the "depth within depth" of Above—we must first preface what is explained in the *Hemshech* of Rosh Hashanah 5698 (fifty years ago), that there are two levels in *teshuvah*: the "matter of *teshuvah*" and the "essence of *teshuvah*." The "matter of *teshuvah*" is *teshuvah* regarding specific matters, or even general matters. Even if this *teshuvah* is not regarding a sin, iniquity, or transgression, its purpose is that one ascends in his *avodah* in goodness itself, going and adding to the perfection of his *avodah* until this specific *avodah* is as it should be and as is required, and until, by perfecting all the specific matters, the totality of his *avodah* is as required. However, the "essence of *teshuvah*" is that from the place where he stands in his spiritual standing and situation, he ascends (he shifts himself over) to a higher place, which is a new standing and situation, to the point that it is not at all comparable to his previous state. This means he ascends to a supreme state that is entirely incomparable, to the point that one cannot even use the terms "higher" and "lower" regarding it; for the term "higher" implies that there is a "lower," and this is the "higher" in relation to it. The essence of *teshuvah*, however, is at a level that is not within the definition or value of "higher" and "lower," as it is not at all related to the previous state (in a positive sense), to the extent that a new reality is created, brought into being from absolute nothingness. And because he stands in a state and situation that is entirely incomparable, this acts upon all the details of his *avodah* and all the minute particulars, so that everything is rectified as it should be. That is, since he is in a new state and situation such that all the actions he performs are good—as per the expression of our Sages and the precision of the order therein: "*teshuvah* and good deeds"—through *teshuvah* there will be the ultimate perfection in the deeds, the ultimate perfection in goodness, and the ultimate perfection in the Jewish people who perform the good deeds.

This is the meaning of "A Song of Ascents; from the depths I have called You, Hashem": that through *teshuvah* from the *omeka d’liba*—the depth within depth, which is the essence of *teshuvah*—one calls and draws down from Above from the level of *amika d’amikta*, even to a depth that is not within the category of levels at all. Through this, all the prayers in this psalm are fulfilled in their simple sense: that the level of the supreme depth of Above is drawn down—the level of depth that is above "depths" (plural), to the point that it is above any definition of depth—and it descends to the lowest of the low, and in a revealed manner, truly, even in time, immediately, and there will be a nullification of all limitations and a revelation of the true *Ein Sof* below. And more than that: for regarding Him, blessed be He, the definition of "above" and "below" does not apply, but rather everything is one. This is established and brought about by the fact that the depth of the Jewish people and the depth of Above become one thing: "From the depths I have called You", so that the Jewish people and the Holy One, blessed be He, will be entirely one.

## As the eagle stirs up its nest, etc., He bears him on His pinions. It is known that the interpretation of the Baal HaHillula is that the eagle represents the attribute of *rachamim*, and "as the eagle stirs up, etc." refers to the attribute of *rachamim rabim*. Regarding the statement "He bears him on His pinions"—even though He has no body nor any likeness of a body—this is because "this Torah is man," and it refers to the 248 limbs of the King, which are formed through the 248 *mitzvos*, for through the performance of *mitzvos*, Hashem says, "I consider it as if you have made Me." This is the concept of "He bears him on His pinions," meaning that due to the *rachamim rabim* of the level of the eagle, through *teshuvah*, an elevation is achieved that is above the level of the 248 limbs of the King (which are formed through *mitzvos*), as indicated by the word *al* (upon) in "He bears him *upon* His pinions." This aligns with what is explained in Kabbalah, that the living creatures of the *Merkavah* are illuminated by the thirteen attributes of the beard, and higher still, the eagle is the level of *Atik*, which is detached and separate from the entire *Seder Hishtalshelus*. Therefore, from the perspective of this level, there are no measurements or limitations—not even the measurements of the highest levels within the *Seder Hishtalshelus*, and even extending to the measurements of Torah and *mitzvos*. Thus, from this level, "He bears him on His pinions." It can be said that this is also the reason why we read *Parshas Haazinu* during the *Aseres Yemei Teshuvah* or immediately after Yom Kippur, for that is the time of *teshuvah*, when even one who has sinned—whether "sin" in the literal sense or "sin" in the sense of deficiency, as it is written, "I and my son Solomon are sinners," meaning he was deficient in the perfection of his *avodah*—is elevated through *teshuvah* to a level above His pinions, due to the revelation of the level of the eagle.

One must understand why specifically the "eagle" is mentioned, for there are four living creatures of the *Merkavah*—the face of the lion, the face of the ox, the face of the eagle, etc., within the thirteen attributes of the beard—so why is it said specifically "as the eagle"? Even though the level of the eagle here refers to the level of *Atik* as mentioned above—and the level of *Atik* is detached and separate from the entire *Seder Hishtalshelus*, and certainly detached and separate from the world of *Beriah*, where the four living creatures of the *Merkavah* reside—nevertheless, from the fact that it says "as the eagle," comparing the level of *Atik* and the level of *rachamim rabim* above to an eagle, it is understood that it is being compared to the level of the eagle within *Hishtalshelus*, the eagle of the *Merkavah*, and even down to the physical eagle below. It is known that the physical eagle below is *rachmani* (merciful), the opposite of the raven, and it is to this merciful eagle below that the level of *rachamim rabim* above is compared. The matter is that among the four living creatures of the *Merkavah*, the face of the lion is to the right, the face of the ox is to the left, and the face of the eagle is in the center. From this, it is understood that the face of the eagle, in the context of the Patriarchs being the *Merkavah*, is the attribute of Yaakov, the middle line. It is known that Avraham is the right line, Yitzchak is the left line, and Yaakov is the middle line. This is also the sequence of the verses: "For Hashem’s portion is His people, Yaakov the lot of His inheritance... as the eagle stirs up its nest, etc.," for the matter of "as the eagle stirs up its nest" follows the mention of Yaakov. Therefore, it says specifically "as the eagle," which is the attribute of Yaakov, for Yaakov is the perfection of the Patriarchs.

The explanation of the virtue of the middle line is that it is known that the middle line is the "middle bar" that extends from one end to the other. There are two aspects to this. The first aspect is that it extends from the highest end to the lowest end. Just as the middle bar in the *Mishkan* extended from one end of the *Mishkan* to the other—which is why it was 48 cubits long—so too is it with the middle line, which extends from the highest end to the lowest end. These two aspects (that it extends from the highest end and reaches down to the lowest end) are interdependent, for because it takes from the highest of the high, it extends to the lowest of the low, as it is known that the "depth of the heights" and the "depth of the depths" are connected. This is also true in *avodah*, as is known regarding the concept of "From the depths I have called You, Hashem," where the *avodah* that comes from the "depth within the depth" (which is the meaning of *ma'amakim* in the plural, as stated in the *Zohar*) below, reaches the "depth" above, even to the perfection of the pardon, forgiveness, and atonement of Yom Kippur, which is "before Hashem," and even to the level of "and the spirit returns to the G-d who gave it," as discussed in *Likutei Torah* on this *parsha*. The second aspect of the middle line is that from its beginning to its end, it is uniform, without changes. The reason for the absence of changes in it is because it extends from the highest end, and for this reason, it extends to the lowest end as mentioned above, and in every place, it is uniform. The right line and the left line, being only illuminations, follow the order of the illumination of light, where the further it extends downward, the more it diminishes (and changes). Not so the middle line, which takes from the highest of the high as mentioned above. It is known that although all the lines reach the *Keter*, it is only in the external aspect of the *Keter*, whereas the middle line reaches the internal aspect of the *Keter*; therefore, it is the same at its end as it is at its beginning, without any change. Similarly, this is also true in the structure of the human body, which is made in the image of the Above, as it is written, "Let us make man in our image, after our likeness," and it is written "Let us make man," using the plural as explained in previous *maamarim*. In it, too, there are three lines: the right hand and right leg, which are analogous to the right line above; the left hand and left leg, which are analogous to the left line above; and the head, torso, and *Yesod*, which are analogous to the middle line above. In the right and left lines, there are changes—the hands are not like the feet, and especially not like the fingernails, which are only secondary to the person. Not so with the middle line; it is known regarding the concept that "there is no hardening except through *Daas*," that the moment it is aroused in the *Daas* of the mind, at that same moment the *Yesod* is aroused, etc., as explained in the *Hosafos* to *Torah Or*. Similarly, this is true in the three lines above: the two lines, right and left, are "one long and one short," they diminish and change, whereas the middle line is "one intermediate," which is uniform from its beginning to its end. This is the virtue of Yaakov, the middle line, the perfection of the Patriarchs, for his bed was perfect. From Avraham came Yishmael, and from Yitzchak came Esav; and although it was only by way of "departing," nevertheless, they did depart from him. This is because Avraham and Yitzchak are in the right and left lines, and in these two lines, the further they extend downward, the more they diminish as mentioned above; therefore, due to the diminution upon diminution that occurs at the end of the extension, it was possible for them to depart. Not so Yaakov; because he is in the middle line, in which there are no changes, his bed was perfect, for even as it extends downward into the aspect of his "bed," it is in the ultimate state of perfection.

With this, one can understand what is written in the writings of the *Arizal* on the verse, "There are three things that are too wonderful for me, and four that I do not know: the way of an eagle in the sky," for Shlomo wondered how the eagle, which is a non-kosher bird, is found in the sky in the aspect of the *Merkavah* above. It is known that the question here is why Shlomo wondered about three—only the eagle—when the lion is also a non-kosher animal, yet the face of the lion is one of the four living creatures in the *Merkavah*. The matter is that *nesher* (eagle) comes from the word *neshirah* (falling/dropping). This is the novelty of the eagle over the lion: that even that which has fallen and dropped is also in the sky. Therefore, he wondered specifically about the eagle and not the lion, for the fact that the lion is in the sky is only the root of the lion, not as it has fallen below into the aspect of a non-kosher animal. And although the very fact that it fell so low indicates the virtue of its root—as is known from what is written in *Ohr HaChayim* that because of the intensity of the light of holiness in the face of the lion in the *Merkavah*, it fell so low into the aspect of the non-kosher—nevertheless, after the fall, it is not in the sky, for through the screens, partitions, and separations that divide, it fell and became separated from its root. Not so "the way of an eagle in the sky," which means that even that which has fallen and dropped is also in the sky. This is the virtue of the middle line over the right line and the left line: the right draws near and the left pushes away. The fact that the right draws near is because it clarifies, refines, and elevates the permitted things, whereas the forbidden things, which are bound by the hands of the *chitzonim*, are pushed away by the left. Not so from the perspective of the middle line, the level of *rachamim* of the eagle; even that which has fallen and dropped is also in the sky. This is the virtue of *teshuvah*, for *teshuvah* transforms even the willful sins into holiness. At the beginning of the *avodah* of *teshuvah*, the willful sins become only unintentional sins, but the ultimate goal of *teshuvah* is that the willful sins are transformed into merits. This is the virtue of *teshuvah*, the level of "seeking peace," over the *avodah* of "turn from evil and do good."

This is "as the eagle stirs up its nest, etc.," which refers to what precedes it: "For Hashem’s portion is His people, Yaakov the lot of His inheritance. He found him in a desert land, etc., He guarded him as the apple of His eye." Yaakov is the level of the middle line as mentioned above, which is why he is uniform from his beginning to his end. This is "Yaakov the lot of His inheritance," which is like a rope whose one end is above and whose other end is below; when one shakes the lower end, the upper end also shakes, as explained in *Iggeres HaTeshuvah*. Because Yaakov is the middle line, he is the "lot of His inheritance," etc. "He found him in a desert land"—*midbar* (desert) is a place where "no man dwells," meaning the level of the "supernal man" that is drawn down through Torah and *mitzvos* does not dwell there. Nevertheless, through *teshuvah* (the middle line), "He found him in a desert land and in the howling waste of the wilderness," for even there He finds him, as explained in *Likutei Torah* regarding the concept of "And you shall seek from there." "He guarded him as the apple of His eye"—this is the black part of the eye from which the light emerges, as Rashi explains, and it is the concept of "transforming darkness into light." When one transforms the darkness into light, there is then the "advantage of light," as is known regarding the concept of the "advantage of light from the darkness." And to this, it continues afterward, "as the eagle stirs up its nest, etc., He bears him on His pinions," for from the perspective of the level of the eagle, which is the level of *Atik* (to which the middle line reaches), "He bears him on His pinions"—above Torah and *mitzvos*, pardon and atonement—until he is drawn from the level that is above Torah and *mitzvos*, "on His pinions," and it concludes, "Hashem alone will lead him, and there is no strange god with Him," that there are no strangers with you, and they shall be yours alone—Israel and the King alone.

## 13 Tishrei (Hilula of the Rebbe Maharash), Erev Sukkos, 5736

"You shall dwell in Sukkos for seven days" (and it adds a further commandment), "Every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos" (and the reason for this is), "In order that your generations may know that I made the Children of Israel dwell in Sukkos when I brought them out of the land of Egypt," etc. The precise nuances of this are well-known in the maamarim of our Rebbes. By way of introduction, the Baal HaHilula stated that the Sukkah is the concept of peace, as it is written, "Spread over us the Sukkah of Your peace." We must understand the connection between the Sukkah and peace.

There are several matters within the seven days of Sukkos that relate to "You shall dwell in Sukkos for seven days." For example, the mitzvah of taking the Four Species ("And you shall take for yourselves on the first day," etc.), which has a connection to the concept of the Sukkah. As is known, the preferred way to perform the mitzvah of the Four Species is within the Sukkah. Furthermore, the seven days of Sukkos include the offering of the seventy bulls, which correspond to the seventy nations, as stated in the Midrash. This matter exists even now, as it is written, "We will render the bulls with our lips." This is especially true according to the explanation that "we will render" (*venishlema*) is not only a matter of compensation, but also relates to the word *shleimus* (completeness). This means that even in the time of exile, through the recitation of the verses concerning the seventy bulls, the matter is complete. Although in the time of exile, since the action is only through "our lips," it is not visibly apparent in the physical realm—and certainly not in the materiality of the world—nevertheless, since the movement of the lips is considered an act, as explained at length in Tanya, the action is complete in the spiritual realm (and at the very least, as a minor act). We must understand the connection of these matters (taking the Four Species and offering the seventy bulls) to the concept of the Sukkah.

Now, when the first day of the festival falls on Shabbos (as is the case this year), there is a difference between the time of the Beis HaMikdash and the time of exile (similar to the blowing of the Shofar on Rosh Hashanah when it falls on Shabbos, where in the Temple they would blow, but not in the provinces). In the time of the Beis HaMikdash, on the first day of the festival, the Lulav would override Shabbos even in the provinces, whereas since the destruction of the Beis HaMikdash, the Lulav does not override Shabbos even on the first day, as explained in the Mishnah and Gemara.

2. The core explanation for all these matters is that the Sukkah is a concept of *makif* (encompassing light). It is not like the *makif* of a garment, which, despite being an encompassing level, still contains divisions—the garments of the head, the garments of the body, and the garments of the feet (each according to the measure of what it clothes). The *makif* of the Sukkah, however, has no such division, for it encompasses his head and his entire body (which means his head and his whole self, for "the majority is like the whole")—head, body, and feet as one.

Based on this, we must understand the phrase "You shall dwell in Sukkos" (as noted in the discourses of our Rebbes, and in *Hemshech Tarsav*). A *makif* (especially as described above) is not a state of "dwelling" (*yishuv*). If so, what is the concept of "You shall dwell in Sukkos"? This matter (dwelling in a Sukkah) is mentioned three times: "You shall dwell in Sukkos," "Every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos," and "I made the Children of Israel dwell in Sukkos." We must also understand the phrase, "In order that your generations may know that I made the Children of Israel dwell in Sukkos," etc. (the reason for "You shall dwell in Sukkos" is so that future generations will know that "I made them dwell in Sukkos"). Since the mitzvah of Sukkah is not one of the mitzvos dependent on the Land, nor one dependent on the time of the Beis HaMikdash, its observance will continue throughout all generations, for this Torah will not be changed, Heaven forbid. Thus, the knowledge of future generations ("that I made them dwell in Sukkos") will come about because they themselves will dwell in Sukkos. What, then, is the meaning of "You shall dwell in Sukkos" in relation to "in order that your generations may know"—that the knowledge of future generations will be through the "dwelling in Sukkos" of the previous generations?

3. The explanation for this is (as mentioned above) that the Sukkah is a concept of *makif*. It is written, "At the covering (*bakeseh*) for the day of our festival"—all matters that are in a state of *keseh* (covering and concealment) during the days preceding our festival are revealed in Sukkos. For example, the Shofar of Rosh Hashanah, the mitzvah of which is the Shofar, comes into revelation on the day of our festival within the *sechach* (covering) of the Sukkah. This is why *sechach* (for all Sukkos, the verse implies that a wall is not called a Sukkah) has the numerical value of 100, corresponding to the one hundred sounds of the Shofar that Israel has the custom to blow on Rosh Hashanah (and the custom of Israel is Torah). The details of the sounds are also hinted at in the letters of *sechach*: *Samech* (60) sounds of *Tekiah*, *Chaf* (20) sounds of *Shevarim*, and *Chaf* (20) of *Teruah*.

Similarly, this applies to the incense of Yom Kippur, which comes into revelation on the day of our festival, as it is known that the *sechach* of the Sukkah is made from the cloud of the incense of Yom Kippur. Although on Yom Kippur there is already a revelation—the essence of *bakeseh* (the month is covered) is at the beginning of the month, on Rosh Hashanah—nevertheless, the revelation of Yom Kippur (its essence) is on a higher plane. In Sukkos, however, the revelation is drawn downward, until the *sechach* of the Sukkah (which comes from the cloud of the incense of Yom Kippur) is visible even to physical eyes. (It can be said that the beginning of this revelation is in the days between Yom Kippur and Sukkos, which is the preparation for the mitzvah of Sukkah and the time for building the Sukkah. That is, these days are the preparation and the beginning of the drawing down of the revelation of Yom Kippur—whose essence is above—into the lower realm as well.)

This is why the Sukkah is a level of *makif*. The difference between incense and offerings is that regarding offerings it is written, "My bread for My fire," for bread is an internal matter (and there are offerings that are also eaten by the Kohanim, and offerings eaten by the owners and everyone), whereas incense (especially that of Yom Kippur) is a *makif*. Therefore, its revelation on the day of our festival is also in the manner of a *makif*—the *makif* of the *sechach*. And since it belongs to the Holy of Holies, which is the true essence of *makif*, the *makif* of the Sukkah, which is drawn from the cloud of the incense of Yom Kippur, is also above the *makif* of a garment and has no division, encompassing the garments of the head, body, and feet as one.

4. This is the connection of the Sukkah to the concept of peace, as it is written, "Spread over us the Sukkah of Your peace." Since the Sukkah is a concept of *makif* (which is above division), the concept of peace is drawn from the *makif* of the Sukkah. Peace is made between the animal soul and the divine soul, and also peace within all the levels of the divine soul itself. Because the Sukkah encompasses all of a person's matters, from head to foot, through it, peace is made in all the levels within a person. It even brings peace to the world. As is known, the Sukkah is similar to the Ark of Noah, in which there was the concept of peace (even though all the animals were in the Ark together, they did not harm or destroy one another), a microcosm of the peace that will be in the future, when "they shall not harm or destroy," etc.

Although the *makif* of the Sukkah is the level of the external aspect of *Binah*, and the Ark of Noah is the level of the internal aspect of *Binah*, and in the future it will be even above the level of the internal aspect of *Binah* as it is in its place, nevertheless, the peace of the *makif* of the Sukkah is similar to the peace of the future. For the reason that "they shall not harm or destroy" (in the future) is through *Da'as* (as the verse concludes, "For the earth shall be full of the knowledge of Hashem"), and therefore the *makif* of the Sukkah, whose purpose is "in order that your generations may know," etc. (the concept of *Da'as*), brings about the concept of peace similar to the peace of the future. This is especially true according to the explanation of the connection of the *sechach* of the Sukkah to the cloud of the incense of Yom Kippur, whose essence is the level of *Atik* (and similar to the general concept of Yom Kippur, a day that requires five prayers because it is the level of *Atik*). Since in the level of *Binah* (even in the external aspect of *Binah*) there is a revelation of *Atik*, the level of *Atik* revealed in the *sechach* of the Sukkah brings about the concept of peace, which is why the Sukkah is called "the Sukkah of Your peace."

5. The intention in drawing down the *makifim* of the Sukkah is that they should be drawn down and act within the internal levels as well. This is the concept of taking the Four Species, which comes in sequence after "You shall dwell in Sukkos" (for the mitzvah of dwelling in the Sukkah begins from the start of the seven days of Sukkos, and afterward, in the morning—at dawn or sunrise—the mitzvah of taking the Four Species begins). The concept of taking the Four Species is to draw the *makif* (of the Sukkah) into the internal levels. As explained in Kabbalah and Chassidus, the concept of the *na'anu'im* (shaking) is to draw the level of *Da'as* into the *Nukvah* of *Ze'er Anpin*, which stands at the chest of *Ze'er Anpin* (which is why the Lulav and its species must touch the chest). The concept of drawing into the *Nukvah* is that the influence should extend into the matters of the world, even to the matters of this lowest world, than which there is no lower. Therefore, the action of peace that comes through the drawing down of *Da'as* is also downward in this lowest world, similar to the future when "they shall not harm or destroy," etc., "for the earth shall be full of the knowledge of Hashem." The connection of "the earth shall be full of the knowledge of Hashem" to "they shall not harm or destroy" is because quarrels and strife stem from a smallness of *Da'as*. The parable for this is well-known: a child, who lacks *Da'as*, becomes angry immediately when his will is not fulfilled (as explained in the holy Tanya, that a child becomes angry over small things), whereas an adult, who possesses *Da'as*, can endure even the opposite. It is the same regarding the peace of the Sukkah; since the concept of the Sukkah is "in order that they may know," through the *Da'as* drawn down by the Sukkah, peace is made—the Sukkah of Your peace.

6. However, in order to draw the *makif* of the Sukkah into the level of the internal, to the point that it becomes a state of "dwelling" as it is written, "You shall dwell in Sukkos" (and through the taking of the Four Species this is drawn into the level of the *Nukvah* of *Ze'er Anpin*), even though the *makif* of the Sukkah is most exalted (as mentioned above, that the *makif* of the Sukkah is above even the division of the garments of the head, body, and feet, and certainly has no division of head, body, and feet), nevertheless, there will be the concept of dwelling. The explanation for this is, "I made the Children of Israel dwell in Sukkos when I brought them out of the land of Egypt"—the beginning was from the Holy One, Blessed be He; "I am the One who begins." Through this, that "I made them dwell in Sukkos," the power was given that there should be "You shall dwell in Sukkos." And it says regarding this, "In order that they may know," that the dwelling in the Sukkah must not be in the manner of "I have decreed a decree," but rather that it should be drawn down and come into *Da'as*, into the internal, and be drawn down into the dimension of time as well. This is "You shall dwell in Sukkos for seven days," for the seven days include the entire duration of time and all the changes within them. As explained in many places regarding the verse, "I was a youth and also I have aged," that every first day of the week, the concept of the seven days of Creation begins anew. This is similar to what was explained above, that through the *na'anu'im* one draws into the level of the *Nukvah* (the level of *Malchus*), which is the beginning of the concept of time.

7. The verse continues, "Every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos." We must understand: the commandment "You shall dwell in Sukkos" is for all of Israel; what does "Every native in Israel" add? The matter is that *ezrach* (native) refers to the level that will shine in the future. Therefore, the word *ezrach* begins with an *Aleph*, which is one of the letters used for the future. This is "Every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos," which has two meanings: that from the aspect of the *ezrach* in Israel that will be in the future, there is the power even now that there should be "You shall dwell in Sukkos," and that even in the level of the *ezrach* in Israel, and even as it will be in the future, in this level too, something is added through the dwelling in the Sukkah. It can be said that this is the reason why the mitzvah of Sukkah will be in the future with greater intensity and strength, as explained in the prophecy of Zechariah (which we read as the Haftorah during the days of Sukkos).

8. Following the mitzvah of dwelling in the Sukkah and taking the Four Species comes the mitzvah of offering the seventy bulls corresponding to the seventy nations. This means that through the offering of the seventy bulls, one protects the seventy nations and reveals the purpose of their creation, for the entire world was not created except to serve this one, and "I was created to serve my Maker." Their true essence (of the nations of the world) is as it will be in the future, to serve Israel, etc., as it is written, "And kings shall be your foster fathers, and their queens your nursing mothers," and certainly those who are below kings and queens. And since all the matters and revelations of the future depend on our deeds and our service during the six thousand years (including the duration of the exile, and on the contrary, they depend primarily on the service during the time of exile), the concept of "And kings shall be your foster fathers," etc., that will be in the future, is brought about by the offering of the seventy bulls in the time of the Beis HaMikdash and by "We will render the bulls with our lips" after the destruction of the Beis HaMikdash, etc., in the time of exile.

It is known that the service of offerings (and similarly the service of "We will render the bulls with our lips") acts upon the entire world. Through the offering of one animal, an elevation is made in the entire species of animals, etc. (through the elevation of the blood and the fat, which are the vitality and the pleasure of the animal being offered, an elevation is made in the entire animal species), and through the influence drawn down from above to below, an influence is made in the entire animal species, etc. Similarly, it is with the offering of the seventy bulls, which correspond to the seventy nations; the elevation and the influence through these offerings act upon all seventy nations. Likewise, it is with the recitation of the verses, for the movement of the lips is an act; through the recitation of the verses of the offerings of the seventy bulls that correspond to the seventy nations, an influence and a subjugation are made upon them, and the purpose of their creation is revealed—that they were not created except to serve this one, etc.—and it is revealed that "You have chosen us from all the peoples." This is what is written, "Praise Hashem, all you nations; laud Him, all you peoples, for His kindness has prevailed over us," etc. Seemingly, what is the connection of "His kindness has prevailed *over us*" that the nations and peoples should praise and laud Him? The explanation is known: through the fact that His kindness prevailed over us, a clarification, refinement, and elevation are made even in the nations and peoples, to the point that this is visibly apparent in them, and therefore they praise and laud Hashem. This matter is accomplished through the offering of the seventy bulls, and similarly through the service of "We will render the bulls with our lips," for since the movement of the lips is an act, the action through our lips is also in the world of *Asiyah*.

9. This is the connection and bond of the offering of the seventy bulls to "You shall dwell in Sukkos," for in order to act upon the nations and peoples, there must first be the drawing down of the *makif* through the Sukkah, for the *Ohr Makif* blinds the eyes of the external forces. And although the clarification of the nations and peoples is through internal service specifically (which is why the clarification of the seventy nations is done through the offering of the seventy bulls, for offerings are a concept of the internal, "My bread for My fire"), and similar to the general concept of clarifications which is through internal service, as the saying goes, "Eat bread at the edge of the sword" (for eating is a concept of internal clarification), nevertheless, first there must be the drawing down of the *makif* of the Sukkah, through which a general nullification and subjugation are made in them. This nullification is a preface, preparation, and qualification for their clarification afterward through the offering of the seventy bulls, and similarly through "We will render the bulls with our lips" in the time of exile, which is also a near preparation for the offering of the seventy bulls in its literal sense.

10. This is "You shall dwell in Sukkos," etc., "in order that your generations may know," etc. Through "You shall dwell in Sukkos," the fact that the *makif* of the Sukkah is in the manner of "dwelling"—and on the contrary, the entire concept of the mitzvah of Sukkah is "dwelling" (dwell as you live, that the eating and drinking and all his matters that he does in his house should be in the Sukkah)—this causes the *makifim* to be drawn down into "your generations" as well, meaning into the revealed powers (*Chabad* and the seven *midos*), until "your generations may know," *Da'as* being a term for connection, that the action of the *makifim* in the revealed powers is in the manner of *Da'as* and connection. The reason (and the empowerment) for this is, "For I made the Children of Israel dwell in Sukkos when I brought them out of the land of Egypt." Since I (the Holy One, Blessed be He) am the One who begins, "I made them dwell in Sukkos," this gives power to each and every one of Israel—"You are called *Adam* (man)," after the likeness of the One Above—that he too should act similar to the action of the Holy One, Blessed be He, that he should draw down the peace of the "Sukkah of Your peace" into all the matters of his service and all his configuration (until "your generations may know" his revealed powers), and until the drawing down of the *makif* of the Sukkah is felt by him in his prayer as well, including the *Mussaf* prayer and the recitation of the verses of the offerings of the seventy bulls, through which he acts upon the seventy nations in their literal sense. As we say in *Hallel* (which we say in its entirety during all seven days of Sukkos), "Praise Hashem, all you nations; laud Him, all you peoples," which refers to the nations and peoples in their literal sense.

This also becomes a near preparation for "Every native in Israel," who will shine in the future, "shall dwell." It is known what the Rambam writes in his commentary on the World to Come, that the World to Come exists and stands (even now), and it is called the World to Come because it comes after (and in sequence to) the service in this world. It is the same regarding the matters of the future—that the matters (are accomplished and) exist even now, and what is called the future is because their drawing down and revelation are after and through the precedence of the service in this world. This is "You shall dwell in Sukkos," etc., "Every native in Israel," etc., that through the fulfillment of the mitzvah of "You shall dwell in Sukkos," we draw down even now the level of "Every native in Israel," and this becomes a near preparation for the revelation of this level in the future, when it will be revealed in all its strength.

11. This is why when the first day of the festival falls on Shabbos, the Lulav does not override Shabbos, because the taking of the Lulav is in order to draw the level of the *makif* (of the Sukkah) into the internal. Therefore, on Shabbos, when the *makif* shines in the internal (due to the concept of Shabbos), there is no need (so much) for the taking of the Lulav, and therefore the decree of the Sages is sufficient to override the taking of the Lulav. The fact that in the time of the Beis HaMikdash they would take it even on Shabbos is because there are several levels in the *makifim*, and through the taking of the Lulav in the time of the Temple, they drew down a level of *makif* higher than the level of *makif* that is drawn on Shabbos by itself (what Shabbos sanctifies and establishes) and also outside the Land (in the time of the Temple). Not so in the time of exile; even through the taking of the Lulav, one cannot draw down the higher level of *makif*, and what we draw down now is only the level of *makif* that is drawn down through Shabbos.

As mentioned above, "You shall dwell in Sukkos," the drawing down of the *makif* of the Sukkah, is a near preparation for "Every native in Israel" who will shine in the future—the revelation of the level of the true *makif*, the level of the internal aspect of *Atik*. This is the innovation in the revelation of the future: that now all revelations are only from the level of the external aspect of *Atik*, and in the future there will be a revelation of the level of the internal aspect of *Atik*. This is what is written, "Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth," that they will learn the Torah of Moshiach from the mouth of Moshiach, whose essence is the level of the internal aspect of *Atik* (this is why Moshiach is above even Adam HaRishon, as it is written, "He shall be exalted and lifted up and very high"—*me'od* (very) has the same letters as *Adam* (man), but in a combination that points to *b'li gevul* (without limit), that he is above even Adam HaRishon). This is "You shall dwell in Sukkos," etc., "Every native," etc., that the drawing down of the *makif* of the Sukkah is a near preparation for the revelations of the future redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, who will come and redeem us and lead us upright to our land, and there we will perform before You the offerings of our obligations according to the mitzvah of Your will, speedily and in our days.

## 13 Tishrei (Hilula of the Rebbe Maharash), Eve of Sukkos, 5738

**"In Sukkos you shall dwell for seven days; all the native-born in Yisrael shall dwell in Sukkos"**. The precise questions raised by our Rebbes regarding this verse are well known. One must understand the redundancy: once the Torah has already commanded, "In Sukkos you shall dwell for seven days" [which is a command for the entirety of Yisrael and for every individual Jew], what is added by the subsequent phrase, "all the native-born in Yisrael shall dwell in Sukkos"?

Furthermore, one must understand the very essence of the mitzvah of Sukkah, which seemingly contains a contradiction. Dwelling in the Sukkah must be "as you dwell" (*ke'ein tadoru*); for all seven days, a person must treat the Sukkah as their permanent residence and their house as a temporary one. Just as throughout the year one lives in their home in a permanent fashion—conducting all their affairs there—so too must one’s dwelling in the Sukkah be: one should conduct all their affairs in the Sukkah, viewing it as their home and place of permanence during these seven days. Yet, at the same time, the very construction of the Sukkah must be a temporary structure. This requirement—that the Sukkah be a temporary structure—is a fundamental condition of the mitzvah. This is why a Sukkah higher than twenty amos is invalid; one of the reasons for this is that up to twenty amos, one’s dwelling is considered temporary, whereas above twenty amos, it is no longer a temporary dwelling but a permanent one, and the Sukkah must be a temporary dwelling specifically. Thus, for the seven days, one’s dwelling in it must be permanent, while the structure itself must be temporary—which is, on the face of it, a contradiction.

Moreover, there is another unique aspect of the mitzvah of Sukkah that requires explanation: it is a time-bound positive mitzvah. Beyond being limited by time, it is also limited by place; the location must be suitable for the construction of a Sukkah. One who builds a Sukkah under a tree is considered as if they built it inside a house, as it is not a place fit for this mitzvah. This is unlike other Yomim Tovim, where we find limitations regarding time, but not regarding place.

2) Following the mitzvah of dwelling in the Sukkah ("In Sukkos you shall dwell") comes the mitzvah of taking the Four Species, as it is written: "And you shall take for yourselves on the first day the fruit of a beautiful tree, palm branches, a thick-leaved branch, and willows of the brook..." [The mitzvah of dwelling in the Sukkah begins from the very first moment of the start of the seven days of Sukkos, and afterward—as a continuation—in the morning (at daybreak, or ideally at sunrise), begins the time of the obligation for the mitzvah of taking the Four Species, which is a mitzvah that must be performed during the day]. From this, it is understood that the mitzvah of taking the Four Species has a connection to the matter of the Sukkah. This is especially true according to the known principle that the most preferred way to perform the mitzvah of the Four Species is specifically within the Sukkah, which emphasizes even further that there is a bond between the mitzvah of the Four Species ("And you shall take for yourselves") and the mitzvah of Sukkah ("In Sukkos you shall dwell").

One must understand what the connection is between these two mitzvos. This is especially puzzling since they are seemingly distinct mitzvos: the mitzvah of Sukkah lasts for seven days, whereas the primary mitzvah of the Four Species—by Torah law—is only on the first day. [There is also the known distinction between the Temple and the provinces, as explained in the Gemara regarding the ordinance of Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai, from which stem various laws and details that apply even in our time regarding the difference between the first day and the six days that follow.]

Furthermore, regarding the Four Species themselves, one must understand why these four were chosen specifically. Although they are all from the category of vegetation, the command emphasizes from the very beginning that they must be these specific types. All the species must be in a state of "beauty" (*hadar*), as it is written, "And you shall take for yourselves... the fruit of a beautiful tree," and the Gemara derives that this applies also to the three subsequent species [even though the verse explicitly mentions *hadar* only regarding the *esrog*, the first species].

3) Beyond the aforementioned points, it is known from our Sages that the mitzvos were given only to refine the creatures (as explained in many places). Beyond the essence of the mitzvah—the purpose of which is "Who has sanctified us with His mitzvos and commanded us," and which one must fulfill to satisfy the command and will of the Commander—there is an additional, specific *avodah* within the mitzvah, a function related to the *avodah* of man in general. This is also true of the *avodah* of Yisrael during the festival of Sukkos, both in the mitzvah of "In Sukkos you shall dwell for seven days," in the mitzvah of "And you shall take for yourselves on the first day," and in the *Simchas Beis HaShoeivah* that follows. All of them have their own content and function in *avodah*—to refine the creatures through them.

This is also true regarding the eighth day, *Shemini Atzeres*. Although the eighth day is a festival in its own right, it is still called the "eighth day," which itself indicates its connection to the seven days of Sukkos that precede it. Furthermore (not only is *Shemini Atzeres* connected to Sukkos, but beyond that), just as in simple terms one must reach the seven days before arriving at the eighth, so too is it in the spiritual content of Sukkos and *Shemini Atzeres*: although the *avodah* of *Shemini Atzeres* and *Simchas Torah* comes after [meaning it is on a higher level than] the *avodah* of the days of Sukkos that precede it, nevertheless, the cause and the factor that enables one to reach the *avodah* of *Shemini Atzeres* is specifically the *avodah* of the seven days that precede it. From this, one can understand the elevation of the *avodah* of Sukkos, which is specifically the cause and factor through which one arrives at the *avodah*, the *hemshech* (drawing down of light), and the levels inherent in the matter of *Shemini Atzeres*.

4) The explanation of this matter is as follows: It is written, "In the covering (*bakeseh*) for the day of our festival," where "the day of our festival" refers to the festival of Sukkos, as explained in many places. This is the meaning of "In the covering for the day of our festival": all the matters that were in the *keseh* (in concealment and hiddenness) during the days preceding our festival—and especially the matters that were in concealment and hiddenness on Rosh Hashanah [to which the term *bakeseh* refers, from the expression "for the day of the covering (*hakeseh*) he shall come to his house", and to this refers the verse "Blow the shofar on the new moon" (as a preface to "In the covering for the day of our festival")]—are revealed on Sukkos.

As it is stated in the Midrash, on Rosh Hashanah we do not know who is victorious, but when Yisrael emerge... with their *lulavim* and *esrogim* in their hands, we know that Yisrael are the victors. This means that on Sukkos, it is revealed that "we are the victors," and in a manner that is manifest to all the inhabitants of the world, to the point that the entire reality of the world sees that we are the victors. Even though the victory of Yisrael already occurred on Rosh Hashanah—as the Midrash states, "Which nation is like this nation that knows the character of its G-d?"—that Yisrael know that the Holy One, blessed be He, will perform a miracle for them and they will be vindicated in judgment; and if so, it is a simple matter that on the day of Rosh Hashanah they had a "good writing and sealing"—nevertheless, the ultimate intent and perfection is that this victory be in a state of revelation. This is accomplished on the festival of Sukkos, "In the covering for the day of our festival," where the victory that was in the *keseh* (in concealment) on Rosh Hashanah is drawn down in revelation on the "day of our festival," and through this, one arrives at the ultimate perfection.

5) Yom Kippur is called "Rosh Hashanah" in Scripture, as is brought in *Likutei Torah*. In the order of the days, Yom Kippur is on the tenth of the seventh month, and at the beginning of the month, on the first of the seventh month, is Rosh Hashanah. Furthermore, our Sages said that these are the ten days between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, meaning that Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur are included in those ten days (and their content is one matter). From this, it is understood that on Yom Kippur, a unique matter is drawn down that relates to the *hemshech* of Rosh Hashanah.

The matter is, as explained in *Likutei Torah*, that on Yom Kippur, the *hemshech* is of the level of *neshamah* and *penimiyus* (inwardness). And since the *hemshech* in the level of *penimiyus* is of an essential nature, it is understood that in all other matters as well, the *hemshech* of Yom Kippur is in the level of *penimiyus*. However, even this *hemshech* on Yom Kippur is still in a manner of "covering" (*bakeseh*). The *hemshech* in the manner of "we are the victors" and in revelation [to the point that it is a revelation of such a nature that through it, it becomes "the time of our rejoicing," for joy is only over something that is specifically in revelation, as is known] is specifically on the "day of our festival" (the festival of Sukkos), on the fifteenth of the month, when the moon stands in its fullness, as explained in many places.

6) With this, one can understand why we find a connection between the blowing of the shofar and Sukkos. As is known, *sechach* [for all of Sukkos, the verse "sechach" implies, and as the Rebbe the Alter Rebbe explains in *Shulchan Aruch, Hilchos Sukkah*, that it is called a Sukkah only because of the *sechach* alone. Meaning, not only is the *sechach* the essence of the Sukkah, but furthermore, in the matter of the *sechach*, the entire content of the Sukkah is expressed (*git zich arois*)] has the *gematria* of one hundred, corresponding to the one hundred sounds of the shofar that Yisrael have the custom to blow on Rosh Hashanah. The reason for this is as mentioned above: the matter of the Sukkah is drawn down through the blowing of the shofar on Rosh Hashanah. On Rosh Hashanah, the *hemshech* (which we draw down through the blowing of the shofar via the one hundred sounds) is in a manner of *bakeseh* (in concealment), and afterward, it comes into revelation on the festival of Sukkos ("the day of our festival") in the *sechach* of the Sukkah.

The same applies to Yom Kippur; it is known that the *sechach* of the Sukkah is a likeness of the Clouds of Glory [according to the opinion in the Gemara that they were Clouds of Glory, and this is also brought as a halachic ruling in the *Shulchan Aruch* of the Alter Rebbe at the beginning of *Hilchos Sukkah*], and it is drawn down from the cloud of the incense of Yom Kippur. The explanation of this is as mentioned above: first, the *hemshech* of Rosh Hashanah comes in the level of *penimiyus* on Yom Kippur. This *hemshech* on Yom Kippur is through the matter of the incense, from which it was required that "the cloud of the incense shall cover the cover" (*kapores*), and in a more general sense, this is through the general *avodah* of the High Priest on Yom Kippur in the Holy of Holies. From the cloud of the incense on Yom Kippur, the *sechach* of the Sukkah is subsequently drawn down (for the Sukkah is named after the Clouds of Glory).

7) Based on the above, the explanation and the point of all the aforementioned matters will be understood. The matter is that the *avodah* of Yom Kippur in the Holy of Holies is at the time called in Scripture "once a year." Just as in time it is the level of "one" in time, so too is it in the soul and in the world (place), where it is in the level of "one" in the soul and in place. The *avodah* is performed by the High Priest, who is the sanctified one and the level of "one" among the people of Yisrael, as it is written, "And Aharon was set apart to sanctify him as Holy of Holies," and the *avodah* is in the Holy of Holies, the place sanctified above the entire world, even above the holy place in the *Beis Hamikdash* (the Sanctuary).

The entire order of *Hishtalshelus* (the chain of creation) is founded upon these three matters of "one" (*echad*), as it is drawn down from its root and source [and therefore the *Sefer Yetzirah* is founded upon these three levels], down to how creation is from the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He. As it is stated in *Iggeres HaKodesh* that the Essence and Being of the Emanator, blessed be He—whose existence is from His Essence and is not caused by any cause that preceded Him, G-d forbid, etc.—is alone in His power and ability to create *yesh me'ayin* (something from nothing) and absolute nothingness. This is the matter of the level of "once a year," in the world (place), and in the soul, which are united together in the *avodah* of the High Priest on Yom Kippur in the Holy of Holies: the level of the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He, which is drawn down into the level of "one."

And the level of "one" is drawn down in revelation afterward on Sukkos. It is explained in the Mishnah regarding the order of the *avodah* of Yom Kippur that the High Priest would wait in the Holy of Holies until the house was filled with smoke, and from this smoke (which is from the *avodah* of the level of "one") the *sechach* of the Sukkah is drawn down on the festival of Sukkos.

8) The beginning of the *hemshech* of the level of "one" is on Rosh Hashanah, at which time the *avodah* is "Make Me King over you," through which we draw down the Kingship from the level of self-transcendence. That is, just as He is in His essential level, it is drawn down in revelation until it becomes "Make Me King over you." And by drawing down that He should be the King of Yisrael ("Make Me King over you"), it is drawn down afterward that He should be "King over the entire world in Your glory."

The manner of Kingship is through will, and not just that "His kingdom rules over all." For rule can exist even by necessity, without will, but Yisrael, through their *avodah* of "His kingdom they accepted upon themselves with will," draw down into the entire reality, into the entire order of *Hishtalshelus*, that it should be "Come, let us bow down and kneel, let us bend the knee before Hashem our Maker". Afterward, on the tenth of the seventh month, the *hemshech* comes in the level of *penimiyus*. And afterward, on the festival of Sukkos, the *hemshech* comes in revelation. The victory that was on Rosh Hashanah—starting from the victory that "King of Yisrael" is drawn down to be "His kingdom rules over all," and higher still, "His kingdom they accepted upon themselves with will"—is drawn down in revelation until it is drawn down in revelation also in the world and also among the seventy nations. This is why we offer seventy bulls on the festival of Sukkos, corresponding to the seventy nations.

This *hemshech* is drawn down in revelation (during the four days between Yom Kippur and Sukkos) first in Torah and its mitzvos, through studying the laws of Sukkah and the laws of taking the Four Species. For through studying this, even though we are in exile and even though it is not possible to fulfill all the details, it is as our Sages said, "I account it for them as if they were occupied, etc." And by first effecting the *hemshech* in revelation in Torah, and afterward drawing this down also into action through the fulfillment of the mitzvos [although this is still the level of action within the mitzvos themselves], it is drawn down afterward in practice also into the entire world, so that the entire world sees how a Jew fulfills the mitzvah. And it is drawn down in such a manner that it is stated in the Midrash that if the nations of the world knew how beautiful the *Beis Hamikdash* was for them, they would have surrounded it with legions to guard it, etc. This effect of the *Beis Hamikdash* on the nations is on the festival of Sukkos in a revealed manner, through the offering of the seventy bulls corresponding to the seventy nations, as explained at length in the Midrashim of our Sages.

9) Based on the above, that the matter of the Sukkah relates to the level of "once a year," and to the world (place) and the soul, it is also understood why regarding Sukkos (specifically) it is said, "All the native-born in Yisrael shall dwell in Sukkos," and similarly, our Sages derived that "all Yisrael are fit to sit in one Sukkah," for the *hemshech* of the level of "one" in revelation is on the festival of Sukkos.

The explanation of this matter will be understood by first addressing why this requires explanation [and as this point is also explained in the discourses of our Rebbes<2>]: why is this teaching that "all Yisrael are fit to sit in one Sukkah" derived specifically from "All the native-born in Yisrael shall dwell in Sukkos"? Could one not also derive this teaching from "In Sukkos you shall dwell," that you shall all dwell together?

The general explanation of this (in the language of Kabbalah) is as follows: In the beginning [meaning before the *Tzimtzum*], the Infinite Light filled the entire space of the void, and there was no place for the existence of the worlds [meaning there was no possibility or reality for worlds]. Afterward, He withdrew His great light to the sides, etc., and the (great circle and) the *Kav* (line) were drawn down, along with the entire length of the order of *Hishtalshelus*, as explained in *Etz Chaim* at its beginning and in *Mavo Shearim* at its beginning (as is brought in many places and explained in Chassidus). This level (that filled the space of the void before the *Tzimtzum*) is already called by the name "Infinite Light," and it is even possible for there to be some connection there to the reality of the worlds, except that since the Infinite Light filled the entire space of the void, for that reason, the reality of the worlds was not possible. But even there, there is a void, and there is also the possibility that one must negate and say that the reality of the worlds is impossible.

Higher than this is what our Sages said: "Before the world was created, He and His Name were alone." However, even in this level, there are (already) details: "He," "His Name," and "alone," and it is also said that the novelty of this level is that it is in a manner of "He was," as explained in the previous discourse. However, His Essence and Being, blessed be He, is higher even than this, to the point that it is impossible to say of Him that He is in the level of *sovev* (surrounding the worlds), and not even in the level of a distant *makif* (encompassing light), and the like. For His Essence and Being, blessed be He, is not in the category of all these matters at all, to the point that He is in the level of "there is nothing else besides Him," and higher still, "there is no 'else'." For "there is nothing else besides Him" is applicable when there is a category of "besides Him," but higher than this is the matter of "there is no 'else'," as this point is explained elsewhere.

Since Yisrael are rooted in the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He, it is understood that they have a connection and it is possible for them to reach and effect change in the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He, even as He is higher in the level of "there is no 'else'," even higher than the level of "He and His Name were alone." This is what our Sages meant when they said, "With whom did He consult? With the souls of the righteous," for the matter of "consultation" (kingship) is applicable even higher and higher, and also in the level that is above the category of place. Because since above, all matters are through will and not through necessity, G-d forbid, it is understood that in this level as well, the matter of kingship is applicable. From this, it is understood that the matter of kingship is, as it were, also in the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He. And regarding this, they say, "With whom did He consult? With the souls of the righteous," which refers to the souls of Yisrael, and "Your people are all righteous", who are rooted in the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He, and therefore they also reach the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He.

The matter of kingship is also in the "small" souls, in a level that is seemingly lower, as understood from the parable of the Rebbe the Maggid, who compares this matter to a father and a small son. And since he compares the Holy One, blessed be He, to a father, as it were, it is understood that great and small are equal before Him. All matters are not in any proportion at all to the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He, but rather it so arose in His will that the souls of Yisrael, "Your people are all righteous," should be rooted in the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He, *itself*, and for that reason, the souls of Yisrael are rooted in the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He.

10) Since it is the beginning of the year and we must draw down the entire order of *Hishtalshelus* anew, and also in a revealed manner, therefore we must draw down from the level of "one." This *hemshech* (in the level of revelation) is accomplished on the "day of our festival" (the festival of Sukkos), as mentioned above. Therefore, the beginning of the *avodah* of Sukkos must also be in the matter of "one Sukkah" (for one Sukkah relates to the *avodah* of Yom Kippur in the three levels of "one" in the *Esh-N* (smoke/fire), as mentioned above), because through the *avodah* of "one Sukkah," one arrives at the level of "one" that is entirely above any category of number (except that since we have no other name to call it, for that reason we call it "one"), to the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He.

"One" (*echad*) means "unique" (*yechidah*), as it is stated in *Tosafos*, and the name *yechidah* is because it receives from the "Unique One" (*Yachid*). So too is it in the level of "one Sukkah," whose content is the matter of *yechidah*, as mentioned above. And since the matter of the Sukkah is rooted in the level of "one," therefore it is drawn down below that all Yisrael are fit to sit in one Sukkah, for the Sukkah is above all division, since its root is in the level that is above division. And from this very reason, the level of "one Sukkah" is also drawn down into this lowest world, than which there is no lower, since in relation to the Essence of the Infinite One, blessed be He, it is not applicable to distinguish between higher and lower.

11) The *hemshech* of the Essence in revelation (the level of "one Sukkah") will be only in the time to come, for then it will be, "And the glory of Hashem shall be revealed, and all flesh together shall see", and higher than this will be, "And your Teacher shall no longer hide Himself, and your eyes shall see your Teacher", as it is stated in *Tanya*. However, higher than this is the level of "one," whose content is the *hemshech* of the Essence below in revelation, and from this, it is understood that the revelation of "one Sukkah" will also be only in the time to come.

Based on this, it is understood why our Sages derived that all Yisrael are fit to sit in one Sukkah from what is written at the end of the verse, "All the native-born in Yisrael shall dwell in Sukkos," specifically, and did not derive it from the beginning, "In Sukkos you shall dwell for seven days." For "In Sukkos you shall dwell for seven days" refers to the *avodah* in this time, in the six thousand years that the world exists, and in this time, the level of "one" has not yet been drawn down in revelation. This revelation will be only in the time to come, and for that reason, they derived it from what is written, "All the native-born (*ha'ezrach*) in Yisrael," for *ezrach* is a term for the future, meaning that which will shine (*yizrach*) in the time to come, as explained in several discourses. And this is also according to the rules of grammar, that the letters of *eitan* (the *alef* in *ha'ezrach*) indicate the future. This is "All the native-born in Yisrael," that in the time to come there will be a shining of the level of "one," and therefore all Yisrael will then sit in one Sukkah.

However, nevertheless, even now, in the six thousand years that the world exists, a radiance and shining from the level of "one" that will be in revelation in the time to come shines. Furthermore, the revelation of the level of "one" of the future is drawn down (like other revelations of the future) specifically through our deeds and our *avodah* of now. From this, it is understood that even now there is a "taste" and a radiance from the level of "one". This is what our Sages meant when they said that all Yisrael are fit to sit in one Sukkah, which refers to this time, that even in this time there is the matter that all Yisrael are fit to sit in one Sukkah (a "taste" of the actual sitting in the time to come).
