The same applies to the lulav, where the very fact that it is said it must be *kapot* implies that it has separate leaves that need to be bound. The same applies to the myrtle, which must have three separate leaves that nevertheless unite with one another (in one nest). The same applies to the willows, which are a multiplicity of willows, yet they unite and grow in brotherhood.

3. This is the connection of the mitzvah of taking the Four Species to the Festival of Sukkos, for the essence of the Sukkah is peace—that is, the creation of unity from multiplicity. From this, it is understood regarding the Festival of Sukkos, which (as mentioned at the beginning of the maamar) is named for "You shall dwell in Sukkos," that its essence is the creation of unity from multiplicity. This is the "Festival of Sukkos for seven days." "Seven days" is the concept of division, for every day is different from the other, and they encompass the division of the entire duration of time. For the seven days of the week are the seven days of the cycle. This is why every week we say "today is the first day," "today is the second day," etc. Seemingly, from the creation of the world until now, there have already been thousands and myriads of days; why do we count first, second, third, etc.? The explanation for this is known: it is because they are the days of the cycle that return repeatedly. The essence of the "Festival of Sukkos for seven days" is that all seven days become one entity: the Festival of Sukkos.

4. The explanation of this (the connection of taking the Four Species to the Festival of Sukkos) in greater depth is that the unity in both is not merely the unification of separate things, but a true unity that transcends division. In the mitzvah of taking the Four Species, beyond the unity within each of the four (as it is in itself), which is the unification of separate things, all four species become one mitzvah, one entity. Even though they are four different species, from one extreme to the other—as it is in the Midrash, that the four species of the lulav correspond to the four types in Yisrael, from the type corresponding to the esrog, which has both taste and scent (Torah and good deeds), to the type corresponding to the willow, which has neither taste nor scent—nevertheless, they all (all four types) become "one band to do Your will." This unity (that they all become one band to do Your will) is a true unity that transcends division.

Similarly, it is with the unity of the Sukkah. After it says, "You shall dwell in Sukkos for seven days"—the unity of separate things (the seven days uniting in one Sukkah)—it adds, "Every citizen in Yisrael shall dwell in Sukkos" (simply "shall dwell," without saying "seven days"), representing a true unity that transcends division.

5. According to this, one can understand the connection of the Festival of Sukkos (celebrating the festival of Hashem for seven days, the mitzvah of dwelling in the Sukkah, and the mitzvah of taking the Four Species) to "gathering the produce of the land." For "gathering" is the concept of unity: initially, there are separate things, and the concept of "gathering" is to collect them (and unify them) together. There are two aspects to this (analogous to the two levels of unity in the Sukkah and the Four Species). First is the gathering (and unification) of the multiplicity of details of each species (grain, wine, and oil) in itself, and afterwards is the gathering (unification) of all three species together, bringing them all into the house (to one place).

6. All these matters come after the avodah of the beginning of the month of Tishrei, the letters of *reishis* (beginning). The ten days between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur are the level of division. On Yom Kippur, they are included and united in *one* day (the day of Yom Kippur), "once in the year." After Yom Kippur, the four days between Yom Kippur and Sukkos are the preparation for the fulfillment of the mitzvah of "dwelling in Sukkos" and taking the Four Species, including the avodah of "gathering your produce from the field." This avodah is (as mentioned at the beginning of the maamar) prior to and a preparation for the Festival of Sukkos. However, on Yom Kippur, which has no eating or drinking, it is in a manner of detachment from the affairs of the world. Especially in the *Ne'ilah* prayer of Yom Kippur, when "Yisrael and the King are alone." This is the meaning of *Ne'ilah* (locking): we lock all the gates, and only Yisrael and the King are found (inside).

In order to complete the intent of "a dwelling place in the lower worlds," there must be an *hemshech* (drawing down) of this unity below, into this physical world, even into the physical affairs of this world, starting with "gathering your produce from the field." Although this is a spiritual avodah, it is nevertheless connected with the gathering of the harvest (grain, wine, and oil) in its literal, physical sense. The same applies to the mitzvah of "dwelling in Sukkos": the *sechach* of the Sukkah must come from your threshing floor and your winepress, and specifically from the waste of the threshing floor and winepress. The same applies to the mitzvah of taking the Four Species: it is impossible to fulfill the mitzvah through *kavanah* (intention), through the avodah of *middos* (even though the Four Species are from the category of the plant kingdom, and the plant kingdom is the concept of *middos*), nor through the avodah of *Malchus* (even though the concept of the *na'anu'im*—the shakings—is to draw down into the *Nukvah* of *Ze'er Anpin* (Malchus) which stands at the chest of *Ze'er Anpin*). One requires the physical Four Species specifically, and they must touch the physical chest specifically.

7. This is "the Festival of Ingathering... when you gather in your produce from the field," which is the gathering of all matters together. The beginning of this is gathering all the matters of Torah (like all avodah, whose beginning is in their state as they are in Torah, since "He looked into the Torah and created the world"). Every matter in Torah has 600,000 interpretations in the *peshat* (literal), 600,000 in the *remez* (allusion), and similarly in the *derush* (homiletic) and the *sod* (mystical). One must gather and include all the interpretations of the *peshat* section, and likewise all the interpretations of the *remez*, *derush*, and *sod* sections, and afterwards gather and include all four sections together. This is "gathering your grain, your wine, and your oil": grain (bread) is the revealed Torah, wine is the secrets of the Torah, and oil is the secrets of the secrets. The avodah of "gathering your grain, your wine, and your oil" consists of two parts: the gathering and inclusion of the multiplicity of matters of each category (revealed, secrets, and secrets of secrets) itself, and also the gathering and inclusion of all categories together, to elevate them to a level that transcends division, up to the level of "the place that includes everything" and even higher.

Although the primary aspect of this (the unification of all matters of Torah) will be in the time of Moshiach, when he will teach the entire nation the entire Torah, including the secrets of Torah and the secrets of secrets—as it is written, "Let Him kiss me with the kisses of His mouth," which Rashi explains means that in the future, the secret of the reasons (of the Torah) and its hidden treasures will be revealed, and all this will be in a manner of vision—nevertheless, since "those who taste it have merited," a semblance of this, in a manner of tasting at least, can and must be now. From this (the matter of "gathering your grain, your wine, and your oil" as it is in Torah) is drawn down that there will be "gathering your grain, your wine, and your oil" in all matters of man's avodah in spirituality, and this is drawn down even into "gathering your grain, your wine, and your oil" in its literal sense, in the physical grain, wine, and oil.

The same applies to the unity of dwelling in the Sukkah (which follows the avodah of "gathering"). There are two levels here: "You shall dwell in Sukkos for seven days," which is the unity as it still relates to the seven days (division), and "every citizen in Yisrael shall dwell in Sukkos" (simply, which transcends the seven days). The primary aspect of this will be in the future, as it is known that the interpretation of *ezrach* (citizen) is that which is destined to shine in the future. Yet, there is an illumination of this even now. As the Baal HaHilula explains (based on this), that which is destined to shine in the future within Yisrael and in their inwardness is something that is already "dwelling in Sukkos" for them, which is revealed to them (now) in the level of *Sukkos* and *makif* (encompassing light).

The same applies to the Four Species (the taking of the Four Species is, as mentioned, after dwelling in the Sukkah). Each of the Four Species is "this is the Holy One, blessed be He." This concept is also present now, but now it is in concealment, and the revelation of it will be in the future, when "the glory of Hashem shall be revealed, and all flesh shall see, etc." Then there will also be a gathering of your grain, wine, and oil in a different manner: there will be no need for "gathering" (that Yisrael will need to gather), but rather "strangers shall stand and pasture your flocks, and foreigners shall be your plowmen, etc." The dwelling in the Sukkah will also be in a more exalted manner, as they will dwell in the Sukkah of the skin of the Leviathan. And this matter of "the Holy One, blessed be He" (in the Four Species) will then be in revelation, "as I am written, so I am read." Yet, a semblance of this (of all these matters) exists even now, as mentioned regarding the interpretation of "every citizen in Yisrael shall dwell in Sukkos." And all the more so in the time of the end of the exile (the footsteps of Moshiach), when only a few moments remain, and the Torah has promised that Yisrael will eventually do *teshuvah* at the end of their exile and immediately be redeemed, with joy and goodness of heart.

## 2nd Day of Sukkos, 5723

"You shall dwell in Sukkos for seven days; every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos, so that your generations will know that I caused the Children of Israel to dwell in Sukkos when I brought them out of the land of Egypt." The Rebbe Rayatz notes in his maamar on this topic (in the *Hemshech* of Rosh Hashanah 5703) that "I caused to dwell" (*hoshavti*) implies that *Hashem* caused the Children of Israel to dwell in Sukkos, whereas "you shall dwell" (*teshvu*) is a command for the Jewish people *themselves* to dwell in Sukkos—meaning, to dwell in Sukkos through their own power. Nevertheless, he states that "I caused to dwell" serves as the *reason* for "you shall dwell," for since *Hashem* caused the Children of Israel to dwell in Sukkos (a matter which is only within the capacity of the Creator), they are therefore able and obligated to dwell in Sukkos through their own power. It can be said that the precision in the maamar points to two matters: Since "I caused to dwell" is a matter of the Creator, how is it possible for the created being to resemble the Creator? Furthermore, the fact that it provides a reason for "you shall dwell" ("for I caused to dwell") proves that for the Jewish people to be able to dwell in Sukkos, it is not by their own power alone, but through the granting of power from above. Yet, after Hashem caused the Children of Israel to dwell in Sukkos (when He brought them out of the land of Egypt), they are able to dwell in the Sukkah through their own power.

The point of the explanation here (in the maamar) is that in the Sukkah, *makifim* (encompassing lights) are drawn down, and through dwelling in the Sukkah, one draws them into the *penimiyus* (internal dimensions), and they become "settled" lights. This is what is meant by "you shall dwell in Sukkos" being through the granting of power from above, because the *makifim* (as they are in and of themselves) are the opposite of "settlement." And for there to be "you shall dwell in Sukkos"—that through the Jewish people dwelling in the Sukkah they draw the *makifim* into the *penimiyus*, and even more so, that they become "settled" lights—this is through "I caused to dwell in Sukkos," that *Hashem* caused the Children of Israel to dwell in Sukkos, an *is'arusa d'leila* (arousal from above). And what is stated, "you shall dwell in Sukkos," which means (as above) that the Jewish people will draw the *makifim* into the *penimiyus* through their own power, is because the fact that "I caused to dwell" occurred by way of *is'arusa d'leila* was from the outset in order that through this, there would be "you shall dwell in Sukkos" through *avodah* by their own power.

b) Now, even regarding the dwelling in Sukkos of the Jewish people by their own power, there are two matters: "You shall dwell in Sukkos," and "every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos." Among the differences between these two matters is that "you shall dwell in Sukkos" is in the language of a command, while "shall dwell in Sukkos" is in the language of narrative and information. Another difference is that the command "you shall dwell in Sukkos" is for all of Israel, whereas those who "shall dwell in Sukkos" (of their own accord, without needing a command for it) are (only) the "native in Israel." The maamar explains that in the *makifim* there are two levels: the near *makif* and the far *makif*. The two matters of "you shall dwell in Sukkos" and "shall dwell in Sukkos" correspond to these two levels. "You shall dwell in Sukkos" is the near *makif*, and "shall dwell in Sukkos" is the far *makif*. This is why regarding "shall dwell in Sukkos" it says "every native in Israel," for a "native in Israel" is the essence of the *neshamah* which is rooted in the *Atzmus* (the Essence of Hashem), and the dwelling in the far *makif* is at the level of the essence of the *neshamah*. It can be said that this is why "shall dwell in Sukkos" is not in the language of a command but in the language of narrative and information, because the concept of a command applies only to the level of the *neshamah* that is enclothed in a body; whereas in the *neshamah* above, and especially in the essence of the *neshamah*, there is no command. And the matter of "shall dwell in Sukkos"—that the essence of the *neshamah* dwells in the far *makif*—is (not because of a command, but) by its own nature, because its root is in the *Atzmus*.

The maamar explains that "every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos" is a reason for "you shall dwell in Sukkos." That is, the power for "you shall dwell in Sukkos," to draw down the near *makif*, is because the essence of the *neshamah* (the native in Israel) is rooted in the *Atzmus* (the far *makif*). One must understand: the reason (and the power) for "you shall dwell in Sukkos" is (as explicitly stated in the verse) "for I caused to dwell in Sukkos," so why is the reason of "every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos" also needed?

c) This can be understood by prefacing that since the *makifim* (as they are in and of themselves) do not come into a state of "settlement," the fact that through the Jewish people dwelling in the Sukkah they are drawn into the *penimiyus* and into a state of settlement is like a change in the *makifim*. Furthermore, the language of the maamar—that through dwelling in the Sukkah one draws the *makifim* into the *penimiyus* and they *become* settled lights (rather than "one draws the *makifim* into the *penimiyus* and into settlement")—implies that the change that occurs through the Jewish people dwelling in the Sukkah is also in the *makifim* themselves. That in addition to the fact that through dwelling in the Sukkah they are *drawn* into the *penimiyus* and into settlement, the *makifim* themselves also change to become settled lights. One must understand how it is possible that through the *avodah* of man (dwelling in the Sukkah) there should be a change in the lights above.

Now, the interpretation of "every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos" (simply) is that the Jewish people below (souls in bodies) must dwell in a Sukkah (as it is simply). According to the known principle that all interpretations of the same verse are related to one another, it is understood that the matter of "shall dwell in Sukkos" in the far *makif* is also through dwelling in the Sukkah as it is simply. Accordingly, through the Jewish people dwelling in the Sukkah, a change is also effected in the far *makif*. One must understand, for it is explained in many places regarding the verse "For I, Hashem, do not change," that the concept of regret and repentance (change) applies only to the category of "man" (*adam*), but in the category of "not man," there is no place for regret or change. The reason for this is that the concept of change is (primarily) in the intellect, for in the intellect there is an argument for this side and an argument for that side, and since "man" is *mochin* (intellect) and *midos* (emotions)—meaning that the emotions in man are also according to the intellect—therefore, when a change occurs in the intellect, the emotions also change. This is not the case with a will that is above intellect, namely an essential will that has no reason, not even a hidden reason; it does not change. Similarly above, in the *Sefiros* of *Atzilus* and also in the external aspect of *Keter* (for the external aspect of *Keter* is also included in the category of "man"), change is possible, but in the internal aspect of *Keter* ("not man"), change is not possible. Accordingly, one must understand this, as the maamar implies that through the Jewish people dwelling in the Sukkah, even the far *makif* is drawn into the *penimiyus*; seemingly, how is change possible in the far *makif*?

d) The explanation for this can be said to be that the fact that a *makif* is defined by the fact that it does not come into settlement is how it is in and of itself, but from the perspective of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, for whom there is no difference between *makif* and *penimi*, even the *makif* can come into settlement. Through the Jewish people dwelling in the Sukkah, they draw from the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, and through this, even the *makifim* become settled lights. This can be understood according to the known interpretation of the verse "On the covering for the day of our festival," that the matters which (on Rosh Hashanah and) on Yom Kippur are in "covering" (concealment and hiding), are revealed on Sukkos. From this it is understood that the *avodah* of "you shall dwell in Sukkos" is the revelation of the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur, through which there is the drawing down from the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* (as follows). Through the *avodah* of "you shall dwell in Sukkos," the drawing down from the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that comes through the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur is in a state of revelation. And through this, the *makifim* also become settled lights.

According to the known principle that the *avodah* of man (*is'arusa d'lesata*) through which the drawing down is effected (*is'arusa d'leila*) must be akin to the drawing down, it should be added that the fact that through the *avodah* of "you shall dwell in Sukkos" a change is effected above (that the *makifim* become settled lights) is because the *avodah* of "you shall dwell in Sukkos" relates (also) to the concept of change. This can be understood by prefacing that the reason (simply) for why through *teshuvah* all blemishes are rectified is because through *teshuvah* the person changes and becomes a new reality. In the language of the Rambam: "I am another and not the same person who committed those deeds." This concept is primarily in *teshuvah* as it is simply. Regarding *Tzaddikim*, even their *avodah* of *teshuvah*, whose nature is that they depart from their previous state and reality, is "forbidden from here," nevertheless, since *Tzaddikim* want to do the will of their Creator, and the intention from above (the will of their Creator) in the descent of the *neshamah* into the body is to arrive at *teshuvah*, then in the will of the *Tzaddikim* (even before their *teshuvah*) is included (in concealment) also the departure from their reality that comes through *teshuvah*. This is not the case with a *baal teshuvah* as it is simply, for since before (the *teshuvah*) he was in a state where he did not do the will of Hashem—for he committed sins intentionally—the fact that he subsequently does *teshuvah* is not a continuation of his previous state, and through *teshuvah* a completely new reality is created.

More profoundly, it can be said that the concept of the innovation in *teshuvah* as it is simply is also regarding the will and intention from above. For the will and intention from above in the creation of the world and man is that man should be a *Tzaddik*, as our Sages say, "With whom did He consult? With the souls of the *Tzaddikim*." And through the fact that he sinned and blemished and deviated from the path (the opposite of the intention of the creation of the world and man) and subsequently rectifies this through *teshuvah*, through this is drawn down a new light that was not in the intention of the creation. And the power to draw down a new light that was not in the intention of "With whom did He consult" is because the root of the *neshamos* is in the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, which is above even their root in the matter of "With whom did He consult." It can be said that the fact that through *teshuvah* the person changes from his reality and becomes a completely new reality—a true innovation that is not a continuation of his previous state—is because through *teshuvah* there is the drawing down of the essence of the *neshamah* as it is rooted in the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, and regarding the essence of the *neshamah*, the person's state before the *teshuvah* makes no difference.

Accordingly, one can add an explanation to this: that through the *avodah* of "you shall dwell in Sukkos" (the revelation of the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur) a change is effected in the *makifim* such that they become settled lights, because the concept of drawing down the essence of the *neshamah* through *teshuvah* is revealed in the fact that the person *changes* from his reality. Therefore, the revelation of the drawing down of the *Atzmus* through "you shall dwell in Sukkos" (the revelation of the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur) is in the matter of change, that the *makifim* change to become settled lights.

e) It can be said that the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* which is rooted in the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* (which is above the root of the *neshamos* in the matter of "With whom did He consult") which comes through *teshuvah*, is in two matters: in the *teshuvah* itself and in the cause that brings one to *teshuvah*. The matter is that the fact that a person does *teshuvah* after he has sinned is from the root of the *neshamah* in the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* which is above the matter of "With whom did He consult." For from the perspective of the root of the *neshamos* in the matter of "With whom did He consult" (the souls of the *Tzaddikim*), through the fact that he sinned and blemished (the opposite of the intention of "With whom did He consult"), he is separated from his root; and the fact that after he has sinned he does *teshuvah* and changes his reality is from the revelation of the root of the *neshamah* in the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, which is above the matter of "With whom did He consult." And there is another matter in this: that the cause that brings him to return in *teshuvah* is because the point of Judaism within him is always in perfection (and when the point of Judaism within him is awakened, he does not want in any way to be separated, and he does *teshuvah*). And the fact that the point of Judaism within him is always in perfection—that even at the time of the sin the *neshamah* is in faith with Him, may He be blessed—is because the connection of the *neshamah* to *Elokus* (Divinity) is an essential connection, which is above the connection through Torah and *mitzvos* (the matter of "With whom did He consult").

The difference between the two matters is that the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* in the fact that even after the sins he returns to *Havayah* is that through the sins he became separated from *Elokus* (which is why he must do *teshuvah* for them), but since the root of the *neshamah* is in the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* which is above the matter of "With whom did He consult," the separation through the sins is only in the externality, and therefore it is in his power to do *teshuvah* (through the revelation of the *penimiyus*). And the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* in the cause that brings him to return in *teshuvah* is that through the sins there was not, from the outset, any deficiency, Heaven forbid, in the essential connection of the essence of the *neshamah* to *Elokus*, and even at the time of the sin it is in faith with Him, may He be blessed. And this is similar to what was explained above (in the maamar *V'chol Adam Lo Yihyeh*), that the atonement through the drawing down from the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*—the Master of Will—is in such a way that through the sins a blemish is created, and through the drawing down from the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*—the Master of Will—which is not compelled by the will, the blemishes are *atoned*. And the atonement that is from the essence of Yom Kippur is (not by way of an action, but) that on this day the connection of the essence of the *neshamah* with the *Atzmus* (which is above the Master of Will) is revealed, in which level there is no blemish from the outset.

Accordingly, one can add an explanation to the fact that the essence of Yom Kippur atones (specifically) for those who repent, because for there to be a drawing down of the matter of the essence of the day—the revelation of the essential connection of the essence of the *neshamah* with the *Atzmus*—there must be some action by man [similar to the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* on Rosh Hashanah, which is through the *avodah* of "Make Me King over you"], and since the cause that brings one to do *teshuvah* is the essential connection, therefore, through *teshuvah* there is the drawing down of the matter of the essence of the day.

f) The explanation of the matter in more detail can be understood by prefacing what was explained above (in the maamar *Yivchar Lanu*), that in the *shofar* blowing of Rosh Hashanah, through which "Make Me King over you" is effected, there are three matters: the fulfillment of the *mitzvah* of *shofar* blowing, which is the *is'arusa d'lesata* that awakens the revealed will. The *teshuvah* of *shofar* blowing, through which is drawn down the resolution that is in the concealed will (the thought and the absolute will) and even higher, in the absolute will in its essence, through which the *is'arusa d'lesata* will awaken the revealed will. And the internal *bitul* (nullification) which is from the essence of the *neshamah*, through which the absolute will itself is drawn down. And according to the known principle that the building of *Malchus* is throughout the ten days of *teshuvah*, it can be said that also during the ten days of *teshuvah* there must be (in addition to the *avodah* of *teshuvah*) also *avodah* which is from the essence of the *neshamah*. This is what is said during the ten days of *teshuvah*, "A song of ascents, from the depths," and it is stated in the *Zohar* that "that depth of depths" (depths in the plural) is called *teshuvah*. The Rebbe Rayatz explains in the aforementioned *Hemshech* that these are the two depths of the concealed will and the absolute will in its essence. The drawing down from the depth of the concealed will is through the cry of the voice that comes from the *makif* of *chayah*, and the drawing down from the depth of the absolute will is through the cry of the heart that comes from the *makif* of *yechidah*. And in another place it is stated in the *Zohar* that it says "A song of ascents" simply, and does not specify who said it, but that it is a song of ascents that all the people of the world are destined to say. And in the continuation of the matter there, "Did David say this? For it is written, 'With all my heart I have sought You,' etc. What does he need 'from the depths'? Rather, so we have learned, every person, etc., from the source of all sources." And it is explained in *Chassidus* that the precision in the *Zohar*, "Did David say this, etc.," is because since he already said "With all my heart I have sought You," and "with all my heart" is the internal aspect of the heart, what need is there to say "from the depths"? It explains that one must draw down from the "source of all sources," from the *penimiyus* and *Atzmus* of *Ein Sof*, and in order to draw down from there, it is through the *avodah* which is from the *penimiyus* and essence of the soul, "from the depths," which is above the *avodah* of the internal aspect of the heart ("with all my heart").

It can be said that in the statement of the *Zohar*, "and that depth of depths is called *teshuvah*," it speaks of the matter of *teshuvah*—the drawing down of the resolution that is in the concealed will and in the absolute will, through which the *is'arusa d'lesata* will awaken the revealed will—which is through the *teshuvah* of Rosh Hashanah and the ten days of *teshuvah*. And since the resolution that is in the concealed will and in the absolute will is regarding the revealed will, therefore, the *avodah* through which the resolution is drawn down is also at the level of the essence of the *neshamah* that relates to the revealed powers, which in general are the *makifim* of *chayah* and *yechidah*. And in the statement "A song of ascents simply, etc." (where it does not mention the matter of *teshuvah*), it speaks of the matter of drawing down the absolute will itself (*penimiyus* and *Atzmus* of *Ein Sof*, the source of all sources), for this drawing down is through the *avodah* which is from the essence of the *neshamah* (*penimiyus* and essence of the soul). This is why in this statement (specifically) it says "A song of ascents simply, etc." that "all the people of the world are destined to say," because the *avodah* which is from the essence of the *neshamah* is the same in everyone.

g) It can be said that on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, the *avodah* of "from the depths" is more elevated than the *avodah* of "from the depths" on the days between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. For during the ten days of *teshuvah*, whose nature is (as their name implies) days of *teshuvah* (and the matter of "Make Me King over you" is not as revealed during these days), the *avodah* of "from the depths" (which is in revelation) is (primarily) in the depth of the essence of the *neshamah* that relates to the revealed powers. And on Rosh Hashanah, whose primary nature is "Make Me King over you" [for this matter is primarily from the essence of the *neshamah*], the *avodah* of "from the depths" is in the depth of the essence of the *neshamah* that is above relation to the revealed powers. And similarly on Yom Kippur, for since its primary nature is the atonement that is from the essence of the day, the revelation of the essential connection of the essence of the *neshamah* with the *Atzmus*, therefore, the *avodah* of Yom Kippur is also from the depth of the essence of the *neshamah* that is above relation to the revealed powers.

It can be said that the *avodah* which is from the depth of the essence of the *neshamah* on Yom Kippur is in the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur, which is more elevated than the *teshuvah* of all days of the year and also than the *teshuvah* of the ten days of *teshuvah*. The matter is that in *teshuvah* in general, the essential connection of the essence of the *neshamah* with the *Atzmus* is only the cause that brings one to *teshuvah*, but the *teshuvah* itself [whose nature is regret for having done the opposite of the will and a commitment to fulfill His will, may He be blessed] is from the essence of the *neshamah* that relates to the revealed powers that reach the Master of Will. And the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur is (primarily) to be connected with the *Atzmus*, and therefore one feels in it, in revelation, the connection of the essence of the *neshamah* (which is above relation to the revealed powers) with the *Atzmus* (which is above the Master of Will). It can be said that this is what the Rambam means when he says that Yom Kippur is a time of *teshuvah* for all, that the intention in "for all" is (also) for all equally. For in the *teshuvah* of all days of the year (including also the *teshuvah* of the ten days of *teshuvah*), which is from the level of the essence of the *neshamah* that relates to the revealed powers, there are many differences of levels. And the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur, which is from the essence of the *neshamah* that is above relation to the revealed powers, is the same in everyone.

h) This is "You shall dwell in Sukkos, etc., every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos, etc., for I caused to dwell in Sukkos, etc." For "every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos" and "for I caused to dwell in Sukkos, etc." are two reasons for "you shall dwell in Sukkos" (as explained in the maamar of the *Hemshech* of Rosh Hashanah 5703, cited above in section b). The matter is that "you shall dwell in Sukkos" is the revelation of the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur, and in the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur there are three matters: the *teshuvah* itself, and two matters in the root and cause of the *teshuvah*. For the *teshuvah* itself (even the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur) is that the person *changes* himself, that he departs from his previous reality and becomes a new reality. This change is in the revealed powers of the *neshamah*. And this change in the revealed powers of man is through awakening and revealing the essence of the *neshamah* within him, meaning the essence of the *neshamah* that relates to the revealed powers. [And similarly regarding above, that the person becomes *pleasing* before Him, may He be blessed, as before the sin, is because through *teshuvah* a new will is drawn down from the *Ohr Ein Sof*—the Master of Will]. And there is another matter in the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur, that one feels in it, in revelation, that the root and the true cause of *teshuvah* is the connection of the essence of the *neshamah* (which is above relation to the revealed powers) with the *Atzmus* (which is above the Master of Will). Therefore, also in the matter of "you shall dwell in Sukkos" (which is the revelation of the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur), there are the three matters: the matter of "you shall dwell in Sukkos" itself—the change in the near *makif*, that it becomes a settled light. And the reason for this is—"for I caused to dwell in Sukkos, etc." by way of *is'arusa d'leila*. It can be said that it is similar to what is brought above in section f, that the fact that through *is'arusa d'lesata* the revealed will is awakened (even though the *is'arusa d'lesata* is incomparable to the will) is not from the revealed will itself (for since the revealed will relates to the worlds, it is defined by the fact that the created beings are incomparable to it and it is not in their power to awaken it), but because it was so decided in the absolute will. And similarly in the matter of "you shall dwell in Sukkos," that the change, so to speak, that is effected in the near *makif* to be a settled light is because "I caused to dwell in Sukkos" by way of *is'arusa d'leila*, a drawing down from the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, for whom there is no difference between *makif* and *penimi* and even the *makif* can come into settlement. [Similar to how through *teshuvah* a (new) *will* is drawn down from the *Ohr Ein Sof*—the Master of Will]. And another reason for "you shall dwell in Sukkos"—"every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos," in the far *makif*. For the matter of dwelling (settlement) in the far *makif* is not by way of an action [for in the far *makif*, the category of "not man," it is not possible for there to be an action and change, as above in section c], but that every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos (the far *makif*) by their own nature, because they are rooted in the *Atzmus*. [And this is what our Sages said regarding the verse "Every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos," that it teaches that all of Israel are fit to dwell in one Sukkah, for at this level all of Israel are equal]. And it is similar to the matter of the essence of Yom Kippur, that from the perspective of the essential connection of the essence of the *neshamah* with the *Atzmus*, there is no blemish from the outset. And just as on Yom Kippur, the matter of the essence shines in revelation also in the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur, that in the *teshuvah* of Yom Kippur one feels (as above) that it is drawn from the essential connection of the essence of the *neshamah* with the *Atzmus*, similarly it is on Sukkos, that "every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos" is a reason for "you shall dwell in Sukkos," that the fact that the near *makif* is drawn into the *penimiyus* and becomes a settled light, one feels in it that the reason for this is "every native in Israel shall dwell in Sukkos," in the far *makif*, the essential connection of the essence of the *neshamah* with the *Atzmus*.

## Second Day of Sukkos, 5731

"Praise Hashem, all you nations; laud Him, all you peoples. For His kindness has prevailed over us, and the truth of Hashem is forever; Halleluyah". We must understand: what is the reason that because "His kindness has prevailed *over us*," the nations and peoples should praise and laud Him? This is the very question raised in the Gemara, Pesachim : "What business do the nations of the world have [praising Him]?" Rashi explains (in the Gemara’s resolution there) that even though His kindness prevailed *over us*, since this is revealed—such that they also see it ("for you have seen what He did for me")—they are therefore obligated to praise and laud Him. However, this is not sufficient, as stated in the *maamar* titled *Halelu Es Hashem* by our Alter Rebbe, that "this is not a sufficient reason." For the phrasing implies that "for His kindness has prevailed over us" is the reason for "Praise Hashem, all you nations..." (which implies he is interpreting it according to Rashi’s aforementioned explanation), yet it is not a sufficient reason. Therefore, the Rashbam explains there that "for His kindness has prevailed over us" is a separate matter, and that "for" here means: how much more so should the nations and peoples praise and laud Him for the mighty acts and wonders He performed in the world, and how much more so should we, for His kindness has prevailed over us more than over any other nation or tongue.

The special connection of "Praise Hashem, all you nations; laud Him, all you peoples" to the festival of Sukkos is as stated in the Gemara and Midrash : the seventy bulls offered during Sukkos correspond to the seventy nations, which are the totality of all nations. Because of this, on Sukkos, "Praise Hashem, *all* you nations; laud Him, *all* you peoples." We must understand : the Zohar states that during the seven days of the festival, the *ushpizin* (guests) are invited—our forefathers Avraham, Yitzchak, and Yaakov, etc., until Dovid Malka Meshicha, who is the seventh. How does this align with what is explained in the Gemara and Midrash that the seventy bulls correspond to the seventy nations? Even though the Ramaz writes there, and the *Sechel Tov*, etc., that the *ushpizin* in the Zohar is a different interpretation, it is still insufficient, as written in the *maamar* there. Yet, "these and those are the words of the Living G-d". How can these two interpretations coexist, especially according to the known principle (and also for practical *halacha*) that all matters contained within a single text are connected to one another? It is understood *a fortiori* in our case (where the two interpretations are not just in one text, but in the entire subject matter) that the interpretation of the Gemara and Midrash—that during the seven days it is the King’s feast for the citizens, and those dining are the seventy ministers—and the Zohar’s interpretation—that on Sukkos the feast is invited for the *ushpizin ilain* (supernal guests)—must have an authentic connection, since both are words of truth. Especially according to how the Gemara and Midrash conclude there, that on Shemini Atzeres a single bull is offered, etc., corresponding to the unique nation, like a parable of a king who said, "Make me a great feast and invite all the citizens," and for the last day said to his beloved, "Make me a small feast; let us mingle, I and you, with whatever you find." The "great feast" in the parable is the seventy bulls, and the "small feast" is the one bull offered on Shemini Atzeres. Given the vast difference in value between seventy bulls (for all the citizens) and one bull (for his beloved), according to the Zohar’s interpretation that even during the seven days of the festival the *ushpizin ilain* are invited (for from the very fact that it uses the term "invited" for the *ushpizin*, similar to the Midrash’s parable of the king who "invited," it implies they are connected), who are our forefathers Avraham, Yitzchak, and Yaakov, etc., until Dovid Malka, we must understand: what is the innovation of the "small feast" of Shemini Atzeres over the feast of the seven days of Sukkos?

The phrase "for His kindness has prevailed *over us*"—specifically "His kindness" (*chasdo*), not just "kindness" (*chesed*)—can be explained according to our Sages regarding the twenty-six times "for His kindness is forever," which refers to the twenty-six generations before the Giving of the Torah that were sustained by the kindness of the Holy One, Blessed be He. It is explained in several places that this refers to a level of *chesed* that is above the *seder hishtalshelus* (order of progression), the *chesed* of *Arich Anpin*. From this, we can understand that in "for His kindness has prevailed over us," His kindness is a level of *chesed* that is above *hishtalshelus*, and "His kindness has prevailed over us" is the intensification of this *chesed*—meaning it is even more elevated than the *chesed* of *Arich*, which is an extremely lofty level. The same applies to what is said afterward, "and the truth of Hashem is forever." The concept of "Hashem is truth" is as the Rambam writes at the *beginning* of his work : "He alone is the Truth, and there is no other truth like His truth." Regarding this, the Rambam writes there, "This is what the prophet says," because created beings, with their intellect, have no knowledge of the matter of His truth. Therefore, he emphasizes, "This is *what the prophet* says" [and so too in what he writes there afterward, "And this is what the Torah says: 'There is nothing else besides Him,' there is no existence of truth besides Him like Him," where he is precise in his language, "And this is what the Torah says" (and not "as we say"), because the knowledge of the wonder of His existence—that there is no existence of truth besides Him like Him—is not within the power of a created being; it is only that the Torah says so]. From this, we understand the greatness of the wonder of the level of "the truth of Hashem," and yet it is said "and the truth of Hashem is forever," meaning the level of "the truth of Hashem" is drawn down into the world. Furthermore, according to the interpretation that "for His kindness has prevailed over us, and the truth of Hashem, etc." is the reason for "Praise Hashem, all you nations; laud Him, all you peoples" (as per Rashi), it follows that the level of "His kindness has prevailed over us"—which is more elevated even than the *chesed* above *hishtalshelus* (as mentioned above)—and likewise the level of "the truth of Hashem," acts even upon the knowledge of *all* the nations and peoples. For although, in general, the knowledge of the nations of the world is only through the name *Elokim*, as it is written, "Elokim will answer the peace of Pharaoh," and their sustenance from the name *Elokim* itself is only from the last forty-five combinations of the name *Elokim*, and the name *Havayah* belongs only to Israel, His "near ones"—nevertheless, on Sukkos, the level of *Havayah*, and even the level of "the truth of Hashem," is felt even by all the nations and peoples, to the point that they must "Praise, etc., laud Him."

To understand all this, we must preface with what is said in the *Haftorah* for the beginning of Sukkos, which explains the matters of the festival of Sukkos even more openly than the reading for Sukkos. Just as in general, the matters of the Written Torah are explained in the Oral Torah, so too it is with the books of the Written Torah themselves: matters stated in the Torah briefly and in hints, etc., are more revealed in the Prophets and Writings, as is evident even simply. In the *Haftorah* for Sukkos, it is said, "And His feet shall stand on that day upon the Mount of Olives," and there are two interpretations of this. One interpretation is that "His feet shall stand" refers to the judgment that will take place in the future. The judgment of the generation of the flood in this world was while sitting, as it is written, "Hashem sat at the flood," and the judgment of the future will be while standing, as it is written, "And His feet shall stand, etc." This is also the continuation of the verses in the *Haftorah*: first it says, "And I will gather all the nations, etc., and Hashem will go forth and fight against those nations as on the day He fought, etc., and His feet shall stand, etc.," until "And Hashem shall be King over all the earth; on that day Hashem shall be One and His Name One." Thus, "His feet shall stand" is the matter of the judgment with which He will judge the nations, etc. As the Midrash cited in *Rabbeinu Bachya* says, "until the treading of the sole of the foot—until the day comes, etc., and His feet shall stand, etc." The second interpretation is that "And His feet shall stand on that day upon the Mount of Olives" refers to an extremely lofty level that will exist in the future. It is written, "a land of olive oil," and it is known that oil signifies the level of *Chochma*, and olives, from which oil is made, signify the level of the source of *Chochma*. The Mount of Olives (the place where olives grow), the source of olives, is above even the level of olives, and "*upon* the Mount of Olives" is above even the level of the Mount of Olives. This is what it means when it says, "And His feet shall stand, etc., upon the Mount of Olives, etc., and the Mount of Olives shall be cleft"—that in the future, the level of "upon the Mount of Olives" will also be revealed. This is also the continuation of what is said afterward, "on that day Hashem shall be One and His Name One," because in order for "Hashem to be One and His Name One"—"as I am written, I am read" —it is specifically through an extremely lofty level, up to the level of "upon the Mount of Olives." Now, according to what was said above that all interpretations of a single verse are connected to one another, we must say that the level of "upon the Mount of Olives," which is an extremely lofty level, is connected to the matter of the judgment that will take place in the future, and we must understand the connection between these two matters.

This will be understood by first explaining the second interpretation of the verse, "And His feet shall stand on that day upon the Mount of Olives." Oil is the level of *Chochma*, as explained regarding the verse, "And you shall gather your grain, your wine, and your oil," that wine is the level of *Binah* and oil is the level of *Chochma*. It is known that water is the level of *Chochma*, and our Sages said, "Water is nothing but Torah," for Torah is the *Chochma* of the Holy One, Blessed be He. For in *Chochma*, there are, in general, two levels. The first level is the level of *Chochma* (in revelation), up to the level of *Chochma Ila'ah* (Supernal Wisdom), and the second level is the level of *Chochma Setimah* (Hidden Wisdom). This is the difference between water and oil: water is the level of revealed *Chochma*, similar to water, which is by nature revealed, as water descends from a high place to a low place. This means that water, by its very nature, is in the state of extension and revelation, to the point that it is drawn down and revealed even in a low place. Not so oil, which by its very nature is hidden and covered within the olives, and requires special effort—crushing—specifically through which it is drawn into revelation. It is the same with the level of *Chochma Setimah*, which by its very nature is in concealment and hiddenness. Even when the level of revealed *Chochma* is drawn from it, it is not that the level of *Chochma Setimah* itself comes into revelation, but rather that *Chochma Setimah* remains in concealment, and it is only a cause through which revelation is drawn into *Chochma*. As is the known example of this in human faculties: the faculty of the intellect (*koach hamaskil*) from which the invention of wisdom is found—the faculty of the intellect itself remains in concealment. This is unlike the extension from *Chochma* to *Binah*, the level of "a point in the palace", where the point of *Chochma* itself is drawn into *Binah*, where the point itself enters the expanse of *Binah* until it becomes a palace and acts (guards) in the palace. Not so in the extension of revealed intellect from the faculty of the intellect; the faculty of the intellect remains in concealment, and only from it is the revealed intellect found. It is the same above regarding the manner of the extension of revealed *Chochma* from *Chochma Setimah*: the level of *Chochma Setimah* remains in concealment, and only from it is the level of revealed *Chochma* found, as it is written, "And wisdom is found from nothingness (*me'ayin*)," that the extension of revealed *Chochma* from *Chochma Setimah* is by way of "finding." To the point that the level of *Chochma Setimah* relative to revealed *Chochma* is called by the name "nothingness" (*ayin*), and the manner of the extension of revealed *Chochma* from *Chochma Setimah* is by way of a *parsah* (partition), or (as the language is sometimes used) by way of *tzimtzum*. This is the difference between water and oil: water signifies the level of revealed *Chochma*, which is the beginning of the *Sefiros* and has a connection to the *Tess Sefiros* that follow, and especially to the *Sefirah* of *Binah*. Not so oil, which signifies *Chochma Setimah*, which is distinct from the *Sefiros*, even from the level of *Chochma*, and only from it is *Chochma* found, as mentioned above. The reason why the manner of the extension of revealed *Chochma* from the level of *Chochma Setimah* is that the level of *Chochma Setimah* itself is not drawn into the level of revealed *Chochma*, and only from it is *Chochma* found, is because the extension of the level of *Chochma Setimah* itself from the level above it is in such a manner. For the level of *Chochma Setimah* is the level of *Chochma* in *Keter*, and its extension is from the level of *Keter* in *Keter*. The manner of the extension of the level of *Chochma* in *Keter* from the level of *Keter* in *Keter* is that the level of *Keter* in *Keter* is only a cause for the extension of the level of *Chochma Setimah*. This is what is meant by "And wisdom is found from nothingness," where "And wisdom" refers (also) to *Chochma Setimah*. As is known that *Chochma Setimah* is the root of the emanated beings, and as will be explained further on, its manner of formation is by way of "found," for *Keter* in *Keter* is the level of *Ayin* relative to *Chochma Setimah*, and from it is "found" the level of *Chochma Setimah*. Since the manner of the extension of *Chochma Setimah* itself is in the manner of "from nothingness is found," therefore the extension of revealed *Chochma* from it is also in this manner. With this, we can understand why there are two opinions regarding oil floating on wine: whether it is a connection or not a connection. Seemingly, it is incomprehensible; after all, oil and wine are *Chochma* and *Binah*, and *Chochma* and *Binah* are "two friends who never separate." What is the reason for the opinion that oil floating on wine is not a connection? The matter is that since oil signifies the level of *Chochma Setimah* as mentioned above, and the level of *Chochma Setimah* is distinct in value from the level of revealed *Chochma*—to the point that there is a matter in *Chochma Setimah* that does not come into revelation at all in revealed *Chochma*, not even by way of a cause (meaning, in addition to the fact that the manner of the extension of revealed *Chochma* from *Chochma Setimah* is that the level of *Chochma Setimah* itself remains in concealment and is only a cause for the finding of revealed *Chochma* as mentioned above, there is a matter in *Chochma Setimah* that does not come into revelation at all in the level of revealed *Chochma*), and how much more so that it does not come into revelation in *Binah*—therefore, there is an opinion that oil floating on wine is not a connection.

As mentioned above, just as the extension of revealed *Chochma* from *Chochma Setimah* is only by way of "found," so too is it regarding the extension of *Chochma Setimah* from the level above it. This is also part of the meaning of what is written, "Their beginning is embedded in their end, and their end in their beginning." "Their beginning" refers to *Chochma Setimah*, which is the root of the emanated beings as mentioned above, and "their end" refers to the level of *Keter* (in *Keter*) of the *Malchus* of *Adam Kadmon* that becomes *Keter* [and in general, the levels above *hishtalshelus*, the level of *Malchus* of *Adam Kadmon*, and higher still, the level of *Malchus* of *Ein Sof* that is before the *Tzimtzum*, which is the level of the letter *Tav*, the end of all letters, as explained in *Likutei Torah*], which is why it is called "end." The connection of the beginning to the end is not a connection in *hishtalshelus* and hierarchy, but only in the manner of "embedding," which is why the language is "embedded" (*na'utz*)—their beginning is in their end, etc. Just as for the standard interpretation explained in most places in the *maamar* of "their beginning is embedded in their end, and their end in their beginning" (where "their end" refers to the end of *hishtalshelus*), it is understood that the embedding of the beginning in the end and the end in the beginning is not by way of connection in the order of *hishtalshelus* and hierarchy—which is why the embedding of the beginning is in the end, and the embedding of the end is in the beginning (and not as in the order of *hishtalshelus* where each level is connected to the level that is close to it)—so too is it for the interpretation that "their end" refers to a level above "their beginning." For what is "embedded"—their beginning in their end, and their end in their beginning—is not by way of connection in hierarchy, but only by way of embedding, and the level of "their end" is not drawn into the level of "their beginning," and is only a cause for it, as mentioned above.

This is also what was explained in previous *maamarim* regarding what is written, "Hashem, my G-d, I will exalt You, etc., for You have made a city into a heap," for it is stated in the Zohar that *Havayah* is the level of the "supernal hidden point." It was explained there that what is stated in the Zohar as "supernal point" is because there is a "lower point," which is the level of lower *Chochma*. Therefore, the Zohar is precise in saying "supernal point," which is the level of supernal *Chochma*. However, the fact that the Zohar adds "hidden" (*setima*) is to indicate that *Havayah* here is not just the level of supernal *Chochma* in general, but the level of *Chochma Setimah*. Regarding this level, it says in the verse "I will exalt You," that they exalt it even higher, and this is the exaltation of the level of *Keter* in *Keter*, as mentioned above, that *Chochma Setimah* is the level of *Chochma* in *Keter*, and the place to which they exalt it is the level of *Keter* in *Keter*. Since the level of *Keter* in *Keter* is not the source for the level of *Chochma* in *Keter*, and only from it is the level of *Chochma* in *Keter* found as mentioned above, therefore it uses the language "I will exalt You," because *Keter* in *Keter* is exalted and distinct from *Chochma* in *Keter*.

This is "And His feet shall stand on that day upon the Mount of Olives." Olives, from which oil is made, are bitter, as our Sages said, "The dove said, etc., 'May my food be as bitter as the olive,' etc.," and there are two interpretations of this. Our Sages said that the olive causes one to forget his learning—but the oil that comes from the olive restores his learning. The matter in *avodah* is that the olive is the *avodah* of *iskafya* (subjugation) and *is'hapcha* (transformation), which are similar to the crushing of the olive. It is written, "pure olive oil for the light" (*katit lama'or*), that through the *avodah* of *katit* (crushing), one reaches the level of *ma'or* (the source of light), from which is found the level of *Chochma*, oil. This means that the olive from which oil is made hints at the level of *Keter* (*Keter* in *Keter*) which is above the level of *Chochma Setimah*. This is why the olive is bitter, for sweetness belongs to light and bitterness to darkness, as it is written, "and the light is sweet." Since *Keter* is the level of darkness, the level of "He made darkness His hiding place", and as the saying goes, "The supernal *Keter*, even though it is 'clear light, polished light,' is dark before the eyes of the emanated beings," therefore the olive, which signifies the level of *Keter*, is bitter. Now, the Mount of Olives is above even the level of olives. Just as explained above regarding *Keter* in *Keter* and *Chochma* in *Keter* (olive and oil), that the level of *Keter* in *Keter* is distinct in value from the level of *Chochma* in *Keter*, so too is it regarding the value of olives to the Mount of Olives. The level of the Mount of Olives is distinct in value from the level of olives, for the growth of olives from the Mount of Olives is only from the level of the (power of) growth that is in the mountain, but the level of the "inanimate" within the inanimate that is in it, which is the essence of the mountain, is distinct from the olives. As is known regarding growth, that it comes not from the essence of the earth, but only from the level of the "growing" that is in it. Therefore, when one recites the blessing over the fruit of the tree, "Who creates the fruit of the ground," he has fulfilled his obligation, because "it grows from the earth," for the nourishment of the tree's branches is not from the essence of the earth, but only from the power of growth that is in it. From this, we understand the greatness of the wonder of the Mount of Olives, that it is distinct in value even from the level of olives. This is what is meant by "And the Mount of Olives shall be cleft"—that in the future, the level of the Mount of Olives itself will be drawn down, meaning the level of the "inanimate" within the inanimate that is in it. This is the matter of "cleft." It is known and explained regarding the *ushpizin* that Yaakov says, "Then your light shall break forth as the dawn". Even though the light that is drawn through the cleft is very small, nevertheless, the cause of the cleft is from the intensification of the light. As is known from the parable of a barrel that fills up to its brims until it cannot contain the liquid within it, at which point it breaks into clefts and cracks, and the liquid comes out through the clefts. Even though the amount of liquid that comes out through the clefts is very small, this cleft indicates the filling of the barrel, etc. This means that in the light of the cleft, the power of the level of the Essence that is above the level of revelation and its root from there is seen, as explained in the *Siddur Sha'ar HaSukkos*, and even more so here. This is the matter of "And the Mount of Olives itself shall be cleft"—not by way of a *parsah* (the walls of the barrel), etc., but that the level of the Essence of the Mount of Olives will be drawn down, the level of the "inanimate" in it, which is above the level of a cause for olives. This is what is meant by "And the Mount of Olives shall be cleft, etc., toward the east and toward the west," for east and west are "before" and "behind," which is the level of the length that is drawn from above to below, similar to the line of the *Vav* that is drawn from above to below. The manner of extension in general is that as the light goes and is drawn, it diminishes. This is specifically when the beginning of the extension is from a level that belongs to *hishtalshelus*. However, in the future, when there will be the cleaving of the Mount of Olives and it will be drawn from the level of the Essence that is above *hishtalshelus*, then the extension will be "toward the east and toward the west," above and below equally. This is also what is meant by what is said afterward, "and half of the mountain shall move toward the north and half of it toward the south." North and south are the left line and the right line. In the future, when "the Mount of Olives shall be cleft," then "and half, etc., toward the north"—the nullification of the lines from their existence or their inclusion, similar to "there is no left in this *Atik*". This is also why it concludes, "On that day Hashem shall be One and His Name One," and our Sages said, "As I am written, I am read," for since there will then be the extension of the Essence that is above and below equally, therefore it will be below as it is above, "read as it is written." This is also the connection of the two interpretations of "And His feet shall stand on that day upon the Mount of Olives," because from the elevation of the level of "upon the Mount of Olives," from this, there will then be the judgment while standing. The matter of standing signifies the extension from the head to the foot equally, as is known regarding "You stood for them in the time of their distress", that even when, from the perspective of *avodah* (of internal faculties), Israel is in a state of "their distress," which from that perspective makes it a time of distress literally, nevertheless, "In all their distress, He is distressed", and then "You stood for them in the time of their distress," for the standing is what is drawn from the head immediately to the foot. This is also why in the future the judgment will be while standing, for judgment is the matter of the *birurim* (refinements). From the level of "the Mount of Olives shall be cleft" where the Essence will be drawn, when the extension will be "toward the east and toward the west" equally (like the matter of standing, which is drawn from the head to the foot), then there will be judgment among the nations, that the nations will also be refined, etc.

All these matters are drawn down through *avodah*, and so it is also regarding "And His feet shall stand on that day upon the Mount of Olives," which is drawn down through the *avodah* of the level of the feet, as the saying goes, "The feet reach the feet." The matter is that at the time the *Beis Hamikdash* existed, there was a revelation of *Elokus*, and likewise it was even after the destruction, and it remained in the days of Rebbe. In this exile, it is said, "the fallen *Sukkah* of Dovid" —from level to level. Therefore, initially, the essence of the *avodah* and the *birurim* was in the level of *Chabad* (Chochma, Binah, Da'as), which is why the *Tana'im* and *Amora'im* had their main occupation in the study of Torah, unlike the *avodah* of prayer, to the point that there were those who would pray only once every thirty days, etc.. In the time that followed, the essence of the *avodah* was in *Chagas* (Chesed, Gevurah, Tiferes), "there is no service like the service of love", which is the *avodah* of the heart, the *avodah* of prayer. However, in the time of the footsteps of *Moshiach*, where the level of the *Sukkah* of Dovid has fallen to the level of the feet and heels, which is the level of *Asiyah*, now the essence of the *avodah* is in the level of *Nehi* (Netzach, Hod, Yesod), and in the level of action, in the fulfillment of *mitzvos* in practice, and especially in the act of *tzedakah*, which is the totality of all *mitzvos*, as explained in *Iggeres HaKodesh*. This is a great *limud zechus* (defense) for this generation, that all Jews of this generation in all countries excel greatly in giving *tzedakah*, in a way that was not so in previous generations, for through this *avodah* they raise the *Sukkah* of Dovid from its fall, as explained in *Iggeres HaKodesh* there. This is especially relevant to the days of Sukkos, as stated in the Zohar regarding the *ushpizin* and explained in the *Siddur Sha'ar HaSukkos* that one should distribute money to the poor on the eve of Sukkos. Through the *avodah* of *tzedakah* in action—the level of the feet—the feet of the *Nukvah* arouse the feet of the *Dachura*, and through this, "And His feet shall stand on that day upon the Mount of Olives" is drawn down, for "the feet reach the feet," and through this, the Essence that is above the level of revelation is drawn down, similar to what is explained in the *Siddur Sha'ar Lag B'Omer* that specifically in the level of *Hod* of *Hod*, the Essence is drawn down.

This is "Praise Hashem, all you nations; laud Him, all you peoples. For His kindness has prevailed over us, and the truth of Hashem is forever," for the level of His kindness that is above *hishtalshelus*, and how His kindness has prevailed over us, and likewise the level of the truth of Hashem, will be drawn down also into the world, so that it will be "read as it is written," and from this, the nations will also be refined, as mentioned above, that from the revelation of "And His feet shall stand, etc.," there will also be judgment among the nations, which is the matter of *birurim*. This is what is accomplished through the offering of the seventy bulls that are offered on the festival of Sukkos—they subjugate the seventy ministers to holiness, that they are subjugated to Israel, and then "only good" is drawn down to Israel, and the greatness of the joy of "the time of our rejoicing" on the festival of Sukkos, and "whoever has not seen the joy of *Beis HaSho'evah* has never seen joy in his life". Afterward, they come to

## Simchas Torah, 5723

"Torah tzivah lanu Moshe morashah kehilas Yaakov" — "The Torah that Moshe commanded us is an inheritance for the congregation of Yaakov." This implies that the Torah is an inheritance for every member of the congregation of Yaakov. There are well-known difficulties with this: Our Sages stated, "Prepare yourself to study Torah, for it is not an inheritance for you," yet here it says that the Torah is an inheritance. Furthermore, one must understand that the simple meaning of "inheritance" is that one inherits from his ancestors (implying that, in and of himself, he has no connection to the thing, but rather inherited it from his ancestors), yet all of Yisrael were (their souls) present at the giving of the Torah at Sinai and received the Torah themselves. Additionally, one must understand why the subsequent verse states, "And there was a king in Yeshurun, when the heads of the people were gathered, the tribes of Yisrael together." What is the connection between "And there was a king in Yeshurun..." and "an inheritance for the congregation of Yaakov"? Also, in this verse, Yisrael are called by the name "Yaakov" (*morashah kehilas Yaakov*), while in the beginning of the following verse they are called "Yeshurun" (*vayehi biYeshurun melech*), and at the end of that verse they are called "Yisrael" (*yachad shivtei Yisrael*).

2) The point of explanation here (as understood from the *drushim*) is that there are two aspects to the Torah. There is the Torah as it is in itself, which is the wisdom and will of Hashem, and there is the Torah as it descends below, clarifying and refining the world, starting with the clarification and refinement of the person studying it, such that the intellect of the *nefesh habehamis* is clarified, refined, and included in *kedushah*. This is what our Sages meant when they said, "A person should always engage in Torah and *mitzvos* even if not for their own sake (*shelo lishmah*), for from *shelo lishmah* one comes to *lishmah*." At the beginning of *avodah*, a person’s engagement in Torah is for the purpose of clarifying and refining his soul, as our Sages said, "Initially, when a person acts, he acts for his own sake." His Torah study is to refine his soul, and through this, he subsequently arrives at Torah study *lishmah* — for the sake of the Torah itself (which is above the level of clarifications). This is why the Torah is called an "inheritance," yet they said, "Prepare yourself to study Torah, for it is not an inheritance for you." The fact that the Torah is called an "inheritance" is due to the clarifications achieved through the Torah. The *nitzotzos* (sparks) in physical things, as well as the *nitzotzos* in the intellect of the *nefesh habehamis*, have their root in *Tohu*, while the root of the souls of Yisrael is in *Tikkun*. This is why Eisav is called "great" and Yaakov is called "small": the root of Eisav is in *Tohu*, where the *oros* (lights) are abundant ("great"), while the root of Yaakov is in *Tikkun*, where the *oros* are fewer and also limited within *keilim* ("small"). Since Yaakov and Eisav are brothers, as it is written, "Is not Eisav a brother to Yaakov?", and brothers inherit from one another, therefore, by the souls of Yisrael clarifying the *nitzotzos*, they inherit the *oros* of *Tohu*. The statement, "Prepare yourself to study Torah, for it is not an inheritance for you," refers to the Torah as it is above the level of clarifications. The drawing down of this level of Torah is through Torah study *lishmah*. This is "Prepare yourself to study Torah": one must exert himself so that his study will be *lishmah*, without any ulterior motive at all (not even the motive of refining his soul). Through Torah study *lishmah*, he draws down the Torah as it is in itself, a level of Torah that is even above the *oros* of *Tohu* (which Yisrael inherit through the *avodah* of clarifications) — *it is not* an inheritance for you.

3) This is "Yaarof kamatar likchi, tizal katal imrati" — "My teaching shall drop as the rain, my speech shall distill as the dew." Rain comes from clouds formed by the moisture of the earth, "and a mist went up from the earth and watered the whole face of the ground." Dew comes from above. It is the same in spirituality: rain is a matter that comes through clarification, refinement, and the elevation of the lower realms, as our Sages said regarding the verse "My teaching shall drop as the rain," that "when the creatures broke their necks (i.e., humbled themselves), immediately the rain descended." Dew is a revelation from above. The reason the Torah is compared to dew and rain is that there are two aspects to the Torah. There is the aspect of the Torah’s descent below to clarify the world; the drawing down of this aspect of the Torah is through Torah study *al da'as nafshi* (for one's own sake) — "My teaching shall drop as the rain." And there is the Torah as it is in itself (as it is in *Atzilus*, above its descent into *Beriah, Yetzirah, Asiyah*); the drawing down of this aspect of the Torah is through Torah study *lishmah* — "My speech shall distill as the dew." Perhaps it can be said that this is what they meant by "Whoever reads and reviews, Hashem reads and reviews opposite him," for through Torah study *lishmah*, there is a drawing down and revelation of the Torah as it is from above, *Hashem* reads and reviews, the "dew" of Torah.

It can be said that these two aspects (the "rain" of Torah and the "dew" of Torah) are the *nigleh* (revealed) Torah and the *pnimiyus* (inner) Torah. In the *nigleh* of the Torah, which is clothed in physical things, the Divine light is hidden. For the light to be revealed to the person studying the Torah, he must refine himself; this is what is meant by "If he merits, it becomes for him an elixir of life," for through "meriting" (*zachah*), from the root *zichuch* (refinement), it specifically becomes an elixir of life for him. In the *pnimiyus* of the Torah, the Divine light is in revelation. To add: even after a person refines himself, such that the light is revealed even in the *nigleh* of the Torah, it is not like the revelation in the *pnimiyus* of the Torah. Since the light in the *nigleh* of the Torah is clothed in physical things that conceal the light, therefore, even after the removal of the concealment, the light is not as revealed as the light in the *pnimiyus* of the Torah. This is why the *nigleh* of the Torah is the "rain" of Torah, which has two aspects: in the study of *nigleh*, there must be the *avodah* of *zichuch* (refinement), the study *al da'as nafshi* to refine his soul — similar to the matter of "the creatures broke their necks." And even then, the Divine light that shines to the person is as it is clothed in the world — "rain." The matter of the "dew" of Torah is in the *pnimiyus* of the Torah.

4) Now, in addition to the two aforementioned aspects of the Torah (the "inheritance" of Torah and "Prepare yourself to study Torah"), the Torah is also called a "gift." This is the phrasing in the blessing of the Torah, "And He *gave* us His Torah," and we conclude with "Giver of the Torah," and the festival of Shavuos is called the "time of the *giving* of our Torah." The matter of the "gift" in the Torah is even higher than the matter of "Prepare yourself to study Torah." The point is that the difference between dew and rain is that the descent of rain depends on the *avodah* of man (as it is written, "And it shall be if you diligently listen... then I will give the rain of your land..."), while dew does not depend on the *avodah* of man (as our Sages said, "Dew is never withheld"). It is the same in spirituality: rain is the drawing down that comes through *is'arusa d'lesata* (arousal from below), and dew is the drawing down from above, of its own accord. From this, it is understood that what was stated above—that the level of the "dew" of Torah is the drawing down of the Torah as it is above through Torah study *lishmah* ("Whoever reads and reviews, Hashem reads and reviews opposite him")—is in a general sense. But specifically, even the drawing down of the Torah as it is above, where *Hashem* reads and reviews, since the drawing down is through *the person* reading and reviewing, it is included in the category of the "rain" of Torah. The category of the "dew" of Torah is a higher level in the Torah, which is not drawn down through the Torah study of man (not even through Torah study *lishmah*), and its drawing down is of its own accord.

This is what our Sages said: "For the first three hours, Hashem sits and engages in Torah." The well-known difficulty here is: if "whoever reads and reviews, Hashem reads and reviews opposite him," and Yisrael are engaging in Torah throughout the entire day (this one studies at this hour, and that one studies at that hour), then Hashem should also be engaging in Torah throughout the entire day! Yet they said that it is specifically during the first three hours that Hashem sits and engages in Torah. The point of explanation is that the fact that "whoever reads and reviews, Hashem reads and reviews opposite him" is a drawing down from above that comes through *is'arusa d'lesata* (the Torah study of man), and therefore, the drawing down is according to the measure of the *is'arusa d'lesata*. This is why they said "opposite him"—that Hashem reading and reviewing is "opposite him," according to the measure of the person's study. The fact that during the first three hours Hashem sits and engages in Torah is a drawing down from above of its own accord, from a place that *is'arusa d'lesata* cannot reach. This matter is the category of the "dew" of Torah, for the root of the dew is in the three *rishonos* (first ones) of *Atik*. This is the connection between "Hashem sits and engages in Torah" (the dew of Torah) and the first three hours, for the first three [hours] are the three *rishonos* of *Atik*.

It can be explained according to what is known, that even in the *pnimiyus* of the Torah, there is an outer and an inner aspect. The fact that the *pnimiyus* of the Torah speaks of the matters of *hishtalshelus* (the chain of creation) is its *nigleh* (revealed) aspect. [This is similar to the *nigleh* of the Torah, which speaks of the matters of the world, except that the *nigleh* of the Torah speaks of physical matters, and the *pnimiyus* of the Torah speaks of spiritual matters.] This level in the *pnimiyus* of the Torah is at the level of *hasagah* (comprehension). The essential matter of the *pnimiyus* of the Torah is that the Torah is the *taanug* (delight) from above, a delight that is above comprehension. It can be said that the drawing down of the *pnimiyus* of the Torah through Torah study *lishmah*—regarding which they said "whoever reads and reviews, Hashem reads and reviews opposite him" [the matter of "Prepare yourself to study Torah"]—is the *nigleh* of the *pnimiyus* of the Torah, which speaks of the matters of *hishtalshelus* and the like. The drawing down of the *pnimiyus* of the Torah of its own accord, regarding which they said "the first three hours Hashem sits and engages in Torah" [the matter of the "gift" of Torah], is the category of the *taanug* of Torah that is above comprehension.

5) This is "Torah tzivah... morashah kehilas *Yaakov*," "vayehi *biYeshurun* melech... yachad shivtei *Yisrael*." The fact that the Torah is an inheritance for every one of Yisrael is due to the aspect of Yaakov within them (*morashah kehilas Yaakov*). The matter of "Prepare yourself to study Torah, for it is not an inheritance for you" is due to the aspect of Yisrael. And the "gift" of Torah is due to the aspect of Yeshurun.

The matter is that Yaakov is the aspect of a servant, as it is written, "Hear now, Yaakov My servant," and Yisrael is the aspect of a son, as it is written, "Yisrael is My son, My firstborn." The *avodah* of Yaakov is that he is a servant of Hashem, to rectify the concealment that is drawn from the name *Elokim*. Yisrael is named for "for you have struggled with *Elokim*," meaning he rules and dominates over the name *Elokim*. This is what is stated in the *Zohar* that Yisrael and Yaakov correspond to the two names, *Havayah* and *Elokim*. Regarding the aspect of Yaakov (*Yud-Akev*), there is the concealment of the name *Elokim*, and his *avodah* is to rectify that concealment.
