The concept of a month (the birth of the moon) is the drawing down of (new) light from the sun into the moon. It is known that the sun and moon in their root are *Ze'er Anpin* and *Malchus*. *Ze'er Anpin* and *Malchus* are the influencer and the recipient, analogous to the sun and moon. This is the connection of "(tomorrow) is the new moon" to "And Yonasan said to him," for Dovid is the level of *Malchus*, the moon, as it is written, "And Dovid is the youngest," and our Sages say he is called this after the "small luminary," the moon. Yonasan is *Ze'er Anpin*, the level of the sun of *Havayah*, which draws down and influences *Malchus*. This is the meaning of Yonasan being *Yeh-u Natan*—the giving and influencing of the level of *Yeh-u* to *Malchus*. This is the connection of "And Yonasan said to him" (the drawing down from Yonasan to Dovid) with "tomorrow is the new moon," for the concept of a month is the drawing down from *Ze'er Anpin* (sun) to *Malchus* (moon).

**Note:** According to the above, the concept of "And Yonasan said to him" pertains to Rosh Chodesh, when there is the drawing down from *Ze'er Anpin* to *Malchus*, *venifkadta*. From the fact that it says "And Yonasan said to him, 'Tomorrow is the new moon,'" it implies that "And Yonasan said to him" pertains (also) to the concept of "your seat will be empty," which is on Erev Rosh Chodesh. The explanation for this is that in order for there to be the concept of "your seat will be empty," and that through this there will be *venifkadta*, it is through the giving of strength from Above. This is "And Yonasan said to him, 'Tomorrow is the new moon'"; it can be said that the fact that "And Yonasan said to him" is stated before the words "tomorrow is the new moon" is because "And Yonasan said to him" is the drawing down of the giving of strength from *Ze'er Anpin* to *Malchus* regarding the concept of "your seat will be empty" (which is on Erev Rosh Chodesh), and through this there will be *venifkadta* (on Rosh Chodesh). Therefore, "And Yonasan said to him" is stated before "tomorrow is the new moon," for "And Yonasan said to him" is the giving of strength for the concept of "tomorrow is the new moon."

5) This can be connected with what is explained in the *derushim* on the verse, "And these are the generations of Yitzchak, son of Avraham; Avraham begot Yitzchak." One must understand: it was already stated "Yitzchak, son of Avraham," so why is "Avraham begot Yitzchak" repeated? The explanation is that Avraham is the drawing down from Above to below, and Yitzchak is the elevation from below to Above. The elevation from below to Above reaches a place loftier than the place from which the drawing down from Above to below originates. This is analogous to what our Sages say: "If a man seeds first, a female is born; if a woman seeds first, a male is born." The birth that comes through the elevation of *MAN* (elevation from below to Above) is loftier than the birth that comes through the drawing down of *MAD* (drawing down from Above to below). However, first there must be the drawing down from Above to below, and afterwards is the elevation from below to Above. There are two reasons for this: because there cannot be an elevation without the preceding drawing down [analogous to a woman seeding, for even when a woman seeds first, it is through the man arousing her]. Also, the power that exists in the elevation from below to Above to reach a very lofty place ("if a woman seeds first, a male is born"), its drawing down (revelation) is through the drawing down from Above to below that precedes the elevation [analogous to "if a woman seeds and gives birth," for the fact that a birth is produced from the woman's seed is through the man]. It can be said that the fact that the power in the elevation from below to Above to reach a very lofty place is through the drawing down from Above to below [even though the elevation reaches a place loftier than the place from which the drawing down (before the elevation) originates] is because the concept of elevation is the elevation of the *lower being*. Therefore, the power in the elevation to reach very high is in concealment, and through the drawing down from Above (the drawing down of the *Higher Being*), the power in the elevation of the lower being is revealed (and it reaches higher than the place from which the drawing down from Above originates).

This is "Yitzchak, son of Avraham; Avraham begot Yitzchak." "Yitzchak, son of Avraham" (that Yitzchak himself is the son of Avraham) is that the elevation itself comes through the preceding drawing down. "Avraham begot Yitzchak"—*begot* is a transitive verb that gives the power of generation to Yitzchak—is that the power in the elevation of the lower being to reach Above is drawn down (revealed) through the drawing down from Above (before the elevation). It can be said that similarly, it is in "And Yonasan said to him, 'Tomorrow is the new moon'": the drawing down of the giving of strength from *Ze'er Anpin* to *Malchus* regarding the concept of "tomorrow is the new moon," which contains two aspects, analogous to the two aspects in the drawing down from Avraham to Yitzchak. The giving of strength for the concept of "your seat will be empty"—the *bitul* and elevation of *Malchus*—is analogous to "Yitzchak, son of Avraham," and the giving of strength that through "your seat will be empty" there will be *venifkadta*—is analogous to "Avraham begot Yitzchak."

6) Regarding the superiority of the elevation from below to Above over the drawing down from Above to below, there are two aspects. The elevation from below to Above arouses and draws down from a place loftier than the drawing down that comes from Above by itself. [As mentioned above from the *derushim*, the superiority of elevation over drawing down is analogous to "if a woman seeds first, a male is born," where the birth of the love that is in the category of male (*ahavah rabbah*) comes from the *is'arusa d'leila*, but the fact that the *is'arusa d'leila* is drawn down from the interiority of the light (which is why *ahavah rabbah* is born from it) is because its drawing down is through the *is'arusa d'l'tata*.] And the superiority of the elevation itself is that it is loftier even than the drawing down that comes through the elevation. As is explained in the *derushim*, the superiority of *Ohr Chozer* (elevation) is that it returns to the very source of the *Matzil*. Therefore, in the future, it will be said to Yitzchak specifically, "For you are our father," because then the superiority that is in the elevation itself will be revealed.

It can be said that the two aspects are connected to each other. The fact that through the elevation, the drawing down is loftier, is due to the elevation in the elevation itself. However, now, when the primary superiority is in the drawing down from Above, the superiority of the elevation is that the drawing down through it is a loftier drawing down; in the future, the superiority of the elevation itself will be revealed. Based on this, it can be said that the concept of Yitzchak [son of Avraham] is loftier than the concept of [Avraham] begot Yitzchak. The concept of *begot* Yitzchak—the drawing down of the power of generation into Yitzchak, the birth of the love that is in the category of male through the elevation of *MAN* ("if a woman seeds")—is the superiority of the *drawing down* that comes through the elevation. The concept of Yitzchak (son of Avraham) is the superiority of the elevation itself. This is why in "Yitzchak, son of Avraham," Yitzchak is mentioned before Avraham, and in "Avraham begot Yitzchak," Avraham is mentioned before Yitzchak, for in "Avraham *begot* Yitzchak," the primary superiority is in the drawing down, and therefore the beginning and the primary focus is Avraham. In "Yitzchak, son of Avraham" (Yitzchak himself), the primary superiority is in the elevation, and therefore Yitzchak is mentioned first, for he is the primary focus (and afterwards it explains that the way to come to the elevation of Yitzchak is through the drawing down from Above to below, "son of Avraham").

7) Similarly, it is with Yonasan and Dovid, *Ze'er Anpin* and *Malchus*. Now, *Ze'er Anpin* is above *Malchus*, for *Ze'er Anpin* influences *Malchus*. Therefore, the primary superiority is in the drawing down of *Ze'er Anpin* (to *Malchus*), *venifkadta*. The superiority of "your seat will be empty" (the *bitul* and elevation of *Malchus*) is that through it, *venifkadta* is brought about. As is explained in the *derushim*, the elevation of Erev Rosh Chodesh is so that through it there will be the drawing down on Rosh Chodesh. In the future, the level of *Malchus* will be revealed as it is from its own source, so that it will receive from the *Ohr Ein Sof* itself (like *Ze'er Anpin*). As it is written, "And the light of the moon shall be as the light of the sun," that the moon will shine from itself, like the sun. And more than that, afterwards the root of *Malchus* will be revealed, which is above the root of *Ze'er Anpin*, and then *Malchus* will be above *Ze'er Anpin*, "a woman of valor is the crown of her husband." This is what is said (in the *Haftorah* of "tomorrow is the new moon"), "until Dovid became great," that Dovid is above Yonasan.

It is known that the beginning of the elevation of *Malchus* is that it nullifies its reality and becomes a point under the *Yesod* [which in general is the concept of "your seat will be empty"], and through this it becomes a vessel for the elevations that are above this, up to the elevation of "a woman of valor is the crown of her husband." Based on this, it can be said that the fact that the elevation of Erev Rosh Chodesh ("your seat will be empty") is so that through it there will be the drawing down from *Ze'er Anpin* on Rosh Chodesh (*venifkadta*) is for now, but in the future, the superiority of "your seat will be empty" is that through it, the revelation of the root of *Malchus* itself is drawn down.

8) The fact that the sun and moon are *Ze'er Anpin* and *Malchus* is how the sun and moon are in the context of the *Sefiros*. But above that, the sun and moon are the *Ribono shel Olam* (the sun of *Havayah*) and Yisrael (who are likened to the moon). As mentioned above (at the beginning of the maamar) from the *derushim*, "your seat will be empty" (of Dovid, the moon) is the *bitul* of Yisrael, and through this *venifkadta* is brought about, that the *Ribono shel Olam* is, as it were, reminded of them and visits them. It can be said that just as through the "your seat will be empty" of *Malchus*, its own essence and root are revealed—that it does not need to receive from *Ze'er Anpin* (the sun)—and more than that, through this there will be the elevation of *Malchus* above *Ze'er Anpin*, so too is it regarding the "your seat will be empty" of Yisrael: through the *bitul* of Yisrael, their essence is revealed, which is above the aforementioned, that Yisrael and the *Ribono shel Olam* are "all one." Therefore, they do not need, as it were, to receive from the *Ribono shel Olam*, for they are truly one. And more than that, since "You yearn for the work of Your hands," Yisrael are, as it were, influencing the *Ribono shel Olam*, "a woman of valor is the crown of her husband." This is "you will be missed, for your seat will be empty": even though the concept of *zichron* is in the level of *Ohr Ein Sof* where "before Him, all is considered as naught," nevertheless, the *bitul* of Yisrael ("your seat will be empty") arouses this level so that there will be *venifkadta*, for the idea that in order to arouse Above it must be through a person who has value and importance is only when the person arousing is a reality unto himself. Through the *bitul* of "your seat will be empty," the lofty essence of Yisrael is revealed—that they are *one* with the *Ribono shel Olam*, truly one.

9) It was explained above (at the beginning of the maamar) that the concept of "tomorrow is the new moon" is the drawing down of a new light. This can be connected with what is written in the *derushim* of the Tzemach Tzedek that "tomorrow (is the new moon)" refers to the future, analogous to our Sages' saying, "Tomorrow to receive their reward," for in the future there will be a revelation of a new light that did not shine even before the *Tzimtzum*. Through this, there will then be the most lofty innovation in all matters: in Yisrael—who are destined to be renewed like it; in Torah—"a new Torah shall go forth from Me"; and through Yisrael and Torah there will be an innovation also in Creation—"new heavens and a new earth." The verse continues, "So shall your seed and your name stand," that even in the future, when there will be new heavens and a new earth, the *avodah* of Yisrael now, through which the innovation is brought about, will stand and be recognized. The *Ribono shel Olam* will delight with Yisrael, through whom His yearning and desire to have a dwelling place in the lower worlds was fulfilled.

May it be the will that the speaking of these matters, and especially on Shabbos Mevarchim HaChodesh, and even more so on Erev Rosh Chodesh ["tomorrow is the new moon"], will hasten and expedite the revelation of Dovid, King Mashiach—Dovid, King of Yisrael, is alive and enduring—may he come and redeem us and lead us upright to our land, in the true and complete Redemption. And all this in a manner of "immediately," analogous to the birth of the moon which is in a fleeting moment—immediately they are redeemed.

## Shabbos Parshas Haazinu, 13 Tishrei, 5722

(Unedited transcript)

"As an eagle stirs up its nest..." He carries them on His pinions; Hashem alone guides them. This refers to what was said immediately prior: "He guards him as the pupil of His eye." It is through the fact that "He guards him as the pupil of His eye" that "as an eagle stirs up its nest," and therefore "He carries him on His pinions; Hashem alone guides them."

To explain the meaning of "He guards him as the pupil of His eye": It is known that there are Sefiros and angels referred to as "eyes," as explained in *Torah Or* regarding the concept of "the eyes of Hashem roaming to and fro," which are two types of angels. However, Yisrael, who are on a level higher than the ministering angels, are the level of the "pupil of His eye." For while the eye is the vessel for the power of sight, the essence of the sight is the pupil within the eye. This is the superiority of Yisrael over the angels: the angels are generally called "eyes," while Yisrael are the "pupil of His eye."

2) To understand this, and to understand the connection between these matters—that through "He guards him as the pupil of His eye," there follows "as an eagle stirs up its nest," and thereby "He carries him on His pinions; Hashem alone guides them"—we must first introduce the *maamar* of the *Baal HaHillula* on the verse, "You have exalted Hashem today... and Hashem has exalted you today..." He cites the continuation of the verse, "and Hashem has exalted you today... to keep all His mitzvos." The *Ohr HaChaim HaKadosh* questions this: Is this not a condition of the honor that Yisrael inscribed in their service to Hashem, as it is written in the verse, "You have exalted Hashem today... and to keep His statutes and His mitzvos," so what place is there to mention it regarding the things that are from Hashem to Yisrael?

However, the matter is understood by first explaining that "exalted" (*ha'amarta* and *he'emircha*) is a term of praise, as per the teaching of Chazal on this verse: The Holy One, Blessed be He, said to Yisrael, "You have made Me a unique entity in the world, and I will make you a unique entity in the world. You have made Me a unique entity in the world, as it is said, 'Hear O Yisrael, Hashem our G-d, Hashem is One.' And I will make you a unique entity in the world, as it is said, 'And who is like Your people Yisrael, one nation on earth.'"

It should be added that which is explained elsewhere, that *ha'amarta* has three interpretations: First, a term of praise, as mentioned above. Second, a term for a garment (*purpira*), as it is stated in the Midrash on the verse, "He tore His garment (*amarto*)," meaning He tore His *purpira* (garment). Third, a term of speech and utterance.

This is the connection of the verse "You have exalted Hashem today..." to Rosh Hashanah, for it is explained in *Likutei Torah* that "today" without qualification refers to Rosh Hashanah. On Rosh Hashanah, the matter of "You have exalted Hashem today" is accomplished in all three of these interpretations. For the *avodah* of Rosh Hashanah is as Chazal said: The Holy One, Blessed be He, said (to Yisrael), "Say before Me on Rosh Hashanah *Malchiyos*... so that you may crown Me over you." This is the essence of the first interpretation of *ha'amarta*—a term of praise—which is the concept of "You have made Me a unique entity in the world," as it is said, "Hear O Yisrael, Hashem our G-d, Hashem is One"—this is *Malchus*. The second interpretation, a term for a garment, is because when Adam HaRishon was created on Rosh Hashanah, he said, "Hashem is King, He has donned majesty," for He was clothed in a garment of royalty. The third interpretation, a term of speech and utterance, is as is known, that Rosh Hashanah is the building of the world of speech.

The *maamar* continues to explain the interpretation of *ha'amarta* and *he'emircha* as a term of praise, for in both it is said "one" (*Hashem is One*, *one nation*), which has three interpretations. The first, as Chazal said, "One—there is none like Him." One must understand what the praise is in "there is none like Him"—how could it ever cross a person's mind that there is someone like Him, G-d forbid? Even the nations of the world call Him "G-d of gods." If so, why is it necessary to say there is none like Him?

The second interpretation is that "One" negates the concept of *avodah zarah*. As Chazal said, "From where did Yisrael merit the *Krias Shema*? From the Giving of the Torah. The Holy One, Blessed be He, said, 'Hear O Yisrael, I am Hashem your G-d.' Yisrael replied, 'Hashem our G-d.' [He said,] 'You shall have no [other gods]...' Yisrael replied, 'Hashem is One.'" Thus, "Hashem our G-d, Hashem is One" is the acceptance of the two commandments of "I am" and "You shall have no." That is, "One" negates the concept of *avodah zarah*.

The third interpretation is that "Hashem is One" is a mitzvah in its own right: the mitzvah to unify Him. For it is possible to fulfill the command of "I am" and "You shall have no," and yet this is not yet the matter of *yichud* (unification). To this is added the matter of "Hashem is One," which is the command to unify Him, and it is the negation of the concept of *shituf* (partnership/associating others with Hashem). This is what Bnei Yisrael were commanded in the verse "Hear O Yisrael... Hashem is One," unlike the nations of the world, who, although they were commanded regarding *avodah zarah* (as it is written regarding Adam HaRishon, "He commanded," and Chazal said "there is no *tzav* except *avodah zarah*"), were nevertheless not commanded regarding *shituf* (as the Rema writes, following the Ran and Tosafos). That is, among the nations of the world, including the righteous among the nations, there is no concept of the belief in *yichud*, only among Yisrael specifically.

3) The explanation of this: It is written, "High above all nations is Hashem, above the heavens is His glory." This is according to the view of the nations of the world, who think that creation is by way of *hishtalshelus* (chain-like progression) of cause and effect, where the cause and effect are comparable to one another. As explained in *Iggeres HaKodesh*, the effect knows and attains some apprehension of its cause, and even in their essence there is not such a great difference, only that one is the cause and one is the effect. Therefore, they think that because He is high and exalted, His glory is only above the heavens, and not on the earth, since it would be a degradation for Him, may He be blessed, to lower Himself into the lowly effects like the earth and all that is in it, for as long as the chain of cause and effect continues, the cause and effect will be comparable.

But the truth, as Bnei Yisrael know (meaning, this matter is not only a matter of faith, but extends also into understanding and apprehension), is that the creation of the world is not by way of cause and effect, but by way of *yesh me'ayin* (something from nothing), which is the concept of that which is incomparable. This means that even the heavens are a level of degradation, as it is written, "Those who dwell (*yoshvei*) in the heavens," as the Zohar emphasizes: it should have said "He who dwells (*yoshev*)," so why does it say *yoshvei* with an extra *yud*? It is because the *yud* indicates smallness and greater degradation, in addition to the degradation in the general concept of "sitting," like a person who sits, thereby lowering his full stature. And regarding this it is written, "He who is exalted to sit, who lowers Himself to see in the heavens and on the earth"—meaning, because He is exalted to sit, therefore He lowers Himself to see in the heavens and on the earth equally.

From this it is also proven regarding the matter of *hashgacha pratis* (Divine providence): that the providence is not only on the species of man, but also on all the details of the created beings, down to the level of the inanimate, as is known from the teaching of the Baal Shem Tov, transmitted to us by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, Nasi of our generation, that there is *hashgacha pratis* even on a leaf falling from a tree and rolling from place to place—if and in what manner it will roll—and this matter touches upon the general purpose, etc.

The foundation of this is the teaching of Chazal in *Chullin*: "Rabbi Yochanan, when he would see a pelican, would say, 'Your judgments are a great deep.'" Rashi explains that He appointed the pelican to judge and execute His vengeance on the fish of the sea, to kill those destined to die. And so it is in *Midrash Rabbah, Parshas Vayishlach*, that even a bird is not trapped without Heaven, for a heavenly voice goes out and says "dimus" (a term of mercy and rescue) or "spekula" (a term of death and punishment).

However, from the words of the Midrash above, there is not such a strong proof, and the primary proof is from the aforementioned teaching of Chazal in *Chullin*. From this, our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe brought a response to those who say that providence is only on the species of man, for it is explicitly stated that even on the fish of the sea there is *hashgacha pratis* to appoint a pelican to kill those destined to die. And that which Chazal said on the verse, "And you make man like the fish of the sea: just as the fish in the sea, the larger one swallows the smaller one, so too with human beings, were it not for the fear of the government, one would swallow the other alive"—this does not mean that the swallowing of the fish by a larger fish is without law and judgment, but rather like Rashi explained in *Chullin* regarding the pelican. Proof of this is that the Gemara used this to prove that it is the same for the species of man, and in the species of man, everyone agrees there is *hashgacha pratis*. If so, this comes to teach and is found to be taught, that even for the fish of the sea there is *hashgacha pratis*. And that which they said that in order that one should not swallow the other alive, there needs to be the fear of the government (and therefore "pray for the welfare of the government"), even though everything is by providence and law and judgment, this is like what they said, that it is forbidden to stand in a place of danger—a shaky ladder, a shaky wall, and the like—because to be saved from danger, one needs many merits, etc. Likewise, one must negate the danger of "one swallowing the other alive" through prayer for the welfare of the government. However, those who do not believe that the Holy One, Blessed be He, oversees the creatures on earth, but rather left the earth in the hands of the constellations, etc., this is a matter of *avodah zarah*, regarding which even the nations of the world were commanded. Nevertheless, the nations of the world were not commanded regarding *shituf*, only Bnei Yisrael, who were also commanded to unify Him, etc., as above.

The *maamar* continues to explain the negation of the concept of *shituf* with further explanation, based on the teaching of Chazal: "There are three partners in man: the Holy One, Blessed be He, his father, and his mother..." From this it is understood that the concept of *shituf* is not only when one thinks they are equal partners, but even a partnership that is only in some minor detail is considered a matter of *shituf*, regarding which Bnei Yisrael were warned in the command to unify Him. For the three partners in man are not equal partners. Since, in addition to the fact that the father's and mother's portion is only the body (bones, etc., from the father, and flesh, etc., from the mother), while the Holy One, Blessed be He, gives him spirit and soul—which is the essence, for when the soul leaves the body, the father's and mother's portion remains like a silent stone—furthermore, even the shaping of the form of the child from the father's and mother's portion, through the father's drop remaining in the mother's womb for nine months, is not done by the father and mother, by their knowledge and will, but by the Holy One, Blessed be He, alone. As Chazal said on the verse, "There is no Rock like our G-d"—there is no *tzayar* (shaper/artist) like our G-d, who shapes a form within a form, and He does so with the power of "He who performs wonders," etc.

It turns out that even though the father's and mother's portion is very small, they still have a small partnership, for they are possessors of free choice, and if they do not marry, they will not give birth. And since they have a small partnership, they are called partners. From this it is understood regarding the negation of the concept of *shituf*: that not only do we negate an important partnership, as the nations of the world think, who call Him "G-d of gods" (for they too are called by the name *elokim*), but that no concept of partnership is possible, G-d forbid, not even the slightest partnership. For even though the influence below is drawn through the stars and constellations—and especially in the time of exile, when the influence must be through the stars and constellations specifically—nevertheless, it is impossible to call them partners at all, since they have no free choice at all, but rather the bounty that is bestowed by the will of the Creator passes through them, and they themselves are only like an axe in the hand of the one who hews with it, where it is not possible to say that the axe is a partner in the work of hewing, because it has no free choice at all. However, the nations of the world were not commanded regarding *shituf*, and this is because regarding the nations of the world it is written (in the *parsha* of the week), "He set the boundaries of the peoples according to the number of Bnei Yisrael," seventy souls who went out from the thigh of Yaakov, and they receive the influence from the seventy ministers, as it is written, "Which Hashem your G-d has allotted to all the peoples," that He did not prevent them from straying after them. But Bnei Yisrael were commanded regarding *shituf*, to know the truth that everything that happens in the world is only by the will of the Holy One, Blessed be He, and "there is none else besides Him."

4) The matter is, it is written (in the *parsha* of the week), "See now that I, I am He, and there is no G-d with Me..." The Zohar asks: "See that I, I am He" is sufficient; what is "now"? This means that what is said, "I, I am He," the connection of the two aspects of "I," is the concept of the unification of *Havayah* and *Elokim*, for *Havayah* and *Elokim* are all one (as explained at length in previous *maamarim*), and this matter will be in the future to come, as it is written, "And Hashem alone shall be exalted on that day." And what is "See *now*"? It refers also to the present time (in addition to the fact that all matters of Torah are eternal).

The *maamar* continues to explain the difference between *Gan Eden* and the resurrection of the dead, from which it is understood that the primary revelation of "I, I am He" in the future to come itself will be specifically in the resurrection of the dead. Based on this, the statement "See now" becomes even more difficult. The matter is that in *Gan Eden* there is a concept of division, and the truth of the concept of unity does not shine there, only in the resurrection of the dead specifically.

For although the level of *Gan Eden* is very great, in that it does not suffer the existence of evil, and furthermore, it does not suffer the existence of the physical (as explained elsewhere), nevertheless, it is a matter of limitation and division, as is known that the general concept of *Gan Eden* is divided into two aspects: the Lower *Gan Eden* and the Upper *Gan Eden*. And although in detail there are many levels in *Gan Eden*, regarding which Chazal said, "The righteous have no rest... as it is said, 'They go from strength to strength,'" and this is that the Patriarchs and Moshe Rabbeinu have already been in *Gan Eden* for more than three thousand years and are always going from elevation to elevation, nevertheless, in a general way, they are only two aspects, Lower *Gan Eden* and Upper *Gan Eden*, which are two types in *Gan Eden*. And it is understood from this that each of them is called by a special name. For seemingly, since there are many levels in *Gan Eden*, then either way, either all of them are called by one name, *Gan Eden*, and we say that it has many levels, or each level in itself is called by a special name. And since there are many levels in *Gan Eden*, and they are called by two names, Lower *Gan Eden* and Upper *Gan Eden*, it is understood that the general concept of *Gan Eden* is in a manner of division into two levels which are two types in *Gan Eden*.

Unlike the resurrection of the dead, which is one aspect and level. For although in the world of resurrection there are also several differences in levels, as Chazal said, "Each and every one is scorched by the canopy of his neighbor," meaning that the level which is a canopy for one righteous person, which encompasses all his internal powers and he delights in it, etc., his neighbor is scorched by it—for "scorched" is a concept of opposites, etc. If so, there are also in the world of resurrection several differences in levels. Nevertheless, the division is only in individual levels, but in general it is only one aspect and level, unlike *Gan Eden*, which has in its general scope two aspects and levels.

The root of the matter is because in *Gan Eden* only the aspect of the light that fills the worlds (*memalei kol almin*) shines, which has differences in levels, and therefore there are two aspects, Lower *Gan Eden* and Upper *Gan Eden*. And because of this, the order of the ascent of souls in *Gan Eden* is only after the introduction of judgment and justice, etc., as Chazal said, "It is better that he be judged... and come to the World to Come," meaning that in order to come to *Gan Eden*, there must be the introduction of the clarification and refinement in *Gehinnom*, which are very great sufferings, as mentioned in *Iggeres HaTeshuvah* in the name of the Ramban, that even the sufferings of Iyov for seventy years have no value at all to the sufferings of the soul for one hour in *Gehinnom*. And even so, all the sufferings of *Gehinnom* are worthwhile in order to come to the World to Come, because of the greatness of the delight in *Gan Eden*, even the Lower *Gan Eden*. And furthermore, that in order to ascend from Lower *Gan Eden* to Upper *Gan Eden*, there must first be the immersion in the River of Fire, and the ascent on the pillar between Lower *Gan Eden* and Upper *Gan Eden*, as is known in the interpretation of the verse, "And with this the young woman came to the King." And there are even souls that are not appropriate and do not come to Upper *Gan Eden* at all, and all their ascents are in Lower *Gan Eden* alone. And all this is because in *Gan Eden* shines the aspect of *memalei kol almin*, which has limitation and division.

Unlike the resurrection of the dead, which is from the aspect of the Infinite Light that encompasses all worlds (*sovev kol almin*), in which there is no possibility of differences in levels, and therefore also in the world of resurrection there are no differences in levels, and it is one aspect in a general way. And therefore they said, "All Yisrael have a portion in the World to Come" (which refers to the world of resurrection). And this is the matter of "I, I am He" that will be in the future in the resurrection of the dead, that the two aspects of "I" will be connected to be only one level in a general way, because then the truth of the concept of unity will be revealed in a manner of the absence of division. And based on this, the statement "See *now* that I, I am He" becomes even more difficult, for this revelation will be in the future to come in the resurrection of the dead specifically.

It will be understood by first explaining the difference between the *serafim* and the *chayos* and *ofanim*. The *serafim* stand "above Him" and say "Holy...", because their station is in the world of *Beriah*, where the Upper *Imma* (Binah) nests in the Throne, and therefore they attain how the Infinite Light is holy and separate, etc., and therefore there is no concept of "noise" among them, since it comes into their attainment, etc. Unlike the *chayos hakodesh*, whose station is in the world of *Yetzirah*, where the aspect of Binah itself does not shine (as in the world of *Beriah*), but only through its enclothement in the six Sefiros that nest in *Yetzirah*, they do not attain, etc. And because of the lack of attainment due to the concealment and hiding that is in the world of *Yetzirah* relative to the world of *Beriah*, the concept of "noise" is created among them. And especially the *ofanim*, who are lower, as it is written, "One *ofan* on the earth," and its translation is "from below," and it is explained in *Likutei Torah* (in the *parsha* of the week) that this is the root and source through which, by way of the multitude of progression, the aspect of evil is formed from it. Thus, among them the noise is even greater.

And "noise" (*ra'ash*) is the letters of *se'ar* (with a *sin* on the left) and *sha'ar* (with a *shin* on the right). *Se'ar* (on the left) is the concept of hair, which indicates a diminution of vitality through the multitude of *tzimtzumim* (contractions), to such an extent that even when one cuts the hair, one does not feel pain. It is known that the opposite of delight is felt more than the matter of delight, and therefore, from the fact that one does not feel pain when cutting hair, it is proven all the more the greatness of the contraction of vitality in the hair. And *sha'ar* (on the right) is as it is written, "This is the gate (*sha'ar*) to Hashem," like the example of a wide gate (not just a short path) that serves for entry and exit.

This is the matter of *ra'ash* being the letters of *se'ar* and the letters of *sha'ar* in the service of man: when a person contemplates that the general Divine light enclothed in the worlds is a very contracted light (for example, the hair), and in the aspect of the light enclothed in the worlds themselves there are many contractions down to the aspect of "one *ofan* on the earth," which is the root of the existence of evil (as above), and down to the multitude of contractions so that the vitality is drawn to the lowest place, lower even than the revelation in the aspect of the *ofanim*—through this contemplation, a great noise is created (even greater than the noise in the aspect of the *ofanim*), and through this, a wide *sha'ar* (gate) is created for all the elevations and influences.

This is also the matter of the Torah that descended below and was enclothed in human intellect, that this matter was done through the multitude of contractions, which is the concept of hair (*se'ar* on the left). And regarding this it is written, "His locks are curled," heaps upon heaps of *halachos*, which refers to the *halachos* of the Torah, of which it is written, "Exalt her and she will raise you up," that through the clarification and "curling" (refinement) of the hair to separate the *halachos* so that there will be no contradiction from one to the other, through this the soul is raised. This means that specifically through the study of the *halachos* of the Torah as they descended and were enclothed in physicality through the multitude of contractions, which is the concept of hair (*se'ar* on the left), a wide *sha'ar* (gate) is created for all the elevations and influences, etc.

However, the essence of the matter of the wide *sha'ar* is created through the *avodah* of *teshuvah*. For the *avodah* of fulfilling the Torah and mitzvos that are enclothed in physical things is in a manner where the physical things are only the *root* of the existence of evil (as above in the interpretation of "one *ofan* on the earth," that its translation is "from below," which is only the root of the existence of evil). Unlike the *avodah* of *teshuvah*, which is after one has sinned and blemished and crossed the path, etc., which is an existence of actual evil that fell in the breaking of the vessels, etc. Since the contraction (the matter of hair, *se'ar* on the left) is greater, the noise created through this is also greater, and consequently, a wider *sha'ar* (gate) is created through this.

Based on this, it will be understood what is written, "See now," etc. For although the revelation of the *Sovev* (Encompassing Light), which is the light of the Infinite (through which the truth of the concept of unity, "I, I am He," is created), will be in the future to come in the resurrection of the dead, nevertheless, this revelation is created through the *avodah* in this time specifically. As our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe said, that all the revelations of the days of Moshiach and the resurrection of the dead depend on our deeds and our *avodah* throughout the duration of the exile. And therefore it is said, "See now," etc., for because of the *avodah* of Torah and mitzvos that are enclothed in physical things, and especially because of the *avodah* of *teshuvah*, there is even now a taste of the revelation of the future to come. And in this, it is also understood what is written, "You have exalted Hashem today... and Hashem has exalted you today... to keep all His mitzvos."

5) This is what is written, "As an eagle stirs up its nest," which is the arousal of great mercy of the aspect of the eagle, which comes after the introduction of the *avodah* of the month of Elul, which is *avodah* from below to above, like the acronym of Elul, "I am to my Beloved and my Beloved is to me," except that there is a granting of strength from above for this, but the *avodah* itself is in a manner of from below to above. In general, this is the *avodah* of *teshuvah*, which is *avodah* from below to above. This is "He guards him as the pupil of His eye." The pupil of the eye is the black in the eye, and nevertheless, the essence of the power of sight is specifically through the pupil. Similarly, it is understood in the *avodah* of man, that specifically through the *avodah* of *teshuvah* in a manner of from below to above, which is the concept of blackness and concealment, through the *se'ar* (with a *sin* on the left), a wide influence is created to arouse great mercy from the aspect of the "Father of Mercy," to draw down and repair all the blemishes that one blemished in the positive and negative commandments.

And this is the continuation of the verses: "He guards him as the pupil of His eye, as an eagle stirs up its nest, He carries him on His pinions; Hashem alone guides them." That through the *avodah* of the pupil of the eye, which is the *avodah* of *teshuvah* as above, through this "as an eagle stirs up its nest," which is the concept of drawing down mercy to fill all the blemishes, both in positive commandments—which is the concept of "He carries him on His pinions," which refers to the 248 commandments, the 248 limbs of the King—and in negative commandments—which is the concept of "Hashem alone guides them" (also, the name *Havayah* itself includes positive and negative commandments; *Vav-Hei* is positive commandments and *Yud-Hei* is negative commandments). All these matters are repaired through the drawing down of mercy that comes through the *avodah* of *teshuvah*, which is the concept of the pupil of the eye.

In truth, according to what was explained above regarding the wide *sha'ar* that is created specifically through the hair, through the *avodah* of the pupil of the eye, not only are all the blemishes filled, but through the *avodah* of *teshuvah*, a light is drawn down that is higher than the light drawn down through the *avodah* of Torah and mitzvos, which is why even the righteous need the *avodah* of *teshuvah*. This is the general concept of the *avodah* of the month of Elul in a manner of from below to above, through which the drawing down of the Thirteen Attributes of Mercy is created on Yom Kippur in forgiveness and pardon for all the blemishes. And not only are the blemishes filled, but a higher light is drawn down, which is what we say at the conclusion of the *Ne'ilah* prayer, "Hear O Yisrael," etc., and we conclude, "For the next year in Jerusalem," in the true and complete redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, for Moshiach comes to return the righteous in *teshuvah*, which is the drawing down of the aspect of the wide *sha'ar* even in the righteous.

## With the help of Hashem. The eve of 13 Tishrei, the Hillula of the Rebbe Maharash, 5748.

### With the help of Hashem. The eve of 13 Tishrei, the Hillula of the Rebbe Maharash, 5748.

"I create the fruit of the lips; peace, peace to him who is far and to him who is near, says Hashem, and I will heal him." My revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, notes in his maamar on this verse, delivered on 13 Tishrei fifty years ago, that "fruit of the lips" (*niv sefaseim*) refers to speech that comes about automatically. [The implication of the phrase *niv sefaseim* is that the *niv* (speech) comes from the lips themselves—meaning the speech is not the result of thought and intention, but comes about of its own accord.] What is the superiority of speech that comes about automatically? Furthermore, the verse uses the term *borei* (I create) the fruit of the lips. The meaning of *briah* (creation) is *chiddush*—the bringing into existence of something new that did not exist previously, *similar* to the *chiddush* of *yesh me'ayin* (something from nothing). [Although the term *briah* also applies to the formation of *yesh mi'yesh* (something from something)—as is understood from the fact that everything that came into being during the six days of creation (including the specific entities that were formed after the first day, even though they were formed from the heaven and earth that were created on the first day) is called a *briah*—as it is written, "And Elokim *created* (*vayivra*) man," even though man's body was formed from dust. Similarly, regarding the soul of man, it is written, "And He breathed into his nostrils the soul of life." Breathing is not a *briah* of *yesh me'ayin*, but rather the extension of a breath that existed previously—*yesh mi'yesh*. (It should be noted that the *yesh* from which the soul was formed is the true *yesh*, for "he who breathes, breathes from within himself," and his "within" (his essence and inwardness) is the true *yesh*.) Thus, the term *briah* refers to *yesh mi'yesh* only when the formation is in a manner of *chiddush*, and even to the extent of a *chiddush* that is *similar* to the *chiddush* of *yesh me'ayin*.] From the fact that it says *borei* (I create) the fruit of the lips—that *niv sefaseim* is a *chiddush*—it is proven that the superiority of *niv sefaseim* (speech that comes about automatically) over speech that comes through thought and intention is a superiority that is incomparable (a *chiddush*). This requires understanding: seemingly, speech that comes through thought and intention is (in simple terms) superior to speech that comes about automatically. Yet, from the fact that it says *borei niv sefaseim*, it is proven that speech that comes about automatically is superior, to the point of an incomparable superiority.

2) The maamar continues by noting that in the Radak’s commentary on this verse, there are two interpretations of "to him who is far and to him who is near." The Radak himself explains "far" as far from Jerusalem and "near" as near to it. Subsequently, he brings that our Sages explained that "far" refers to *ba'alei teshuvah* (penitents) and "near" refers to *tzaddikim*. As it is stated in the Gemara, "In the place where *ba'alei teshuvah* stand, even perfect *tzaddikim* cannot stand," as it is said, "Peace, peace to him who is far and to him who is near"—"far" is mentioned first, and then "near." It must be understood: from the fact that the maamar prioritizes the Radak’s interpretation before the Gemara’s, it implies that the Radak’s interpretation is more relevant to the subject matter of the maamar. This requires explanation, as the maamar discusses the superiority of the *ba'al teshuvah* over the *tzaddik* (as will be explained later). Why, then, does it prioritize the Radak’s interpretation—that "far" means far from Jerusalem—over the Gemara’s interpretation—that "far" means *ba'alei teshuvah*?

The reason for the greatness of the *ba'alei teshuvah* over the *tzaddikim* is (as brought in the maamar) because the *avodah* of the *ba'alei teshuvah* is with *chila yatir* (greater strength). The maamar notes that this *chila yatir* in the *avodah* of the *ba'alei teshuvah* is due to their distance—because they were initially distant from Elokus. Why, then, should their merit be greater than that of the *tzaddikim* who were always close to Elokus? It also needs to be understood (as the maamar notes) that after saying "Peace, peace to him who is far and to him who is near," the verse continues, "says Hashem, and I will heal him." Seemingly, since the "far" one has become "near" and has achieved *peace*, why is healing necessary?

3) To explain this, the maamar introduces the verse, "Everyone who is called by My name, and for My glory I have created him, I have formed him, and also I have made him." "Created, formed, and made" correspond to the three worlds of *Beriah, Yetzirah,* and *Asiyah*, which encompass all the worlds. It explains that the connection of the worlds to *Ohr Ein Sof* (from bottom to top) and the extension of *Ohr Ein Sof* into the worlds (from top to bottom) is through Torah. This is the meaning of "for My glory I have created him, I have formed him, and also I have made him"—the connection of the worlds to *Ohr Ein Sof* and the extension of *Ohr Ein Sof* into the worlds is through "My glory," for there is no glory other than Torah. The maamar continues that the connection of the worlds to *Ohr Ein Sof* and the extension of *Ohr Ein Sof* into the worlds is through three things: Torah, *avodah*, and *gemilus chasadim*. The reason for this is that the three worlds of *Beriah, Yetzirah,* and *Asiyah* correspond to thought, speech, and action. Therefore, the connection of the three worlds to *Ohr Ein Sof*, as well as the extension of *Ohr Ein Sof* into the three worlds, is through Torah, *avodah*, and *gemilus chasadim*, because these three things are thought, speech, and action [*avodah*—thought; Torah—speech; *gemilus chasadim*—action]. The reason it says "for My glory" regarding "created, formed, and made"—where "My glory" is Torah—is because the two other matters, *avodah* and *gemilus chasadim*, must also be according to Torah.

Similarly, this applies to the creation of man and the descent of his soul below. The descent is through the three worlds of *Beriah, Yetzirah,* and *Asiyah*: "You created (*Beriah*), You formed (*Yetzirah*), You breathed into me (*Asiyah*)." The connection of man to *Ohr Ein Sof* and the extension of *Ohr Ein Sof* into man are through his *avodah* in the three areas of Torah, *avodah*, and *gemilus chasadim*. Through this, the return of the soul to its source in the essence of *Ohr Ein Sof* is accomplished. For in addition to being elevated to the level of *tehorah hi* (*Atzilus*, as it was before the descent of "You created"), it is elevated to its primary source in the essence of *Ohr Ein Sof*, and "the spirit returns to Elokim who gave it." That is to say, even while it is below, clothed in a physical body, it is unified with Him, may He be blessed, in the ultimate unification, just as it was included in the essence of *Ohr Ein Sof* (before the descent of "and He breathed").

4) This matter of "and the spirit returns to Elokim who gave it" [that even when the soul is below, it is unified with Him, may He be blessed, just as it was included in the essence of *Ohr Ein Sof*] is primarily through the *avodah* of *teshuvah*. It can be said that this is also hinted at in the language of the verse, "and the spirit will return (*tashuv*)..."—*tashuv* from the word *teshuvah*—because the *essence* of the soul's return to its source (to the Elokim who gave it) is through the *avodah* of *teshuvah*. The concept is (as explained in the maamar) that the *avodah* of the *tzaddikim* (Torah, *avodah*, and *gemilus chasadim*) is within the realm of measurement and limitation, whereas the *avodah* of *teshuvah* is above measurement and limitation. This is why *teshuvah* can happen in a single moment. Therefore, the return of the soul to its source through *teshuvah* is a higher return than the return through Torah, *avodah*, and *gemilus chasadim* (the *avodah* of the *tzaddikim*). This is "Peace, peace to him who is far and to him who is near"—"far" is mentioned first, and then "near"—because the peace achieved through the *avodah* of *teshuvah* (the "far" one who has been brought near) is higher than the peace through the *avodah* of the *tzaddikim* (the "near" one).

This is also why the verse continues, "says Hashem, and I will heal him," because the superiority of the *avodah* of *teshuvah* (peace for the "far" one) is that *teshuvah* is like a medicine ("and I will heal him"). The concept is (as explained in the maamar) that with food and drink, a large quantity is needed to strengthen the connection of the soul to the limbs of the body. Not so with medicine; even a single drop of medicinal ingredients has the power to strengthen the connection of the soul to the body. The reason for this is that the power in medicinal ingredients is not as limited as the power in food, and therefore it does not depend as much on quantity. Similarly, with *teshuvah*, which is compared to medicine—the reason *teshuvah* is effective in a single moment, to the point that through one thought of *teshuvah* one is transformed from a completely wicked person to a completely righteous person, is because *teshuvah* is above limitation. Based on this, we can explain the phrase "says Hashem, and I will heal him." The reason it says "says Hashem" regarding both medicine and *teshuvah* (even though the operation of all things is not by their own power but by the power of the Holy One, blessed be He) is because medicine and *teshuvah* involve a matter that is above limitation [which is why *teshuvah* is in a single moment, and similarly, medicine in its simple sense can be effective even through a single drop], and this matter is through the granting of a special power from above: "says Hashem."

5) It should be added [especially since this matter (below) is something the generation needs, and many are in need of it] that the meaning of "and I will heal him" is that "I (Hashem) will heal." That is to say, in addition to the fact that healing in general (even when the healing is through a messenger, a human being) is through "says Hashem," healing is also drawn from the Holy One, blessed be He, Himself: "I, Hashem, am your healer." Therefore, it is in a manner of "all the diseases... I will not place upon you" (from the outset). Furthermore, even when the healing of "I, Hashem, am your healer" comes after an undesirable event occurred due to various causes, the healing is in such a way that no trace remains, and it is even uprooted *retroactively*. For healing through a human being, in addition to the fact that it only heals from that point forward, also leaves a trace afterward. Through the healing of "I, Hashem, am your healer," it is uprooted from its beginning.

6) The maamar continues that the reason "says Hashem, and I will heal him" is stated *after* saying "peace to him who is far" is because, since he was initially distant from Elokus, therefore, even after he becomes close to Elokus (through *teshuvah*, the *avodah* of man), and even after "peace" is made for the "far" one (through an extension from above), he still needs healing. The healing is through the study of Torah. This is "says Hashem, and I will heal him"—the healing is "says Hashem," which engraves the letters of the Torah into the power of his memory.

**It should be noted** that what is said in the maamar—that "I will heal him" is through the study of Torah—also applies to healing in the physical sense, as it is written, "and healing for all his flesh." Based on this, it can be said that what was mentioned above (Section 5)—that the healing of "says Hashem, and I will heal him" is in a manner where no trace remains and it is uprooted from its beginning—is true even when the healing from the Holy One, blessed be He, is drawn through a human doctor and through medicinal ingredients. Since the Torah is the "master of the house" over all of reality, including the reality of healing (the science of medicine, etc., and even the very existence of healing), it is understood that through the power of the Torah, even healing through human beings and through medicinal ingredients can be in a manner where no trace remains and it is uprooted from its beginning. It should be noted that even in healing according to nature, there is healing that uproots the illness. However, this healing is not common, and the common medicines are in a manner where a trace remains. Based on this, it is even more understood that it is within the power of the Torah to cause even the healing through common medicines to be in a manner where no trace remains, since this phenomenon already exists in the world.

7) The maamar continues with the interpretation of the verse, "I create the fruit of the lips; peace... to him who is far," that the advice and remedy for the "far" one, the *ba'al teshuvah*, is *niv sefaseim*—confession of words. It is called *niv sefaseim*—speech that comes about automatically—because the confession of the *ba'al teshuvah* comes about automatically, from the pain of the depths of his heart. Now, this interpretation—that *niv sefaseim* is confession—is according to the Gemara’s interpretation that "peace to him who is far" refers to a *ba'al teshuvah*. Regarding the Radak’s interpretation that "peace to him who is far" refers to those far from Jerusalem, the maamar explains that Jerusalem is the concept of fear of Heaven, and "far from Jerusalem" is one who by nature is not a fearer of Heaven. The advice for this is *niv sefaseim*—speech of Torah words. It can be said that the fact that speech of Torah is called in this verse by the name *niv sefaseim*—speech that comes about automatically—is to hint that a person must be so immersed in Torah that the speech of Torah becomes "automatic" (similar to the Sages' statement, "it came from itself"). This can be connected with what was brought above from the maamar: that the concept of "says Hashem, and I will heal him" is that the words of Torah should be *engraved* in the power of his memory. The concept of engraving is that the thing being engraved becomes one with the thing it is being engraved upon. Through becoming one with the Torah, so that his existence is Torah, all his speech—even when he is not intending to speak words of Torah—is words of Torah. This is "I *create* the fruit of the lips"—that the *niv sefaseim* of the person (the speech that comes of itself, without intention) is words of Torah, which is through his becoming a new existence, an existence of Torah.

**Based on this**, we can explain why the maamar brings the Radak’s interpretation before the Gemara’s: because the superiority of *niv sefaseim* (the first point in the maamar) is more relevant to the Radak’s interpretation. According to the Gemara’s interpretation that "far" is *ba'alei teshuvah*, *niv sefaseim* is confession. But the fact that *niv sefaseim* is the speech of Torah is according to the Radak’s interpretation that "far" is far from Jerusalem. And the primary superiority of *niv sefaseim* (speech that comes about automatically) is when the speech of Torah is in an automatic way, which indicates that the person has become a new existence, an existence of Torah.

8) **It should be added** that because my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, the Nasi of our generation, said the maamar "I create the fruit of the lips," and especially because he *published* the maamar, the power is given to every Jew, and especially to those who follow in his footsteps, that their Torah study—and similarly their engagement in all the matters of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, the Nasi of our generation—should be in a manner of *niv sefaseim*. That is, they should be completely immersed in the matters of the Nasi of our generation, to the point that all their actions (even those that come about automatically, without intention) will be in the matters of the Nasi of our generation. And in a manner of "ascending in holiness," that every day the engagement in the matters of the Nasi should be with new vitality and in a renewed manner, and even to the point of a complete *chiddush*, similar to the *chiddush* of *yesh me'ayin*—"I *create* the fruit of the lips." Especially regarding Torah study, as *niv sefaseim* refers to the speech of Torah (as mentioned above), the increase in Torah study should be incomparable. Especially after fifty years have passed since the delivery of the aforementioned maamar, for "there is no world but fifty years," and after that, a new world begins. It should be noted that the superiority of fifty years relates to matters of wisdom and intellect, as the Sages say, "fifty years for counsel." From this, it is understood that the fact that a new world begins after fifty years from the delivery of the aforementioned maamar is primarily regarding the study of Torah in understanding and comprehension. The increase and elevation in the understanding and comprehension of Torah will be in an incomparable manner. Similar to the elevation of Rabbi Zeira from the Babylonian Talmud to the Jerusalem Talmud.

**It should be added** that although for all matters to be in a manner of a "new world" it is through *avodah* and toil, and there are those who think and imagine that they do not want to toil to become a new existence and that their conduct from now on will be as it was before, they must know that their *ainredenish* (self-persuasion) that they will remain in the previous state will not help at all, for "the word of our G-d shall stand forever." And what they think regarding their will (that they do not want to toil to become a new existence) is only an imagination, for the true will of every Jew is to fulfill the will of Hashem, as the famous ruling of the Rambam states. When they explain to themselves what their true will is, the will will be revealed, and their *avodah* in the above will be out of will and *taanug*.

**Through** the toil in all the above, the success in this will be in a manner of "finding"—"I toiled and I found"—until they arrive at the primary "finding": "I have found David My servant," which refers to *Mashiach Tzidkeinu*, who comes when one is distracted. My revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, the Nasi of our generation, explained that this is not a contradiction to the fact that one must demand the coming of *Mashiach Tzidkeinu*, speak about the coming of *Mashiach*, write about this matter, and make noise about it. On the contrary, this is the true "distraction" that brings the coming of *Mashiach* closer, below ten handbreadths, and in a manner that "it did not delay even for the blink of an eye," immediately and truly.

## Simchas Torah 5742

To understand the concept of Simchas Torah, we must recall the well-known points made in the maamar of the Rebbe Rayatz regarding the nature of this joy. The manner of celebrating Simchas Torah is to take a Sefer Torah, wrapped and covered in its mantle, and circle the reading table with it; only after circling the table do we dance with the Torah. Although it is written regarding the Torah, "For it is your wisdom and your understanding," nevertheless, the joy of Simchas Torah is not achieved through study, analysis, or intellectual comprehension, but specifically through dancing with one’s feet. This concept—that Simchas Torah is associated specifically with the feet—applies not only to the person (whose joy is expressed through dancing with the feet) but also to the Torah itself. The joy of Simchas Torah is the joy of Yisrael with the *letters* of the Torah, as is evident from the fact that the celebration involves circling the reading table where, throughout the year, we read the letters of the Torah. It is known that among the four levels of the Torah (TaNTa), the letters are the lowest level, the "feet" of the Torah. This focus on the letters is also understood from the fact that this day is called "Simchas Torah" because we conclude the Torah on this day, and it is fitting to rejoice in its completion; for the completion of the Torah on this day refers to the completion of the reading of the letters of the Torah. Regarding the understanding and comprehension of Torah, there is no such thing as a "conclusion," for "its measure is longer than the earth and wider than the sea," and it is written, "and the maidens are without number—these are the halachos." Even though the halachos that have already been revealed are finite—"sixty are the queens" (the sixty tractates) and "eighty are the concubines" (the eighty braisos)—there is no end or limit to the halachos that are yet to be innovated. These, too, were given at Sinai, as the saying goes, "Everything that a veteran student will eventually innovate was already given to Moshe at Sinai." From this, it is understood simply that the "conclusion" of the Torah on Simchas Torah refers only to the completion of the letters of the Torah. Furthermore, regarding the reading of the Torah, intellectual comprehension is not a prerequisite, for even an unlearned person who does not know what is being said is called to the Torah and recites the blessing. From all this, it is understood that the joy of Simchas Torah—the joy of completing the Torah—is the joy of the letters of the Torah, the "feet" of the Torah. This is why, for the person as well, the joy is expressed specifically through dancing with the feet. We must also understand (as the aforementioned maamar points out) why Simchas Torah was established on Shemini Atzeres; seemingly, it should have been on Shavuos, the time of the giving of our Torah. Why is it celebrated on Shemini Atzeres?

2. This point—why Simchas Torah is celebrated on Shemini Atzeres and not on Shavuos—is addressed in the aforementioned maamar, following the observation that regarding Shemini Atzeres it is written, "The eighth day shall be an Atzeres for you." Since all the festivals and holidays (as well as all the Torah and mitzvos) were given to Yisrael, why does the Torah specifically say "for you" regarding Shemini Atzeres? What is the unique innovation of Shemini Atzeres compared to all other festivals? The maamar cites the Midrash: "The Atzeres of the festival (Sukkos) should have been fifty days away, like the Atzeres of Pesach (Shavuos)." By citing this Midrash, the difficulty is sharpened: if Shemini Atzeres is analogous to Shavuos, why is Simchas Torah celebrated on Shemini Atzeres rather than Shavuos? The Midrash explains why the Atzeres of the festival is not fifty days away: because they are transitioning from summer to winter, "it is not in their power to come and go." It is like a king who had many daughters; some were married nearby and some far away. One day, they all came to greet their father the king. The king said, "Those who are married nearby have the capacity to come and go, but those married far away do not. Until they are all here, let us make one festival and rejoice together." So it is with the Atzeres of Pesach—as they are transitioning from winter to summer, Hashem says, "It is in their power to come and go." But regarding the Atzeres of the festival, because they are transitioning from summer to winter, Hashem says, "It is not in their power to come and go; rather, while they are all here, let us make one festival and rejoice." Therefore, Moshe warns Yisrael, "The eighth day shall be an Atzeres for you."

3. The core of this explanation is that "near" and "far" refer to *tzaddikim* and *baalei teshuvah*. As it is written, "Peace, peace to the far and to the near." It is known that "peace to the near" refers to *tzaddikim*, and "peace to the far" refers to *baalei teshuvah*—the one who was far and has become near. Through *teshuvah*—the drawing near that follows distance—one draws from a higher source, a level of "far" (above the *seder hishtalshelus*), a level of concealment that is not within the realm of existence. Therefore, in the place where *baalei teshuvah* stand, even perfect *tzaddikim* cannot stand. Although *tzaddikim* also perform the *avodah* of *teshuvah* (since *teshuvah* is a mitzvah for everyone), the virtue of *teshuvah* is that it operates through a "leap." This is true of *teshuvah* in its simple sense. When one performs *teshuvah* because it is a *mitzvah* to do so, the *teshuvah* follows a set order and progression. The aspect of the "leap" in *teshuvah* is primarily found in simple *teshuvah*, among those who were initially in a "far" place. Even though *tzaddikim* also feel a sense of distance—for they, too, have contemplation of the greatness of Hashem and the lowliness of man—it is understood that the sense of distance arising from this contemplation is not comparable to the sense of distance of "evil and bitter is your forsaking of Hashem your G-d." Since *baalei teshuvah* were initially in a very far place, at the very lowest depths, their *avodah* is with greater intensity, with a storm, with greater strength. Through this, they reach the highest place. This is especially true according to the teachings of the Arizal, that a person must elevate the *domem, tzomei'ach, chai,* and *medabeir* because the sparks within them are higher than the sparks within the *medabeir* (humanity). Everything that is highest, when it descends, falls to the lowest depths. From this, it is understood *a fortiori* regarding the matters (sins) that are the subject of simple *teshuvah*: the sparks within them are of the highest level, which is why they fell to the lowest depths. This is also one of the reasons why simple *teshuvah* is performed with the internal and hidden powers of the *nefesh*, reaching even to the "inner voice that is not heard," for to clarify and elevate these sparks—which are the highest and fell the lowest—one must use the internal and hidden powers, the level of concealment that is not within existence.

4. This is the parable of the king who had many daughters, some married nearby and some far away. The "daughters" are the *neshamos* clothed in a body. The daughters are "married"—the descent of the *neshamos* below to be clothed in a body is to make for Him a dwelling place in the lower worlds through the fulfillment of Torah and *mitzvos*, beginning with the first mitzvah, "Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth and conquer it." This refers to being fruitful and multiplying in spirituality (an addition of light through the *avodah* of Torah and *mitzvos* in general) and also in the literal sense, "He created it to be inhabited," through which the world becomes a dwelling place for Him. This is the concept of "married daughters"—the marriage is for the sake of being fruitful and multiplying, both spiritually and physically, so that there will be a drawing down and revelation of the power of the *Ein Sof* below. Yet, within this, there are those married "nearby"—the *avodah* of *tzaddikim* who occupy themselves with Torah and *mitzvos*—and those married "far away"—the *avodah* of *birurim* (refining) in mundane matters, and especially the *avodah* of *teshuvah*. The king says that those married nearby have the capacity to come and go, while those married far away do not. *Tzaddikim*, since they did not descend as far and are in a "near" place, do not require special effort or urgency; it can take time ("they have the capacity"), meaning their *avodah* is in a set order and progression. *Baalei teshuvah*, however, since they descended to the lowest depths and were in a "far" place—and even after *teshuvah* there is a concern they might return to their old ways—require effort and urgency and cannot wait ("it is not in their power"). Their *avodah* is by way of a "leap," outside of order and progression. Even in terms of time, the *avodah* of *teshuvah* is "in one hour and in one moment"—one acquires his world in a single hour.

Although the fact that *baalei teshuvah* require special effort and urgency is due to their deficiency—because they were in a far place—nevertheless, this very fact (that they were in a far place) awakens in them a "distance for the sake of elevation." *Tzaddikim* who were in a "near" place perform their *avodah* with the "near" powers of the *nefesh* (the revealed powers). Their *avodah* itself—their daily ascent—is in a state of closeness, in order and progression (meaning each level is relative and close to the previous one), and the place (the level above) they reach is also a "near" place, a concealment that exists within reality. *Baalei teshuvah*, however, who were in a "far" place, perform their *avodah* with the "far" and hidden powers of the *nefesh*, reaching to the essential powers. The fact that they were initially in a far place awakens the "far" powers of the *nefesh*. Their *avodah* itself is in a state of distance and "leaping," not in order and progression (the level they ascend to is distant and not at all comparable to their previous level), and the place they reach is a "far" place, a concealment that is not within reality.

5. This is the difference between the Atzeres of Pesach and the Atzeres of the festival. The Atzeres of Pesach is the concept of the giving of the Torah (the first Tablets), which follows the exodus from Egypt: "When you take the people out of Egypt, you shall serve G-d on this mountain." This is the *avodah* of *tzaddikim*, for Yisrael were then (at the time of the exodus) in the state of *tzaddikim*, "as G-d made man upright." Therefore, all matters from the exodus until the giving of the Torah follow a set order and progression: first the exodus, then the days of *Sefiras HaOmer* (the forty-nine gates of holiness and the fiftieth gate as it relates to the forty-nine), leading up to the drawing down of the fiftieth gate as it is in itself, as explained in the discourses on the giving of the Torah. The Atzeres of the festival, however, comes in the wake of the *teshuvah* of the month of Elul, the days of *Selichos*, *Rosh Hashanah*, the Ten Days of *Teshuvah*, and *Yom Kippur*. This is also why the joy of Simchas Torah (which was established on Shemini Atzeres) is over the second Tablets, which were given on *Yom Kippur*—the concept of *teshuvah*. This is also the reason for the great joy of Shemini Atzeres and Simchas Torah, for the concept of *teshuvah* is "peace to the far who has become near," and the joy of the closeness that follows distance is the greatest joy.

6. This is also why on the Atzeres of Pesach we transition from winter to summer, and on the Atzeres of the festival we transition from summer to winter. The fact that walking during the winter days is harder than during the summer days is because that is how it is in the spiritual realm (as all physical matters are derived from spiritual ones). In the summer, the sun is in its strength, which is because it is so in the spiritual realm: the aspect of the "Sun of Hashem" shines then in revelation. Even though the "shield and casing" of the Name *Elokim* exists then, it does not conceal and hide as much, and therefore walking in the path of Torah and *mitzvos* is easier—they are "married nearby." In the winter days, however, the shield and casing of the Name *Elokim* conceals and hides to a greater degree, and therefore walking in the path of Torah and *mitzvos* is harder—they are "married far away." This is why the transition from winter to summer is associated with the Atzeres of Pesach, and the transition from summer to winter with the Atzeres of the festival; for Pesach (and the Atzeres of Pesach) is the *avodah* of *tzaddikim*, and the festival (and the Atzeres of the festival) is the *avodah* of *teshuvah*. As is known regarding the verse, "In the covering (*bakeseh*) for the day of our festival," that on the festival of Sukkos there is the revelation of the matters of *Rosh Hashanah* and *Yom Kippur*—the concept of *teshuvah*.

To explain this in greater detail: the *avodah* of *Rosh Hashanah* is "Make Me King over you," the concept of the exaltation of *Malchus*, even to the level of essential exaltation. This is then drawn down into the "one day" of *Yom Kippur*, "once a year." This is what the Midrash means when it says, "One day they all came to greet their father the king"—"one day" refers to *Yom Kippur*, "once a year," on which all Yisrael are equal, and therefore *all* came to greet their father the king. This is also the significance of the phrasing "the king their father," placing "king" before "father," because then is the completion of the building of *Malchus*, the *avodah* of "Make Me King," the drawing down of essential exaltation. Therefore, "king" precedes "father," because the primary emphasis then is the concept of the King, and this (the concept of the King) is drawn down through the level of "their father" to the lowest levels. However, even on *Yom Kippur*, it is still "in the covering" (*bakeseh*), which is why *Yom Kippur* is above eating and drinking, and Yisrael are like angels (and the fact that they are "like angels"—when a human being is like an angel—is a higher level than an angel), for this is not drawn down into the physical matters of eating and drinking. The revelation of this is for "the day of our festival"—through the *Sukkah*, the matters that were initially (on *Rosh Hashanah* and *Yom Kippur*) "in the covering" are drawn down in revelation, and through "you shall dwell in Sukkos for seven days," the revelation is in a state of settledness. Yet, this is still not enough, for the *Sukkah* is a temporary dwelling, and the ultimate intent is that it be a permanent dwelling for Him, which will be in the Third Beis Hamikdash, an eternal dwelling. For this, there is the mitzvah of taking the Four Species, through which the *makif* (encompassing light) of the *Sukkah* is drawn down into the level of *pnimiyus* (internalization). As explained elsewhere, the reason the *Lulav* and its species must touch the chest during the *na'anuim* (shaking) is that the purpose of taking the Four Species is to draw down into the *Nukvah* in the chest of *Ze'er Anpin*. But even this is not enough; the ultimate perfection is the "absorption" (*klitah*), through which the "birth" occurs. This happens on Shemini Atzeres—*Atzeres* from the word *klitah* (absorption)—where all the matters of the month of Tishrei (*Rosh Hashanah*, the Ten Days of *Teshuvah*, *Yom Kippur*, the four days between *Yom Kippur* and Sukkos, and Sukkos) are drawn down into Shemini Atzeres in a state of internal absorption, through which the "birth" occurs: the revelation of the power of the *Ein Sof*, down to the very lowest levels.

7. This is why it is specifically on Shemini Atzeres that it is said, "The eighth day shall be an Atzeres for you" (even though all festivals were given to Yisrael). Since Shemini Atzeres is the absorption of all the influences of the month of Tishrei—*Rosh Hashanah*, *Yom Kippur*, etc.—the concept of *teshuvah* that reaches (as mentioned) the level of concealment that is not within existence, even to the level of essential concealment, this matter is "for you" specifically: "They shall be for you alone, and no stranger with you"—Yisrael and the King alone. This is also the reason why Simchas Torah was established specifically on Shemini Atzeres, for Shemini Atzeres is the absorption of all the influences of the month of Tishrei that are drawn down through *teshuvah*, including the matter of the second Tablets given on *Yom Kippur*, and the joy of Simchas Torah is over the second Tablets, as mentioned above (section 5).

This is why the joy of Simchas Torah is with the letters of the Torah [as mentioned at the beginning of the maamar, that the manner of joy is circling the reading table where we read (all year) the letters of the Torah], for the revelation that comes through the letters of the Torah [letters (*osiyos*) from the word *ata boker*—the morning comes] is above understanding and comprehension. As explained elsewhere regarding the acronym of *Anochi* (I): "I wrote and gave My own Self." The fact that Hashem caused His own essence to be written and given in the Torah is primarily through the letters of the Torah. Since Shemini Atzeres is the absorption of the matters drawn down through *teshuvah*—the far that has become near—through which one draws from the level of the "far place," the concealment that is not within existence, down to the essential concealment of His essence, and this drawing down is in such a way that Yisrael become one with His essence ("Yisrael and the King alone"), therefore the joy then is specifically with the letters of the Torah. For the drawing down of the Essence is primarily in the letters of the Torah, and through them Yisrael become one with the Essence. As explained in the maamar, every single Jew has a letter in the Torah; this letter gives him vitality, protects him from all undesirable matters, and is a channel and vessel for the blessing of Hashem in all that he needs, beginning with the absorption (on Shemini Atzeres) of all the influences drawn down for him in the month of Tishrei—the letters of *reishis* (beginning)—for the entire year.

From the joy of Simchas Torah—"let us make one festival and rejoice"—is drawn the strength for the journey of "Yaakov went on his way," even in the journey of the winter days, which is a difficult journey, as mentioned. We fulfill in practice all the resolutions made on Simchas Torah, for since the resolutions are made with joy, and joy is above all limitations and breaks through all barriers, one does not take into account any obstacles or delays and fulfills all resolutions with strength. And since the joy is with the letters of the Torah, which are the channel and vessel for the blessing of Hashem in all that is needed, through this is drawn an addition of the greatest blessing and success in all matters throughout the entire year, and especially in the primary matter: that it be a year of *Geulah*, the true and complete *Geulah* through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, speedily and truly in our days.
